This is why even modest applied loads initiate *many* micro-crazes at the particle equator in HIPS and ABS—the local stress is doubled, easily exceeding the matrix crazing stress of ~30–40 MPa in atactic polystyrene.

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Volume Fraction for the Brittle–Tough Transition (Wu's Percolation Model)

**Step 1 — Geometric Definition of Ligament Thickness.**
For a monodisperse dispersion of spherical rubber particles of diameter $d$ at a volume fraction $\phi$, the **average center-to-center spacing** is:

$$L_{cc} = d \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6 \phi}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Matrix Ligament Thickness.**
The matrix ligament thickness $\tau$ is the average edge-to-edge distance between two adjacent particles, derived by subtracting one particle diameter from the center-to-center distance:

$$\tau = d \left[\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6\phi}\right)^{1/3} - 1\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply Wu's Empirical Criterion.**
For each polymer matrix, there exists a *critical* ligament thickness $\tau_c$ below which the BTT occurs. For example, for HIPS: $\tau_c \approx 0.25$ µm, and for Nylon 6,6: $\tau_c \approx 0.30$ µm.

**Step 4 — Solve for the Critical Rubber Volume Fraction $\phi_c$.**

Setting $\tau = \tau_c$:

$$\tau_c = d \left[\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6\phi_c}\right)^{1/3} - 1\right]$$

$$\dfrac{\tau_c}{d} = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6\phi_c}\right)^{1/3} - 1$$

$$\dfrac{\tau_c}{d} + 1 = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6\phi_c}\right)^{1/3}$$

Cube both sides:

$$\left(\dfrac{\tau_c}{d} + 1\right)^3 = \dfrac{\pi}{6\phi_c}$$

Solve for $\phi_c$:

$$\phi_c = \dfrac{\pi}{6\left(\dfrac{\tau_c}{d} + 1\right)^3}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation for HIPS with $d = 0.4$ µm and $\tau_c = 0.25$ µm.**

$$\dfrac{\tau_c}{d} + 1 = \dfrac{0.25}{0.4} + 1 = 0.625 + 1 = 1.625$$

$$\left(1.625\right)^3 = 4.291$$

$$\phi_c = \dfrac{\pi}{6 \times 4.291} = \dfrac{3.1416}{25.746} \approx 0.122$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** A minimum rubber volume fraction of **~12.2%** is required in HIPS to achieve the brittle-to-tough transition. Below this, the rubber particles are too widely spaced to interact through their stress fields, and the blend remains effectively brittle. This is precisely why commercial HIPS contains 8–25% polybutadiene rubber—operating right at and beyond the Wu threshold.

### 3.3 Derivation: Toughness Ratio via Energy Balance (Bucknall's Approach)

**Step 1 — Total Energy Absorbed in Toughening.**
In a rubber-toughened polymer, the total energy absorbed $U_{total}$ per unit volume during impact is the sum of contributions from (a) the matrix deformation, (b) crazing, and (c) shear yielding:

$$U_{total} = U_{matrix} + U_{craze} + U_{shear}$$

**Step 2 — Quantify the Shear Yielding Contribution.**
For shear yielding localized in shear bands of width $w_b$ and shear strain $\gamma$ under a shear yield stress $\tau_y$:

$$U_{shear} = \tau_y \cdot \gamma \cdot V_{shear}$$

where $V_{shear}$ is the volume fraction of matrix undergoing shear yielding. In well-toughened blends, the stress field interaction causes $V_{shear} \to 1$ (the entire matrix yields).

**Step 3 — Quantify the Crazing Contribution.**
For multiple crazing, if $N_c$ crazes of unit area $A_c$ form per unit volume, each requiring surface energy $G_{craze}$:

$$U_{craze} = N_c \cdot A_c \cdot G_{craze}$$

**Step 4 — Compare the Toughness Ratio.**
For a typical rubber-toughened thermoplastic like ABS:

$$U_{shear} \sim 50 \text{–} 100 \text{ MJ/m}^3, \quad U_{craze} \sim 5 \text{–} 15 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{U_{total}^{toughened}}{U_{total}^{neat}} \sim 10 \text{ to } 30$$

This matches the experimentally observed one-order-of-magnitude jump in K_IC for ABS over plain PS, validating the energy-balance model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive" a toughening relationship, always start with a *clearly drawn free body diagram* of a representative volume element (RVE) containing one particle, then write the energy balance explicitly. Examiners award 2 of the 7 marks specifically for the RVE schematic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of Toughening Mechanisms (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the micro-mechanical decision-tree that governs energy dissipation in toughened polymers. The rendering follows the **KTU Mermaid Compilation Safeguards**—all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed, no markdown formatting is used inside node labels, and reserved keywords are avoided.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Applied Tensile Load sigma_infty] --> B1[Rubber Particle Stress Field Activation]
    B1 --> C1{Is Matrix Glassy or Ductile?}
    C1 -- Glassy e.g. PS, SAN --> D1[Mechanism 1: Multiple Crazing]
    C1 -- Ductile e.g. PA, PC --> D2[Mechanism 2: Shear Yielding]
    D1 --> E1[Rubber Particles Act as Craze Initiators]
    E1 --> F1[Craze Growth in Equatorial Plane]
    F1 --> G1[Particle Acts as Craze Terminator]
    G1 --> H1[Massive Crazes Cannot Coalesce into One Crack]
    D2 --> E2[Hydrostatic Stress Relief by Particle Cavitation]
    E2 --> F2[Volumetric Strain Localization in Matrix Ligaments]
    F2 --> G2[Matrix Shear Band Nucleation and Propagation]
    G2 --> H2[Ligament Shear Yields Before Fracture]
    H1 --> I1[Energy Absorbed: 5 to 15 MJ per m3]
    H2 --> I2[Energy Absorbed: 50 to 100 MJ per m3]
    I1 --> J1[Stress Whitening Visible]
    I2 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Total K_IC Increased by 10x to 50x]
    B1 --> L1{Is Ligament Thickness tau Less than tau_critical?}
    L1 -- YES --> M1[Brittle to Tough Transition Achieved]
    L1 -- NO --> M2[Blend Remains Brittle]
    M1 --> N1[Commercial Success e.g. HIPS, ABS, PA6/EPR]
    M2 --> N2[Failure Analysis: Insufficient Rubber or Wrong Particle Size]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Block-Diagram: Cavitation → Shear Yielding Cascade (Lazzeri–Bucknall Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step A: External Load Applied] --> P2[Step B: Hydrostatic Tension Develops]
    P2 --> P3[Step C: Internal Particle Cavitation or Debonding]
    P3 --> P4[Step D: Local Free Volume Created]
    P4 --> P5[Step E: Stress State Converts to Plane Strain Shear]
    P5 --> P6[Step F: Shear Band Propagation in Matrix Ligament]
    P6 --> P7[Step G: Massive Energy Dissipation]
    P7 --> P8[Step H: Stabilized Crack without Catastrophic Growth]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Matrix: Crazing vs. Shear Yielding

| Property | Multiple Crazing | Shear Yielding |
|---|---|---|
| Localized strain | 50–100% in crazes | 20–40% in shear bands |
| Volume of matrix affected | 10–20% | 40–80% |
| Energy density (MJ/m³) | 5–15 | 50–100 |
| Dominant in | Glassy polymers (PS, SAN, PMMA) | Ductile polymers (PC, PA, toughened epoxy) |
| Stress state required | High hydrostatic tension | Low hydrostatic + high shear |
| Visible signature | Stress whitening (micro-voids) | Shear bands (45° birefringence) |
| Particle role | Initiate + terminate crazes | Cavitate to relieve constraint |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define the term "toughened polymer" and identify the two principal micro-mechanical mechanisms responsible for its enhanced fracture resistance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **toughened polymer** is a heterogeneous polymer system in which a brittle or low-ductility matrix is modified with a dispersed secondary phase (typically an elastomer or rigid filler) to enhance its fracture toughness and impact resistance. The two principal micro-mechanical toughening mechanisms are:
1. **Multiple Crazing** – the dispersed phase initiates and terminates numerous micro-crazes, forcing the crack to dissipate energy across thousands of small crazes rather than one fatal crack.
2. **Shear Yielding** – the stress field around the particles promotes localized shear-band formation throughout the matrix, dissipating energy through plastic deformation.

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each for the two mechanisms = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*State Wu's criterion for the brittle–tough transition in rubber-modified polymers. What is the practical significance of the critical matrix ligament thickness?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Wu's criterion (1985)** states that a rubber-toughened polymer undergoes a brittle-to-tough transition (BTT) when the **average matrix ligament thickness** (the edge-to-edge distance between adjacent rubber particles) becomes equal to or less than a **critical value $\tau_c$** characteristic of the matrix polymer:

$$\tau \leq \tau_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Tough behavior}$$

**Practical significance:** $\tau_c$ sets the **minimum rubber content** and **maximum allowable particle size** for effective toughening. If the particles are too small relative to their spacing (i.e., $\tau > \tau_c$), the blend remains brittle regardless of further increases in rubber content. The criterion is widely used to design the *minimum-cost* toughened formulation that still meets impact specifications.

*(Allocation: 2 marks criterion + 1 mark significance = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the **crazing–shear yielding interaction model** for rubber-toughened glassy polymers. Show how rubber particles simultaneously initiate crazes and promote localized shear yielding through stress-field overlap. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A HIPS (High Impact Polystyrene) blend contains polybutadiene rubber particles of mean diameter $d = 0.4$ µm at a volume fraction $\phi = 0.18$. The critical matrix ligament thickness for the polystyrene matrix is $\tau_c = 0.25$ µm. Using Wu's criterion, determine whether the blend is in the **tough** or **brittle** regime. If it is brittle, what *minimum* rubber volume fraction would be required to achieve the brittle–tough transition at the same particle size? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Crazing–Shear Yielding Interaction Model:**

The modern understanding (Bucknall, 1977; later refined by Lazzeri & Bucknall, 1993) of rubber-toughened glassy polymers recognizes that **multiple crazing** and **shear yielding** are *not mutually exclusive*. They operate as a coupled, synergistic pair:

1. **Initiation Stage:** Under tensile load, the rubber particles generate local stress concentration factors of approximately 2× (from the Eshelby inclusion model). This localized stress exceeds the matrix crazing stress, nucleating many micro-crazes at the particle equators.
2. **Stress Relief Stage:** As crazes form, they relieve the local hydrostatic tension. The stress state around the particle transitions from a triaxial (craze-favoring) state to a more deviatoric (shear-favoring) state.
3. **Shear Band Nucleation:** The deviatoric stress state, combined with the geometric stress-field interaction between adjacent particles, nucleates shear bands at 30°–45° to the loading direction in the matrix ligaments.
4. **Stabilization:** Rubber particles also act as *craze terminators*, preventing individual crazes from growing into fatal cracks. The net result is a *massive* absorption of mechanical energy—visible as stress whitening.

A labeled schematic would show: (i) rubber particles, (ii) equatoria l crazes, (iii) shear bands at ±45°, (iv) the stress concentration contours, and (v) the inter-particle overlap zone.

*(Mark split: 2 marks schematic + 3 marks mechanism description + 2 marks for highlighting the interaction = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the actual matrix ligament thickness.**

$$\tau = d\left[\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6\phi}\right)^{1/3} - 1\right] = 0.4\left[\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6 \times 0.18}\right)^{1/3} - 1\right]$$

Compute the inner term:

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6 \times 0.18} = \dfrac{3.1416}{1.08} = 2.909$$

$$\left(2.909\right)^{1/3} = 1.426$$

Therefore:

$$\tau = 0.4 \times (1.426 - 1) = 0.4 \times 0.426 = 0.170 \, \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the critical value.**

$$\tau = 0.170 \, \mu m \quad \text{vs} \quad \tau_c = 0.25 \, \mu m$$

Since $\tau < \tau_c$, the blend is in the **TOUGH** regime. The brittle–tough transition has already been achieved at the given formulation.

**Step 3 — (Hypothetical) Minimum $\phi$ Required.**

For an alternative blend with the same $d = 0.4$ µm but unknown $\phi$, set $\tau = \tau_c$:

$$\tau_c = d\left[\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6\phi_c}\right)^{1/3} - 1\right]$$

$$0.25 = 0.4\left[\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6\phi_c}\right)^{1/3} - 1\right]$$

$$\dfrac{0.25}{0.4} = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6\phi_c}\right)^{1/3} - 1$$

$$0.625 + 1 = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6\phi_c}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$1.625^3 = \dfrac{\pi}{6\phi_c}$$

$$4.291 = \dfrac{3.1416}{6\phi_c}$$

$$\phi_c = \dfrac{3.1416}{6 \times 4.291} = \dfrac{3.1416}{25.746} = 0.122$$

**Result:** A minimum rubber volume fraction of **$\phi_c \approx 12.2\%$** would be required. The given blend at 18% is well above this threshold and is operating in the tough regime.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for correct substitution + 2 marks for arithmetic + 1 mark for comparison + 1 mark for finding $\phi_c$ + 1 mark for final conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice for Internal Option): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **cavitation-induced shear yielding** model of Lazzeri and Bucknall. How does internal particle cavitation (or interfacial debonding) act as a *precursor* to massive shear yielding in the surrounding matrix ligament? Include in your answer the role of the **cavitation pressure** and the critical particle size for effective voiding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An epoxy resin is toughened with 15% by volume of a carboxyl-terminated butadiene-acrylonitrile (CTBN) rubber with mean particle diameter $d = 0.2$ µm. The surface energy of the rubber is $\gamma = 0.04$ J/m², the matrix yield stress is $\sigma_y = 65$ MPa, and the cavitation pressure for effective shear-band triggering is $P_{cav} = 20$ MPa. Determine whether the rubber particles will cavitate under an applied tensile stress of $\sigma_{\infty} = 40$ MPa. Comment on the implications for the toughening effectiveness of this blend. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cavitation-Induced Shear Yielding Model (Lazzeri & Bucknall, 1993):**

In rubber-toughened polymers under triaxial stress, the rubber particles (or their interfaces) can undergo **internal cavitation**—the formation of a micro-void within or around the particle. This is *not* a failure event; it is a **necessary precursor** to the dominant energy-absorbing event: massive shear yielding in the matrix ligament.

**Mechanism (4 steps):**

1. **Hydrostatic tension build-up:** Under tensile loading, the bulk matrix develops a triaxial stress state. Around the (essentially incompressible) rubber particles, the local hydrostatic stress is amplified.
2. **Cavitation event:** Once the local hydrostatic stress reaches the **cavitation pressure** $P_{cav}$, the rubber particle either fractures internally or debonds from the matrix, creating a small void. The cavitation pressure is given by the energy balance:

$$P_{cav} = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r} + \dfrac{2}{3}\sigma_y$$

where $r$ is the particle radius, $\gamma$ the interfacial energy, and $\sigma_y$ the matrix yield stress.

3. **Stress-state transformation:** The void *converts* the surrounding stress state from a hydrostatic (triaxial) state, which *favors* brittle failure, into a **deviatoric (shear) state**, which *favors* plastic yielding.
4. **Ligament shear yielding:** The matrix ligament between adjacent cavitated particles now yields in shear at a stress far below the brittle fracture stress, dissipating massive amounts of energy.

**Role of critical particle size:** There exists a **minimum particle size** for effective cavitation. Too small, and the cavitation pressure (which scales as $1/r$) becomes unattainably high. Too large, and the particles act as macroscopic stress concentrators that initiate cracks rather than relieve stress. The optimum is typically $d = 0.1$–$1.0$ µm.

*(Mark split: 2 marks schematic of the 4-step process + 3 marks mechanism description + 1 mark cavitation pressure equation + 1 mark particle-size criterion = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Cavitation Check Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Compute the actual local hydrostatic stress around the particles.**

For a stress concentration factor $K_t \approx 2$ (rubber in stiff epoxy):

$$\sigma_{local} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{\infty} = 2 \times 40 = 80 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cavitation pressure $P_{cav}$ for the actual particle size.**

$$P_{cav} = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r} + \dfrac{2}{3}\sigma_y$$

With $r = 0.1$ µm $= 1 \times 10^{-7}$ m:

$$\dfrac{2\gamma}{r} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.04}{1 \times 10^{-7}} = 8 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.8 \text{ MPa}$$

(Note: the surface-energy term is *negligible* at this particle size.)

$$\dfrac{2}{3}\sigma_y = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 65 = 43.33 \text{ MPa}$$

$$P_{cav} = 0.8 + 43.33 = 44.13 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compare the local stress to the cavitation pressure.**

$$\sigma_{local} = 80 \, \text{MPa} \quad > \quad P_{cav} = 44.13 \, \text{MPa}$$

The local hydrostatic stress **significantly exceeds** the cavitation pressure. **Cavitation will occur.**

**Step 4 — Implications for Toughening.**

Because cavitation occurs, the Lazzeri–Bucknall mechanism is *activated*, and the surrounding matrix ligaments will undergo massive shear yielding. The blend is expected to be **highly effective in toughening**, with a predicted K_IC increase of 10×–30× over the neat epoxy. The CTBN rubber at 15 vol% with $d = 0.2$ µm is operating near the optimum for epoxy toughening.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for $\sigma_{local}$ + 2 marks for $P_{cav}$ + 1 mark for the comparison + 2 marks for the engineering interpretation = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **The "single mechanism" trap:** Students often describe *only* multiple crazing OR *only* shear yielding. The 2024 scheme expects you to identify the **synergy** between the two. A complete answer always mentions that crazes relieve hydrostatic tension, *enabling* shear yielding—a coupled mechanism.
> 2. **Units error in ligament thickness:** The matrix ligament thickness is in **µm**, not mm. A dimensional slip here is a guaranteed 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing the stress concentration factor:** $K_t \approx 2$ for a *soft rubber particle in a stiff matrix*. Do not write $K_t = 3$ (which is the value for a *circular hole in a plate*). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting the glass transition caveat:** Above the rubber's $T_g$ (e.g., at elevated service temperatures), the rubber particles *lose their toughening function* because the modulus contrast vanishes. This is a frequent board question.
> 5. **Numerical rounding errors in Wu's equation:** Cube roots and the $\pi/6$ factor must be carried to at least 3 significant figures. Rounding too early is the most common arithmetic mistake in Part B.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Toughened polymers** are modified to convert brittle failure into ductile failure by dispersing a secondary phase that activates energy-dissipation mechanisms.
- **Three pillars of toughening:** (1) Multiple Crazing, (2) Shear Yielding, (3) Crack/Particle Interactions (pinning, deflection, bridging, cavitation).
- **Stress concentration factor** at a rubber particle in a glassy matrix: **K_t ≈ 2** (this is what initiates the crazes).
- **Wu's criterion:** $\tau \leq \tau_c$ is the *necessary* condition for the brittle-to-tough transition. The critical ligament thickness is matrix-specific.
- **Wu's equation:** $\tau = d\left[(\pi/6\phi)^{1/3} - 1\right]$ — memorize this; it is a guaranteed exam formula.
- **Cavitation pressure equation:** $P_{cav} = 2\gamma/r + (2/3)\sigma_y$ — the Lazzeri–Bucknall threshold.
- **Toughness benchmark:** A *successful* toughening solution increases $G_{IC}$ (or $K_{IC}$) by at least **10×** while keeping the modulus drop below **30%**.
- **The Synergy Rule:** Multiple crazing and shear yielding *cooperate*, not compete. Crazes relieve the hydrostatic component; shear yielding dissipates the deviatoric component.
- **Industrial benchmarks:** HIPS (rubber-toughened PS), ABS (rubber-toughened SAN + SAN graft shell), PA6/EPR (impact-modified nylon), toughened epoxy/CTBN (composite matrices).
- **Common failure modes of toughened systems in service:** (i) rubber particle aging / cross-linking above the use temperature, (ii) cavitation without subsequent shear yielding (brittle *and* voided — the worst case), (iii) particle agglomeration raising the effective $\tau$ above $\tau_c$.
- **Design rule of thumb:** Optimal rubber particle size for glassy polymer toughening is **0.1–1.0 µm**; for crystalline/ductile matrix toughening, somewhat larger (1–5 µm) is acceptable.
- **KTU 2024 Cognitive-Level Tagging:** This topic addresses **CO1 (Understand)** and **CO2 (Apply)** at Bloom's Levels 2, 3, and 4 — a high-yield area for **14-mark** structured questions in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure of Highly Filled Systems/Composites. Fracture Mechanics: Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM)
Fracture Predictions Based on Stress Concentration and Stress Intensity Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT867 - FAILURE ANALYSIS OF POLYMERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fracture Predictions Based on Stress Concentration and Stress Intensity Factor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fracture Prediction** in polymers is the engineering process of estimating the conditions (applied stress, flaw size, or material toughness) under which a cracked or notched polymer component will fail catastrophically. It is performed using one of two principal frameworks: **(a) the Stress Concentration Approach** (Inglis / Neuber classical theory) and **(b) the Stress Intensity Factor Approach** (Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics – LEFM, Irwin-Orowan formalism). In the **stress concentration approach**, fracture is predicted when the local stress at a notch tip (amplified by a geometric stress concentration factor $K_t$) exceeds the **ultimate tensile strength** $\sigma_u$ (or cohesive strength) of the polymer. In the **stress intensity factor approach**, fracture is predicted when the stress field intensity at a crack tip — quantified by the parameter $K$ — reaches a critical material property, the **fracture toughness** $K_c$ (or $K_{IC}$ in Mode I). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are pulling a sheet of polyethylene with a small razor cut on its edge. - **Stress Concentration Viewpoint**: "The razor cut causes the local stress at the cut tip to be 10×–100× higher than the average pull. If the local stress exceeds the polymer's breaking strength, the sheet snaps." → This is like saying a *paper tear* propagates because a single thread is overloaded. - **Stress Intensity Factor Viewpoint**: "Instead of asking how big the local stress is, we ask how much *energy* is being pumped into the crack tip per unit area. When that energy input rate equals the polymer's resistance (toughness), the crack runs." → This is like saying the *energy of the tear* is what matters, not the stress on a single thread. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favorite):** > - Stress concentration theory → strength-based criterion (stress vs. strength). > - Stress intensity factor theory → energy/toughness-based criterion (driving force vs. resistance). > Both converge to the same physical answer for brittle polymers (PS, PMMA), but LEFM is rigorously valid when plastic zone size is small compared to crack length. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted - **$K_t$** = Elastic (geometric) stress concentration factor → **dimensionless**. - **$K$** = Stress intensity factor → units of **MPa·m$^{1/2}$** or **psi·in$^{1/2}$**. - **$K_c$ / $K_{IC}$** = Fracture toughness (critical SIF) → **MPa·m$^{1/2}$**. - **$a$** = Crack / flaw half-length → **m**. - **$\sigma$** = Applied far-field (nominal) stress → **MPa**. - **$Y$** = Geometry correction factor → **dimensionless** (typically 1.0 – 1.5). - **$G_c$** = Critical strain energy release rate → **J/m²** or **N/m**. - **$E$** = Young's modulus → **MPa**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Stress Concentration Framework #### 2.1.1 Inglis' Classical Solution (1913) For an elliptical hole of major axis $2a$ (perpendicular to load) and minor axis $2b$ in an infinite plate under remote tension $\sigma$: $$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma \left(1 + \dfrac{2a}{b}\right)$$ For a sharp crack ($b \to 0$), $\sigma_{\max} \to \infty$ — the classical **infinite stress paradox**. This is why a purely stress-based approach fails for sharp cracks and we need LEFM. #### 2.1.2 The Stress Concentration Factor $K_t$ $$K_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{nominal}}}$$ For an elliptical hole: $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$, where $\rho = b^2/a$ is the notch tip radius. #### 2.1.3 Neuber / Peterson Notch Analysis For a notch of root radius $\rho$ and depth $d$ in a finite body, Neuber introduced the **relative stress gradient** and provided tabulated $K_t$ values. Neuber's equation: $$K_t = 1 + \dfrac{2\sqrt{d/\rho}}{1 + \sqrt{d/\rho} \cdot \text{(function of geometry)}}$$ **Failure prediction (classical):** $$\sigma_{\text{nominal}} \cdot K_t \leq \sigma_u \quad \text{(equivalently: } \sigma_{\text{nominal}} \leq \sigma_u / K_t\text{)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why classical $K_t$ fails for sharp cracks in polymers:** > 1. $\sigma_{\max} \to \infty$ as $\rho \to 0$. > 2. Polymers undergo localized yielding/crazing at the crack tip, blunting the notch. > 3. The "notch strength" of polymers does NOT follow the simple $K_t$ rule — this led to the birth of fracture mechanics for polymers (especially by Berry, Knauss, and later Kinloch & Williams). ### 2.2 The Stress Intensity Factor (LEFM) Framework Irwin (1957) showed that the stress field near a crack tip in a linear-elastic body can be written universally as: $$\sigma_{ij}(r,\theta) = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cdot f_{ij}(\theta) + \text{higher order terms}$$ where $r$ is the distance from the crack tip and $\theta$ is the angle. The **single scalar** $K$ controls the entire intensity of the crack-tip stress field. #### 2.2.1 Three Modes of Loading | Mode | Type | Displacement Field | Common in Polymers? | |:----:|:----:|:------------------:|:-------------------:| | **I** | Opening (tensile) | Crack faces separate | **Yes — dominant** | | **II** | In-plane shear | Sliding | Rare (mixed-mode) | | **III** | Out-of-plane shear | Tearing | Occurs in films | Most polymer failure is treated as **Mode I**; the critical toughness is denoted $K_{IC}$. #### 2.2.2 Definition of SIF for Common Geometries For a central crack of length $2a$ in an infinite plate under remote tension $\sigma$: $$K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$ For an edge crack of length $a$ in a semi-infinite plate: $$K_I = 1.12 \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$ Generalized form (Irwin): $$K_I = Y \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$ where $Y$ is the dimensionless **geometry (compliance) factor**. #### 2.2.3 Fracture Criterion (Irwin's Postulate) $$\boxed{K_I \geq K_{IC} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Fracture occurs}}$$ Equivalently, the **critical far-field stress** to fracture a component with flaw size $a$ is: $$\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a}}$$ #### 2.2.4 Energy Equivalence — The Irwin Relation The SIF approach is energetically equivalent to Griffith's energy balance. For plane stress: $$G = \dfrac{K_I^2}{E}$$ For plane strain: $$G = \dfrac{K_I^2 (1-\nu^2)}{E}$$ Critical condition: $G = G_c \iff K_I = K_{IC}$, giving $K_{IC} = \sqrt{E \, G_c / (1-\nu^2)}$ (plane strain). ### 2.3 Comparison of the Two Approaches | Aspect | Stress Concentration (Inglis/Neuber) | Stress Intensity Factor (Irwin) | |:------|:-------------------------------------|:--------------------------------| | **Governing parameter** | $K_t$ (dimensionless) | $K_I$ (MPa·m$^{1/2}$) | | **Material property used** | Ultimate strength $\sigma_u$ | Fracture toughness $K_{IC}$ | | **Valid for sharp cracks** | ❌ (predicts infinite stress) | ✅ | | **Valid for blunt notches** | ✅ | ⚠ Requires modification | | **Account for plastic zone** | ❌ | Partially (via $r_p$ correction) | | **Best for polymers** | Ductile/blunt notches | Brittle (PS, PMMA, epoxy) | | **Difficulty in KTU exam** | Easy (algebra) | Moderate (LEFM derivations) | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning | Units | |:-:|:---------|:--------|:------| | 1 | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ | Inglis stress concentration | — | | 2 | $\sigma_{\max} = K_t \cdot \sigma$ | Local peak stress | MPa | | 3 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_u / K_t$ | Classical fracture stress | MPa | | 4 | $K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Irwin's SIF | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ | | 5 | $K_I = 1.12 \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Edge crack SIF | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ | | 6 | $\sigma_c = K_{IC} / (Y\sqrt{\pi a})$ | Critical applied stress | MPa | | 7 | $a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sigma}\right)^2$ | Critical crack length | m | | 8 | $G_c = K_{IC}^2 / E$ (plane stress) | Energy release rate | J/m² | | 9 | $G_c = K_{IC}^2(1-\nu^2)/E$ (plane strain) | Energy release rate | J/m² | | 10 | $r_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2$ | Plastic zone (plane stress) | m | | 11 | $r_p = \dfrac{1}{6\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2$ | Plastic zone (plane strain) | m | > [!NOTE] > **Typical $K_{IC}$ values for polymers (KTU Reference Data):** > - Polystyrene (PS): **0.8 – 1.1** MPa·m$^{1/2}$ (brittle) > - PMMA: **0.7 – 1.6** MPa·m$^{1/2}$ (brittle, optically clear) > - Epoxy: **0.3 – 0.6** MPa·m$^{1/2}$ (brittle thermoset) > - Polycarbonate (PC): **2.0 – 3.0** MPa·m$^{1/2}$ (tough, ductile) > - Nylon 6,6: **2.5 – 4.0** MPa·m$^{1/2}$ (semi-ductile) > - Polyethylene (HDPE): **1.0 – 2.0** MPa·m$^{1/2}$ ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Plastic pressure vessels & pipes**: ASME and ISO codes use LEFM to set maximum allowable flaw sizes in HDPE gas pipes. - **Aircraft composites**: Carbon-fibre/epoxy skins are designed so that barely-visible impact damage (BVID) does not propagate under $K_I = K_{IC}$. - **Medical devices**: Polymeric implants (UHMWPE hip liners) are assessed using both $K_t$ (notch sensitivity) and $K_{IC}$ (sub-crack propagation). - **Failure analysis**: When a polymer component fails, an investigator measures the **mirror-mist-hackle** region on the fracture surface and uses empirical relations like $\sigma \cdot r_m^{1/2} = A_{m}$ (Kerkhof–Glaser) to back-calculate the failure stress. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Inglis' Elliptical Hole Result **Statement (KTU module outcome CO1):** Derive the maximum stress at the tip of an elliptical hole in an infinite plate under uniaxial tension. **Setup:** Consider an infinite 2D plate (plane stress) with a central elliptical hole of major axis $2a$ (along $x$-axis) and minor axis $2b$ (along $y$-axis). Remote tension $\sigma$ is applied in the $y$-direction. We use Inglis' Airy stress-function approach. The stress function for an elliptical hole is: $$\phi(x,y) = \text{Re}\left[\dfrac{\sigma}{2}\left(\zeta - \dfrac{a^2-b^2}{\zeta}\right)\right] \quad \text{where} \quad \zeta = x + iy$$ Transforming to elliptical coordinates $(\xi, \eta)$ via $z = c \cosh(\xi + i\eta)$ with $c = \sqrt{a^2 - b^2}$, the stress components are: $$\sigma_{\xi} + \sigma_{\eta} = \dfrac{2(\sigma \sinh\xi)(\cosh 2\xi - \cos 2\eta + e^{2\xi}\cos 2\eta)}{\cosh 2\xi - \cos 2\eta - e^{2\xi}\cos 2\eta} \quad \text{(boundary terms)}$$ For points on the ellipse boundary $\xi = \xi_0$ where $a = c \cosh\xi_0$ and $b = c \sinh\xi_0$, evaluating the hoop stress $\sigma_{\eta}$ at the end of the major axis ($\eta = \pi/2$): **Step 1:** Substitute $\eta = \pi/2$ into the boundary expression for $\sigma_{\eta}$. $$\sigma_{\eta}\big|_{\eta = \pi/2} = \sigma \cdot \dfrac{\cosh 2\xi_0 + 1 + e^{2\xi_0}\cdot(-1)}{\cosh 2\xi_0 + 1 - e^{2\xi_0}\cdot(-1)} = \sigma \cdot \dfrac{\cosh 2\xi_0 + 1 - e^{2\xi_0}}{\cosh 2\xi_0 + 1 + e^{2\xi_0}}$$ **Step 2:** Use the identity $\cosh 2\xi_0 = 2\sinh^2\xi_0 + 1 = 2\cosh^2\xi_0 - 1$ and $a = c\cosh\xi_0$, $b = c\sinh\xi_0$. $$\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{\cosh\xi_0}{\sinh\xi_0} = \coth\xi_0$$ **Step 3:** After algebraic simplification (using $\cosh 2\xi_0 - e^{2\xi_0} = -1$): $$\sigma_{\eta}\big|_{\eta = \pi/2, \xi=\xi_0} = \sigma \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2a}{b}\right)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = \sigma\left(1 + \dfrac{2a}{b}\right)}$$ **Limiting case — sharp crack:** As $b \to 0$ (sharp crack with $a \gg b$), the ratio $a/b \to \infty$, so $\sigma_{\max} \to \infty$. This is the **infinite stress singularity** that classical strength-of-materials theory cannot resolve — the very motivation for LEFM. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — Irwin's Stress Intensity Factor from Griffith Energy Balance **Statement (CO2):** Show that the SIF criterion $K_I = K_{IC}$ is equivalent to Griffith's energy release rate criterion $G = G_c$. **Starting point — Griffith's energy balance:** $$\dfrac{dU}{da} = \dfrac{dW}{da} - \dfrac{dU_e}{da} = 0 \quad \text{at fracture}$$ where $U$ is total energy, $W$ is work done by external load, $U_e$ is elastic strain energy, and the surface energy $4a\gamma_s$ must be supplied. **Step 1:** For a center-cracked plate of half-length $a$ under remote stress $\sigma$, the elastic compliance (per unit thickness) is: $$C(a) = \dfrac{\pi a^2}{E'} \quad \text{where } E' = E \text{ (plane stress) or } E/(1-\nu^2) \text{ (plane strain)}$$ **Step 2:** The strain energy stored in the plate is $U_e = \dfrac{1}{2} F \cdot \delta$ where $\delta = C(a) F$. Differentiate with respect to crack length at constant load: $$\left.\dfrac{\partial U_e}{\partial a}\right|_F = \dfrac{1}{2} F^2 \dfrac{dC}{da} = \dfrac{F^2 \pi a}{E'}$$ **Step 3:** For fixed grips (constant displacement), the work done by the external load decreases as the crack grows, releasing energy: $$G = -\left.\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial a}\right|_\delta = \left.+\dfrac{\partial U_e}{\partial a}\right|_F = \dfrac{F^2 \pi a}{(E' B)^2} \cdot B = \dfrac{\sigma^2 \pi a}{E'}$$ where $B$ is the plate thickness and $F/B = \sigma$. **Step 4:** Equate to surface energy for critical condition: $$G_c = \dfrac{\sigma_c^2 \pi a}{E'} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sigma_c\sqrt{\pi a} = \sqrt{E' G_c}$$ **Step 5:** Compare with the SIF definition $K_I = \sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$: $$K_I^2 = \sigma^2 \pi a = E' G_c$$ **Step 6:** Therefore the **critical** SIF, defined as the value of $K_I$ at which fracture occurs, is: $$\boxed{K_{IC} = \sqrt{E' G_c}}$$ with $E' = E$ for plane stress and $E' = E/(1-\nu^2)$ for plane strain. **Interpretation:** The SIF $K_I$ is a measure of the *intensity* of the crack-tip stress field; its square is proportional to the energy available to drive the crack. Equating $K_I$ to $K_{IC}$ is mathematically equivalent to equating $G$ to $G_c$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation III — Critical Flaw Size $a_c$ **Statement:** A polymer pipe (HDPE, $K_{IC} = 2.5$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$) is designed to operate at hoop stress $\sigma = 8$ MPa. Geometry factor $Y = 1.0$. Find the maximum tolerable internal flaw length. **Step 1:** Start from the SIF criterion (failure condition): $$K_{IC} = Y \, \sigma \sqrt{\pi a_c}$$ **Step 2:** Square both sides to isolate $a_c$: $$K_{IC}^2 = Y^2 \, \sigma^2 \, \pi a_c$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $a_c$: $$a_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}^2}{\pi \, Y^2 \, \sigma^2}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute numerical values (SI units): $$a_c = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^6)^2}{\pi \cdot (1.0)^2 \cdot (8 \times 10^6)^2}$$ **Step 5:** Compute the ratio: $$a_c = \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^{12}}{3.1416 \cdot 64 \times 10^{12}} = \dfrac{6.25}{201.06} \text{ m}$$ $$a_c \approx 0.03108 \text{ m} \approx 31.1 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 6:** State the result with units and physical interpretation: $$\boxed{a_c \approx 31 \text{ mm}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Any surface or internal flaw longer than **31 mm** in this pipe will propagate catastrophically at the design stress. This sets the **NDT inspection threshold** during quality control (typically one uses a *safety factor* of 4–10, requiring detection of flaws $\geq 3$–8 mm). --- ### 3.4 Derivation IV — Plastic Zone Size Correction (Irwin) **Statement:** For a polymer approaching plane-strain conditions, the effective crack length must be increased by half the plastic zone radius. **Step 1:** The von Mises yield criterion at the crack tip gives (for $\sigma_{yy}$ at $\theta = 0$): $$\sigma_{yy}(r,0) = \dfrac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}}$$ **Step 2:** Yielding occurs when $\sigma_{yy} = \sigma_y$: $$\sigma_y = \dfrac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r_p}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad r_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2$$ This is the **plane-stress** plastic zone radius. **Step 3:** Under plane strain, the constraint is higher (triaxiality). The factor becomes $1/(6\pi)$: $$r_p^{\text{(PE)}} = \dfrac{1}{6\pi}\left(\dfrac{K_I}{\sigma_y}\right)^2$$ **Step 4:** Irwin's correction: the effective crack length becomes $a_{\text{eff}} = a + r_p$. Substitute back: $$K_I^{\text{corr}} = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi(a + r_p)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polymer relevance:** Most thermoplastics have substantial plastic zones due to crazing (PS, PMMA) or shear yielding (PC, PE). Strict LEFM requires $a, B \geq 2.5(K_{IC}/\sigma_y)^2$. When this fails, **Elastic-Plastic Fracture Mechanics** (J-integral, CTOD) is used. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Polymer | $K_{IC}$ (MPa·m$^{1/2}$) | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | $r_p$ at $K_{IC}$ (plane strain) | LEFM valid? | |:-------:|:-----------------------:|:----------------:|:--------------------------------:|:-----------:| | PMMA | 1.0 | 70 | 0.034 mm | ✅ (brittle) | | PS | 0.9 | 40 | 0.108 mm | ✅ | | Epoxy | 0.5 | 60 | 0.015 mm | ✅ | | PC | 2.5 | 60 | 0.38 mm | ⚠ Borderline | | HDPE | 2.0 | 25 | 1.35 mm | ❌ Need EPFM | ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define the term "stress concentration factor" $K_t$. How does it differ from the "stress intensity factor" $K_I$ in fracture mechanics?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **$K_t$ (Stress Concentration Factor):** The dimensionless ratio of the maximum local stress at a notch/hole to the nominal applied stress. $K_t = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\text{nominal}}$. It is a **geometric** quantity that does not depend on the applied load magnitude, only on notch shape and body geometry. (1.5 marks) - **$K_I$ (Stress Intensity Factor):** A scalar (units MPa·m$^{1/2}$) that characterizes the **intensity of the crack-tip stress field** in a linear-elastic body. It depends on applied stress $\sigma$, crack length $a$, and geometry factor $Y$: $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$. It is a **mechanical loading** parameter. (1.5 marks) **Key distinction:** $K_t$ becomes infinite for a perfectly sharp crack (singularity problem), while $K_I$ remains finite and physical, making it the proper descriptor for sharp cracks. $K_t$ applies to **stress** while $K_I$ applies to **field intensity**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** *State the Irwin relation between the strain energy release rate $G$ and the stress intensity factor $K_I$ for plane stress and plane strain conditions.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Plane stress** (thin polymer, free surfaces): $$G = \dfrac{K_I^2}{E} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$ - **Plane strain** (thick polymer, constrained): $$G = \dfrac{K_I^2 (1-\nu^2)}{E} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$ where $E$ is Young's modulus and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio. The plane-strain value is *smaller* than plane-stress for the same $K_I$ (because the factor $1-\nu^2 < 1$), meaning more energy is available for crack propagation in thin components — explaining why thin films tear more easily than thick blocks of the same polymer. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain Inglis' classical solution for stress concentration at an elliptical hole. Derive the expression for the maximum stress at the crack tip and discuss its limitation for sharp cracks. **(b) [7 Marks]** A PMMA sheet (fracture toughness $K_{IC} = 1.2$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$, ultimate strength $\sigma_u = 70$ MPa) contains a central crack of length $2a = 10$ mm. Using: (i) the classical stress concentration approach with $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ and a notch root radius $\rho = 0.1$ mm, and (ii) the LEFM/SIF approach with $Y = 1.0$, calculate the critical applied stress for fracture in each case and comment on the result. **Model Solution:** **(a) Inglis' classical solution (7 marks):** - *Sketch:* Draw an infinite plate with a central elliptical hole of major axis $2a$ (horizontal) and minor axis $2b$ (vertical), loaded by uniform tension $\sigma$ in the vertical direction. Mark the point of maximum stress at the end of the major axis (top and bottom of the ellipse). (1 mark) - *Airy stress function* $\phi$ in elliptical coordinates; boundary conditions on the ellipse. (1 mark) - *Substitution* at $\eta = \pi/2$, simplification using $\cosh 2\xi_0$, $a/b = \coth\xi_0$. (2 marks) - *Final result:* $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma(1 + 2a/b)$. (1 mark) - *Limitation:* As $b \to 0$ (sharp crack), $\sigma_{\max} \to \infty$, contradicting the finite strength of real materials. Classical $K_t$ approach fails because polymers undergo local yielding/crazing that blunts the tip. (2 marks) **(b) Numerical comparison (7 marks):** **(i) Classical $K_t$ approach (3.5 marks):** - $a = 5$ mm = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho = 0.1$ mm = $1 \times 10^{-4}$ m - $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho} = 1 + 2\sqrt{5 \times 10^{-3} / 1 \times 10^{-4}} = 1 + 2\sqrt{50} = 1 + 2(7.071) = 15.14$ - $\sigma_c = \sigma_u / K_t = 70 / 15.14 = 4.62$ MPa **(ii) SIF / LEFM approach (3.5 marks):** - $a = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $Y = 1.0$, $K_{IC} = 1.2$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$ - $K_{IC} = Y \sigma_c \sqrt{\pi a}$ - $\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}} = \dfrac{1.2}{1.0 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}}$ - $\sqrt{\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \sqrt{0.01571} = 0.1253$ m$^{1/2}$ - $\sigma_c = 1.2 / 0.1253 = 9.58$ MPa **Comment:** The LEFM prediction (9.58 MPa) is **roughly 2× higher** than the classical $K_t$ prediction (4.62 MPa) because $K_t$ over-amplifies the stress for this relatively sharp notch. In real PMMA (brittle, crazing material), experimental fracture stress is typically closer to the LEFM value, confirming the superiority of $K_{IC}$ for sharp flaws. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the three modes of crack loading in Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics. Why is Mode I the most relevant for polymer failure? Derive the relationship $K_{IC} = \sqrt{E G_c}$ for plane stress. **(b) [7 Marks]** An HDPE pipe (inner diameter 100 mm, wall thickness 8 mm) operates at an internal pressure of 0.6 MPa. The pipe material has $K_{IC} = 2.0$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$ and contains a longitudinal surface crack of length $a = 4$ mm. Take $Y = 1.12$ for an edge crack. (i) Calculate the hoop stress in the pipe wall using thin-wall approximation. (ii) Calculate $K_I$ and check whether the pipe will fail by brittle fracture. (iii) If the design safety factor is 3, what is the maximum allowable operating pressure? **Model Solution:** **(a) Modes of loading and derivation (7 marks):** - *Three modes* (1.5 marks): - Mode I: opening (tensile, crack faces pull apart) - Mode II: in-plane shear (sliding) - Mode III: out-of-plane shear (tearing) - *Mode I dominance* in polymers: most service loads are tensile; crazes initiate perpendicular to the maximum principal tensile stress; experimental $K_{IC}$ is measured in Mode I geometry (SENB, CT). (1.5 marks) - *Derivation of $K_{IC} = \sqrt{EG_c}$* (plane stress): (4 marks) - Start with $K_I = \sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ (central crack). - Energy release rate from compliance: $G = \sigma^2 \pi a / E$. - Identify $G = G_c$ at fracture, and $K_I = K_{IC}$ at fracture. - From $G_c = K_{IC}^2 / E$ → $K_{IC} = \sqrt{EG_c}$. **(b) HDPE pipe analysis (7 marks):** **(i) Hoop stress (2 marks):** Thin-wall formula $\sigma_h = pD/(2t) = (0.6 \times 100)/(2 \times 8) = 60/16 = 3.75$ MPa. **(ii) SIF check (3 marks):** $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a} = 1.12 \times 3.75 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}$ - $\sqrt{\pi \times 0.004} = \sqrt{0.01257} = 0.1121$ m$^{1/2}$ - $K_I = 1.12 \times 3.75 \times 0.1121 = 0.4708$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$ Compare with $K_{IC} = 2.0$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$: since $K_I = 0.47 \ll K_{IC} = 2.0$, the pipe is **SAFE** at this pressure (no brittle fracture). The safety factor is $2.0/0.47 \approx 4.25$. **(iii) Maximum allowable pressure with safety factor $n = 3$ (2 marks):** - Required SIF: $K_{I,\text{allow}} = K_{IC}/n = 2.0/3 = 0.667$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$ - Allowable stress: $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = K_{I,\text{allow}} / (Y\sqrt{\pi a}) = 0.667 / (1.12 \times 0.1121) = 0.667 / 0.1256 = 5.31$ MPa - From $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = p_{\max} D/(2t)$: $p_{\max} = 2t\sigma_{\text{allow}}/D = (2 \times 8 \times 5.31)/100 = 0.850$ MPa. **Final answer:** $p_{\max} \approx 0.85$ MPa (≈ 8.5 bar), a **~42% increase** over the current 0.6 MPa operating pressure while still meeting the safety factor of 3. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Points in KTU Valuation (Examiner's Report):** > 1. **Forgetting units of $K_{IC}$:** Always express in **MPa·m$^{1/2}$**, not bare MPa. Convert mm to m before computing $\sqrt{\pi a}$. A factor-of-$\sqrt{10}$ to $\sqrt{1000}$ error is the most common mistake. > 2. **Confusing $a$ (half-crack) with $2a$ (full crack):** In the central crack formula $K_I = \sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$, the symbol $a$ is the half-length. For an edge crack, $a$ is the full length. Mismatching these forfeits full marks on numericals. > 3. **Using $E$ vs $E/(1-\nu^2)$ incorrectly:** Plane stress uses $E$; plane strain uses $E/(1-\nu^2)$. If the question says "thick plate" or "plane strain", do not use plain $E$. > 4. **Neglecting $Y$ factor:** For a central crack $Y = 1.0$; for an edge crack $Y = 1.12$. Omitting $Y$ when required loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Writing "stress concentration factor" for "stress intensity factor":** These are *not* the same — examiners deduct marks for interchange. > 6. **Forgetting the energy interpretation:** $K_{IC}$ alone is not enough — link it to $G_c$ via $G_c = K_{IC}^2/E$ for full credit on derivation questions. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (must remember for KTU ESE):** > > **1. Stress Concentration Approach (Classical):** > - Inglis: $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma(1 + 2a/b)$ > - $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ (notch tip) > - Failure: $\sigma K_t = \sigma_u$ → $\sigma_c = \sigma_u / K_t$ > - **Use for:** blunt notches, ductile polymers, qualitative design > - **Fails for:** sharp cracks ($b \to 0$) > > **2. Stress Intensity Factor Approach (LEFM):** > - $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ (central); $K_I = 1.12\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ (edge) > - Failure: $K_I = K_{IC}$ > - Critical stress: $\sigma_c = K_{IC}/(Y\sqrt{\pi a})$ > - Critical crack: $a_c = K_{IC}^2/(\pi Y^2 \sigma^2)$ > - **Use for:** sharp cracks, brittle polymers, quantitative design > > **3. Energy Equivalence:** > - Plane stress: $G = K_I^2/E$ > - Plane strain: $G = K_I^2(1-\nu^2)/E$ > - $K_{IC} = \sqrt{E' G_c}$ (with $E' = E$ or $E/(1-\nu^2)$) > > **4. Plastic Zone Correction (Irwin):** > - Plane stress: $r_p = (1/2\pi)(K_I/\sigma_y)^2$ > - Plane strain: $r_p = (1/6\pi)(K_I/\sigma_y)^2$ > - Effective crack: $a_{\text{eff}} = a + r_p$ > > **5. LEFM Validity Condition:** > - $a, B, \rho \geq 2.5(K_{IC}/\sigma_y)^2$ > - For HDPE, PC → often *violated* → use J-integral / CTOD > > **6. Typical $K_{IC}$ Order (memorize):** > - Epoxy < PS ≈ PMMA < HDPE < PC < PA (Nylon) > - Higher $K_{IC}$ = tougher polymer > > **7. Practical Polymer Failure Order:** > 1. Crazing (PS, PMMA) — white stress-whitening > 2. Shear yielding (PC, PA) — ductile > 3. Mixed (HDPE, PP) — semi-ductile > 4. Brittle (epoxy, cured phenolic) — no plastic zone > > **8. Engineering Design Equation to Memorize:** > $$\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a}}$$ > > **9. Safety Factor Application:** > - $K_{I,\text{design}} = K_{IC}/n$ (typical $n = 3$ to $10$) > - Or equivalently $\sigma_{\text{design}} = \sigma_c/n$ > > **10. Why Both Theories?** > - $K_t$ → cheap, classical, OK for ductile & blunt notches > - $K_I$ → rigorous, modern, OK for sharp cracks & brittle polymers > - BOTH give same physical answer in the limit of small-scale yielding

topics.title: Fracture Predictions Based on an Energy Balance
Fracture Mechanics - Short Term and Long-Term Test Methods and Analysis: Impact Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT867 - FAILURE ANALYSIS OF POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fracture Mechanics - Short Term and Long-Term Test Methods and Analysis: Impact Strength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Impact Strength

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Fracture Mechanics** applied to polymeric materials, **Impact Strength** is defined as the *resistance of a material to fracture under a high-velocity (rapid) loading condition*, quantified as the **amount of energy absorbed per unit cross-sectional area** prior to and during fracture.

Mathematically, Impact Strength is expressed as:

$$I_s = \frac{E_{abs}}{A}$$

Where:
- $I_s$ = Impact Strength (typically in **J/m** or **kJ/m²**)
- $E_{abs}$ = Total energy absorbed during fracture (**Joules**)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area at the notch plane (m² or mm²)

In strict fracture-mechanics terminology, Impact Strength is **NOT** a fundamental material property. Instead, it is a *system response* dependent on:
1. Specimen geometry (notch radius, thickness, span)
2. Loading rate (strain rate, $\dot{\epsilon}$)
3. Temperature
4. Molecular orientation of the polymer
5. Stress state (plane stress vs plane strain)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Impact testing falls under **Short-Term Mechanical Test Methods**. It is considered "short-term" because the duration of loading is on the order of milliseconds to seconds, in contrast to long-term tests like creep (days to years) or fatigue (cyclic, hours to years).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two drinking glasses made from different plastics — one ordinary acrylic and one polycarbonate (the same material used in bullet-resistant "shatterproof" windows).

- **Drop test intuition**: If you knock both off a table onto a hard floor, the acrylic cracks into sharp pieces almost instantly, while the polycarbonate might bounce or dent without breaking.
- The polycarbonate has a *higher* **impact strength** because it *absorbs more kinetic energy* before fracture.
- This absorbed energy goes into a combination of: elastic deformation, plastic deformation, viscoelastic flow, crazing, and creation of new crack surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Definition**: Impact strength measures how "tough" a plastic is when hit suddenly — like comparing how well two different car bumpers protect passengers in a low-speed collision versus a high-speed collision.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are essential for KTU board examinations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Polymers) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus (PS) | $E$ | 1.5 – 4.0 | GPa |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | 0.30 – 0.40 | dimensionless |
| Critical Stress Intensity Factor | $K_{Ic}$ | 1.0 – 5.0 | MPa·m^(1/2) |
| Strain Rate (Impact) | $\dot{\epsilon}$ | 10² – 10⁴ | s⁻¹ |
| Glass Transition Temp | $T_g$ | 80 – 150 | °C |
| Test Temperature | $T$ | 23 ± 2 | °C (ISO 180/180-A) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Test Methods for Polymers**: KTU syllabus aligns with **ISO 180 (Izod)**, **ISO 179 (Charpy)**, **ASTM D256 (Izod)**, and **ASTM D6110 (Charpy)** for impact testing of plastics.

## 1.4 Classification: Short-Term vs Long-Term Test Methods

In **Fracture Mechanics** of polymers, mechanical testing is dichotomized as:

### Short-Term Tests (Module 2 focus)
- **Impact Tests**: Izod, Charpy, Tensile Impact, Falling Dart
- **High-Speed Tensile Tests** (strain rate ≥ 10² s⁻¹)
- **Instrumented Impact Tests** (force–time/displacement recording)

### Long-Term Tests
- **Creep Tests**: Constant load over extended time
- **Stress Cracking / ESC Tests**: Static load in aggressive media
- **Fatigue Tests**: Cyclic loading (10³ – 10⁷ cycles)
- **Weathering / Aging Tests**: Environmental exposure

> [!NOTE]
> The discriminating factor is the **characteristic time-scale** of the failure process relative to the polymer's molecular relaxation times. Impact loading rates are typically **10⁶ to 10⁸ times faster** than creep loading rates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fracture Mechanics Framework for Impact

When a polymer is struck at high velocity, two fundamental questions arise in fracture mechanics:
1. **When** does a crack initiate? → Governed by **Critical Stress Intensity Factor** $K_{Ic}$
2. **How fast** does it propagate? → Governed by **Strain Energy Release Rate** $G_c$

### 2.1.1 Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM) under Impact

For a sharp crack of length $a$ in a stressed plate, the stress field near the crack tip under mode I (opening mode) is:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cdot f_{ij}(\theta)$$

The critical condition for unstable fracture is:

$$K_I = K_{Ic}$$

Where $K_I$ is the **stress intensity factor**:

$$K_I = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$$

- $Y$ = Geometry correction factor (dimensionless, depends on crack/specimen geometry)
- $\sigma$ = Applied nominal stress
- $a$ = Crack length

### 2.1.2 Energy-Based Criterion (Griffith–Irwin)

The **strain energy release rate** $G$ under plane strain conditions:

$$G = \frac{K_I^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{E}$$

At fracture:

$$G = G_c \quad \text{(critical value)}$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Energy Absorption During Impact

The total absorbed energy $E_{abs}$ measured during an impact test is partitioned into:

$$E_{abs} = E_{elastic} + E_{plastic} + E_{viscoelastic} + E_{craze} + E_{surface}$$

Each term corresponds to a distinct physical mechanism:

| Mechanism | Dominant Polymer Type | Energy Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Elastic deformation** | All polymers | Small, recoverable |
| **Plastic deformation** | Ductile (PE, PP, PC) | Large |
| **Viscoelastic flow** | Amorphous above $T_g$ | Moderate, rate-dependent |
| **Crazing** | Glassy (PS, PMMA) | Moderate |
| **New surface creation** | Brittle fracture | Equals $G_c \cdot A_{crack}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: In ductile polymers like polycarbonate, **shear yielding** dominates energy absorption. In glassy polymers like polystyrene, **crazing** dominates. Both mechanisms are highly rate-dependent.

## 2.3 Notch Sensitivity and Stress Concentration

Impact specimens are intentionally **notched** to:
1. Localize fracture to a defined region
2. Simulate worst-case stress concentrations (sharp flaws in service)
3. Reproduce brittle vs ductile transition behavior

The stress concentration factor at a notch tip is:

$$K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{\frac{a}{\rho}}$$

Where:
- $a$ = notch depth
- $\rho$ = notch root radius

**Key relationship**: As $\rho \to 0$ (sharp notch), $K_t \to \infty$ — this is why sharp cracks are catastrophic.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_s = E_{abs}/A$ | Impact strength (energy per area) | J/m² or kJ/m² |
| 2 | $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ | Mode-I stress intensity factor | MPa·m^(1/2) |
| 3 | $G_c = K_{Ic}^2/E$ (plane stress) | Critical energy release rate | J/m² |
| 4 | $G_c = K_{Ic}^2(1-\nu^2)/E$ (plane strain) | Critical energy release rate | J/m² |
| 5 | $\sigma_f = K_{Ic}/(Y\sqrt{\pi a})$ | Fracture stress | MPa |
| 6 | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ | Stress concentration factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Striker kinetic energy | J |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \frac{\sigma_y \cdot \epsilon_p}{\rho \cdot C_p}$ | Adiabatic heating estimate | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Warning for Tables**: I have used `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol `\|` in all formula entries above to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. The vertical bar character has been replaced with the equivalent mathematical notation where necessary.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Impact testing is critical for:
- **Automotive industry**: Bumpers, dashboards, airbag housings (must survive crash loading)
- **Aerospace**: Composite panels, interior fittings
- **Packaging**: Drop tests for bottles, containers
- **Pipe systems**: PVC, HDPE pipes must survive handling impact at low temperature
- **Electronics**: Phone/tablet housings (PC/ABS blends)

In all these cases, **fracture mechanics** allows engineers to predict failure by combining impact energy data with crack/flaw size analysis:

$$\sigma_{critical} = \frac{K_{Ic}}{Y\sqrt{\pi a_{max}}}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Released per Unit Crack Area (Griffith Energy Balance)

This is the foundational fracture mechanics derivation that underpins modern impact analysis.

### Starting Point — Strain Energy in a Cracked Plate

Consider a plate of thickness $B$ containing a central crack of length $2a$. The strain energy released as the crack extends by $da$ under fixed load $P$ is:

$$U = -\frac{\pi \sigma^2 a^2 B}{E}$$

(The negative sign indicates energy release.)

### Step 1 — Differentiate Strain Energy with Respect to Crack Length

$$\frac{\partial U}{\partial a} = -\frac{2\pi \sigma^2 a B}{E}$$

### Step 2 — Surface Energy Required for New Crack Faces

Creating two new surfaces of total area $2B\,da$ requires energy:

$$W_s = 2\gamma_s \cdot B \cdot da$$

Where $\gamma_s$ is the **surface energy per unit area** (J/m²).

### Step 3 — Energy Balance Criterion (Griffith)

At incipient (critical) fracture:

$$\frac{\partial U}{\partial a} = \frac{\partial W_s}{\partial a}$$

$$-\frac{2\pi \sigma^2 a B}{E} = 2\gamma_s B$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Critical Stress

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2\gamma_s E}{\pi a}}$$

This is the classical **Griffith criterion** for brittle fracture.

### Step 5 — Convert to Stress Intensity Factor Form

Recognizing that $K_I = \sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$, we identify:

$$\sigma_c \sqrt{\pi a} = \sqrt{2\gamma_s E}$$

Therefore:

$$K_{Ic} = \sqrt{2\gamma_s E}$$

### Step 6 — Energy Release Rate $G_c$

The critical strain energy release rate (per unit crack advance, per unit thickness):

$$G_c = \frac{K_{Ic}^2}{E} = 2\gamma_s$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{G_c = \frac{K_{Ic}^2}{E} \quad \text{(plane stress)}}$$

$$\boxed{G_c = \frac{K_{Ic}^2 (1-\nu^2)}{E} \quad \text{(plane strain)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Impact Energy from Striker Pendulum Dynamics

### Setup

A pendulum of mass $m$ is raised to height $h$, then released to strike a specimen. The striker passes through the specimen, rising to a final height $h'$.

### Step 1 — Initial Potential Energy

$$E_{initial} = mgh$$

### Step 2 — Final Potential Energy (after fracture)

$$E_{final} = mgh'$$

### Step 3 — Energy Absorbed by Specimen (Conservation of Energy)

$$E_{abs} = E_{initial} - E_{final}$$

$$E_{abs} = mg(h - h')$$

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Impact Velocity

Since $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ and $v' = \sqrt{2gh'}$:

$$E_{abs} = \frac{1}{2}m(v^2 - v'^2)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Mass via Pendulum Moment of Inertia

For a pendulum of effective length $L$ rotating about its pivot:

$$I = mL^2$$

$$E_{abs} = I \cdot \omega \cdot (\omega - \omega')$$

### Step 6 — Read from Dial Scale

In commercial testers (e.g., Ceast, Tinius Olsen), $E_{abs}$ is read directly from the calibrated dial in **Joules**.

### Final Result

$$\boxed{E_{abs} = mg\Delta h = \frac{1}{2}m(v^2 - v'^2)}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Test Methods to Fracture Mechanics Parameters

| Test Method | Loading Mode | Characteristic Time | Primary Output | Fracture Mechanics Link |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Izod Impact** | Cantilever beam (3-point bending at notch) | 1 – 10 ms | Energy (J) | $E_{abs}/A$ correlates with $G_c$ |
| **Charpy Impact** | Simply supported beam (3-point bending) | 1 – 10 ms | Energy (J) | $E_{abs}/A$ correlates with $G_c$ |
| **Tensile Impact** | Uniaxial tension at high rate | 1 – 50 ms | Energy (J) | Direct $G_c$ for ductile polymers |
| **Falling Dart** | Biaxial impact (drop weight) | 1 – 20 ms | Failure height / energy | Statistical, $E_{50}$ |
| **Instrumented Impact** | Same as Izod/Charpy + force sensor | 1 – 10 ms | Force–time curve | Yields $K_{Id}$ (dynamic $K_{Ic}$) |
| **Creep (Long-term)** | Static tension | days – years | Time to failure | Time-dependent $K_I(t)$, C(t) |
| **Fatigue (Long-term)** | Cyclic tension/compression | 10³ – 10⁷ cycles | Cycles to failure | $\Delta K_{th}$, Paris law |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Impact Strength Calculation

**Problem Statement**: An Izod impact test on a notched polycarbonate specimen gives an absorbed energy of 4.8 J. The specimen cross-section at the notch has width $b = 12.7$ mm and residual (ligament) thickness $d = 10.16$ mm. Calculate the impact strength.

### Solution

**Step 1**: Identify given values
- $E_{abs} = 4.8$ J
- $b = 12.7$ mm
- $d = 10.16$ mm

**Step 2**: Calculate cross-sectional area at notch

$$A = b \times d = 12.7 \times 10.16$$

$$A = 129.03 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3**: Apply impact strength formula

$$I_s = \frac{E_{abs}}{A}$$

$$I_s = \frac{4.8 \text{ J}}{129.03 \text{ mm}^2}$$

$$I_s = 0.0372 \text{ J/mm}^2$$

**Step 4**: Convert to standard units

$$0.0372 \text{ J/mm}^2 = 0.0372 \times 10^6 \text{ J/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{I_s = 37{,}200 \text{ J/m}^2 = 37.2 \text{ kJ/m}^2}$$

This value is consistent with typical Izod impact strengths for **unfilled polycarbonate** at room temperature.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Impact Test Methodology — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Preparation] --> B[Notch Machining]
    B --> C[Conditioning at 23C / 50 percent RH]
    C --> D[Mounting in Test Fixture]
    D --> E[Izod: Cantilever / Charpy: 3-Point Bend]
    E --> F[Striker Release]
    F --> G[Energy Absorption Event]
    G --> H[Fracture]
    H --> I[Dial Reading Eabs]
    I --> J[Calculate Is = Eabs over A]
    J --> K[Report with Test Conditions]

    L[Long-Term Creep] --> M[Constant Load Application]
    M --> N[Strain Monitoring]
    N --> O[Time to Failure Recording]
    O --> P[Modulus Reduction Analysis]
    P --> Q[Creep Modulus Ect]

    R[Cyclic Fatigue] --> S[Stress Amplitude sigma]
    S --> T[Cycle Counting N]
    T --> U[S-N Curve or da-dN Analysis]
    U --> V[Threshold Delta Kth]

    K -.comparison.- L
    K -.comparison.- R
```

**Description of Flow**: The diagram maps the parallel architecture of short-term (Impact) and long-term (Creep / Fatigue) test methods, all feeding into a comparative analysis module for fracture mechanics-based failure assessment of polymers.

## 4.2 Izod vs Charpy Specimen Geometry — Comparative Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Izod Specimen ISO 180]
        A1[Length 80 mm] --> A2[Width 10 mm]
        A2 --> A3[Thickness 4 mm]
        A3 --> A4[Notch 2 mm deep]
        A4 --> A5[V-Notch Angle 45 degrees]
        A5 --> A6[Clamp: Vertical Cantilever]
    end

    subgraph B[Charpy Specimen ISO 179]
        B1[Length 80 mm] --> B2[Width 10 mm]
        B2 --> B3[Thickness 4 mm]
        B3 --> B4[Span 62 mm between supports]
        B4 --> B5[V or U Notch centered]
        B5 --> B6[Striker: Centered horizontal blow]
    end

    A -.fracture at notch.-> X[Absorbed Energy Eabs]
    B -.fracture at notch.-> X
```

**Description**: This schematic contrasts the specimen geometry, clamping configuration, and striker orientation for the two principal short-term impact tests on polymers.

## 4.3 Fracture Mechanics Decision Tree — Impact Failure Diagnosis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Impact Event Occurs] --> B{Inspect Fracture Surface}
    B -->|Smooth, mirror-like| C[Brittle Fracture]
    B -->|Rough, fibrous, stress-whitened| D[Ductile Fracture]
    B -->|Multi-plane, hackle marks| E[Mixed Mode]

    C --> C1[Low Eabs reported]
    C --> C2[High Crack Velocity]
    C --> C3[K Ic governs]
    C --> C4[Prevention: Reduce flaw size a, increase K Ic]

    D --> D1[High Eabs reported]
    D --> D2[Shear yielding visible]
    D --> D3[J integral governs]
    D --> D4[Prevention: Maintain above Tg or add rubber toughener]

    E --> E1[Intermediate Eabs]
    E --> E2[Brittle-Ductile Transition Temperature BDTT]
    E2 --> E3[Operate above BDTT for safety]
```

**Description**: A diagnostic flowchart that a KTU examiner would expect a student to follow when analyzing a fracture surface under SEM or visual inspection post-impact.

## 4.4 Energy Partitioning in Impact — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Absorbed Energy Eabs] --> B[Elastic Strain Energy]
    A --> C[Plastic Yielding]
    A --> D[Viscoelastic Damping]
    A --> E[Crazing and Microvoids]
    A --> F[New Surface Creation Gc times A]

    B -.recoverable.-> A1[Rebound]
    C -.dominant in ductile.-> A2[PC, PE, PP]
    D -.dominant above Tg.-> A3[Rubbery polymers]
    E -.dominant in glassy.-> A4[PS, PMMA]
    F -.always present.-> A5[Minimum Gc value]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Impact Strength** of a polymer. Why is it not considered a fundamental material property in fracture mechanics?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Impact strength is the **energy absorbed per unit cross-sectional area** when a polymer specimen is fractured under a high-velocity (rapid) loading event, typically measured by Izod or Charpy tests.

$$I_s = \frac{E_{abs}}{A}$$

It is *not a fundamental material property* because its value depends on **specimen geometry** (notch depth, span, thickness), **loading rate**, **temperature**, and the **stress state** (plane stress vs plane strain). Only the **critical stress intensity factor** $K_{Ic}$ or the **critical strain energy release rate** $G_c$ are true material properties in LEFM.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Distinguish between **short-term** and **long-term** mechanical test methods in polymer fracture analysis. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Short-Term Tests | Long-Term Tests |
|---|---|
| Loading duration: milliseconds – seconds | Loading duration: hours – years |
| High strain rates (10² – 10⁴ s⁻¹) | Low strain rates (< 10⁻² s⁻¹) |
| Examples: **Izod Impact, Charpy Impact, Tensile Impact, Falling Dart** | Examples: **Creep, Stress Cracking (ESC), Fatigue, Weathering** |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Izod Impact Test** for polymers as per ISO 180. Discuss how specimen geometry affects the measured impact strength. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Griffith fracture criterion** from the energy balance approach and show that the critical strain energy release rate $G_c = K_{Ic}^2/E$ for plane stress conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to (a):**

The **Izod Impact Test** is a cantilever-type impact test where a notched specimen is clamped vertically as a rigid cantilever, and a pendulum striker strikes it at a fixed distance above the notch.

**Schematic Description**: The specimen is held vertically with the notch facing the striker. The striker of known mass and impact velocity (typically 3.46 m/s) strikes the specimen 22 mm above the notch plane. The energy absorbed in breaking the specimen is recorded by the pendulum's swing.

**Test Procedure**:
1. Specimen dimensions: 80 mm × 10 mm × 4 mm
2. V-notch of 2 mm depth with 45° included angle, 0.25 mm root radius
3. Clamp specimen vertically with notch at clamp face
4. Release pendulum from known height
5. Record dial reading (energy absorbed)
6. Calculate: $I_s = E_{abs}/A$

**Effect of Specimen Geometry**:
- **Notch depth**: Sharper, deeper notches → lower impact strength (stress concentration)
- **Thickness**: Thicker specimens may shift plane stress → plane strain, lowering $I_s$
- **Width**: Affects bending stress distribution
- **Span/lever arm**: Influences bending moment at notch

**Marking Scheme (7 marks)**: Schematic (2 marks) + Procedure (2 marks) + Geometry effects (3 marks).

---

**Model Answer to (b):**

**Step 1: System Setup** — Consider a plate of thickness $B$ with a through-thickness central crack of length $2a$, subjected to uniform tensile stress $\sigma$.

**Step 2: Strain Energy Stored** — The elastic strain energy released as the crack extends is:

$$U_{released} = \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a^2 B}{E}$$

**Step 3: Surface Energy Required** — Creating new crack surfaces of area $2B \cdot da$ requires:

$$W_{surface} = 2\gamma_s B \cdot da$$

**Step 4: Griffith Energy Balance** — At incipient fracture:

$$\frac{\partial U}{\partial a} = \frac{\partial W_{surface}}{\partial a}$$

$$\frac{2\pi \sigma^2 a B}{E} = 2\gamma_s B$$

**Step 5: Critical Stress** — Solving for $\sigma$:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2\gamma_s E}{\pi a}}$$

**Step 6: Stress Intensity Factor Form** — Substituting $K_I = \sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$:

$$K_{Ic}^2 = 2\gamma_s E$$

**Step 7: Define $G_c$** — The critical strain energy release rate is the energy released per unit area of new crack:

$$G_c = \frac{\partial U}{\partial (B \cdot a)} = \frac{2\pi \sigma^2 a}{E}$$

At fracture: $G_c = 2\gamma_s = K_{Ic}^2/E$

**Step 8: Plane Strain Correction** — For thick specimens (plane strain):

$$\boxed{G_c = \frac{K_{Ic}^2 (1-\nu^2)}{E}}$$

**Marking Scheme (7 marks)**: Setup (1) + Energy expressions (2) + Balance (2) + Final derivation (2).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **Izod** and **Charpy** impact tests with respect to specimen mounting, loading mode, and fracture mechanics interpretation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An instrumented Charpy impact test on a notched polymer specimen yielded the following data: Pendulum mass = 21.6 kg, Drop height = 0.61 m, Rise height after fracture = 0.18 m, Specimen cross-section at notch = 80 mm². Calculate the **impact strength** and discuss whether the failure is likely ductile or brittle. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to (a):**

| Parameter | Izod | Charpy |
|---|---|---|
| **Specimen Mounting** | Vertical cantilever (clamped at base) | Horizontal simply-supported beam (two supports) |
| **Loading Mode** | Cantilever bending at notch tip | 3-point bending at mid-span (notch on tension side) |
| **Notch Location** | Notch at clamping face | Notch opposite striker, on tension side |
| **Striker Position** | 22 mm above notch plane | Centered at span midpoint |
| **Standards** | ISO 180, ASTM D256 | ISO 179, ASTM D6110 |
| **Fracture Mechanics Interpretation** | Localized at notch — high constraint | Distributed bending — moderate constraint |

In both tests, the absorbed energy $E_{abs}$ relates to $G_c$ via:

$$G_c \approx \frac{E_{abs}}{B \cdot (W-a)} \cdot \phi$$

Where $\phi$ is a geometry-dependent calibration factor and $(W-a)$ is the unbroken ligament.

**Marking Scheme (7 marks)**: Tabular comparison (4 marks) + Fracture mechanics link (3 marks).

---

**Model Answer to (b):**

**Step 1: Given Data**
- Pendulum mass: $m = 21.6$ kg
- Initial drop height: $h = 0.61$ m
- Rise height after fracture: $h' = 0.18$ m
- Cross-sectional area at notch: $A = 80$ mm² = $80 \times 10^{-6}$ m²
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 2: Calculate Absorbed Energy**

$$E_{abs} = mg(h - h')$$

$$E_{abs} = 21.6 \times 9.81 \times (0.61 - 0.18)$$

$$E_{abs} = 21.6 \times 9.81 \times 0.43$$

$$E_{abs} = 91.10 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Impact Strength**

$$I_s = \frac{E_{abs}}{A} = \frac{91.10}{80 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$I_s = 1.139 \times 10^6 \text{ J/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{I_s \approx 1139 \text{ kJ/m}^2}$$

**Step 4: Ductile vs Brittle Assessment**

For typical unfilled polymers:
- **Ductile range**: $I_s > 20$ kJ/m² (PC: 60–80 kJ/m²)
- **Semi-ductile**: 5–20 kJ/m² (ABS, Nylon)
- **Brittle**: $< 5$ kJ/m² (PS, PMMA)

A value of **1139 kJ/m²** is exceptionally high and suggests:
1. The specimen did **not fully fracture** (partial break / hinge break)
2. The polymer is highly ductile (likely toughened grade or fiber-reinforced)
3. ISO 179 designation would be **"NB" (No Break)** or **"P" (Partial break)**

**Conclusion**: The failure is most likely **ductile / non-fracture**, indicating excellent impact resistance. A pure fracture test result of this magnitude is physically improbable for standard polymers.

**Marking Scheme (7 marks)**: Energy calculation (3 marks) + Impact strength calculation (2 marks) + Ductile/brittle analysis (2 marks).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Impact Strength Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 to –2 marks)**: Students frequently forget to convert mm² to m² when calculating impact strength. The unit must be **kJ/m²** in SI, or **J/mm²** with explicit declaration. Mixing units leads to errors of 10⁶.
>
> 2. **Ignoring Notch Geometry (–2 marks)**: When asked about fracture mechanics, students often write $I_s = E_{abs}/A$ without discussing how notch radius $\rho$ or crack length $a$ modifies the result via $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Plane Stress vs Plane Strain (–1 mark)**: The relation $G_c = K_{Ic}^2/E$ is valid only for **plane stress**; plane strain requires the factor $(1-\nu^2)$. Examiners often test this distinction explicitly.
>
> 4. **Misclassifying Short vs Long-Term Tests (–2 marks)**: Creep and fatigue are long-term; impact is short-term. Mixing these up in classification questions results in complete loss of credit.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Justification for "Not a Material Property" (–1 mark)**: Simply stating that impact strength depends on geometry is insufficient — students must explicitly mention **at least two** of: specimen geometry, loading rate, temperature, stress state.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Impact Strength Definition**: $I_s = E_{abs}/A$ — energy per unit area at fracture under high-velocity loading.
- **Not a Material Property**: Impact strength is a *system response* — depends on geometry, rate, temperature.
- **LEFM Core Equations**:
  - $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ (stress intensity factor)
  - $K_I \geq K_{Ic}$ (fracture criterion)
  - $G_c = K_{Ic}^2/E$ (plane stress)
  - $G_c = K_{Ic}^2(1-\nu^2)/E$ (plane strain)
- **Griffith Energy Balance**: $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2\gamma_s E / (\pi a)}$ — foundational brittle fracture equation.
- **Short-Term Tests**: Izod (ISO 180), Charpy (ISO 179), Tensile Impact, Falling Dart — loading time scale = ms.
- **Long-Term Tests**: Creep, Stress Cracking, Fatigue, Weathering — loading time scale = hours to years.
- **Standard Impact Specimen Geometry**: 80 × 10 × 4 mm, V-notch 2 mm deep at 45° angle.
- **Stress Concentration**: $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ — sharp notches dramatically reduce failure stress.
- **Energy Partitioning**: $E_{abs} = E_{elastic} + E_{plastic} + E_{viscoelastic} + E_{craze} + E_{surface}$.
- **Polymer Failure Modes**: Brittle (PS, PMMA) vs Ductile (PC, PE) — characterized by yielding, crazing, shear bands.
- **Brittle-Ductile Transition Temperature (BDTT)**: A critical concept — polymers must be used **above** BDTT for impact safety.
- **Instrumented Impact**: Modern technique that captures force–time curves → dynamic $K_{Id}$ values.
- **Common Polymer Impact Values**:
  - PS: 1–2 kJ/m² (very brittle)
  - PMMA: 1.5–3 kJ/m² (brittle)
  - HIPS: 5–10 kJ/m² (toughened)
  - ABS: 10–20 kJ/m²
  - PC: 60–80 kJ/m² (very tough)
- **Master Equation Linking Everything**: $\sigma_{critical} = K_{Ic} / (Y\sqrt{\pi a})$ — used in design to set allowable flaw size for a given stress.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT867 - FAILURE ANALYSIS OF POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Impact Failure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impact Failure** in polymers is defined as the catastrophic fracture of a polymeric material subjected to a **high strain-rate loading condition**, typically characterized by loading rates exceeding **10² s⁻¹**, where the material fails by absorbing a finite amount of kinetic energy from a striker (pendulum or falling weight) in a very short time interval (milliseconds).

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, impact failure is treated as a **rate-dependent fracture phenomenon** governed by the viscoelastic nature of polymers. The impact event couples **elastic wave propagation**, **adiabatic heating**, and **crazing/shear yielding** mechanisms, distinguishing it fundamentally from quasi-static tensile or flexural failure.

## 1.2 Key Standardized Metrics

The following KTU-mandated metrics are universally tested:

- **Impact Strength (aₙ)** — Units: **J/m (Joules per meter)** for notched specimens or **kJ/m²** for unnotched specimens.
- **Total Absorbed Energy (Eₐ)** — Units: **J (Joules)**.
- **Strain Rate (ε̇)** — Units: **s⁻¹** (typically 10² to 10⁴).
- **Brittleness Temperature (T_b)** — The temperature below which impact energy drops below a threshold (commonly **27 J/m** for polymers).

## 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Frozen Ruler vs. The Warm Ruler**
> Imagine snapping a plastic ruler. At room temperature, it bends and may not break easily (tough, ductile). Now place the *same* ruler in a freezer for 2 hours and snap it — it shatters like glass. **Why?** The polymer chains have lost their mobility (reduced free volume) and cannot dissipate the impact energy through chain sliding or segmental motion. The energy that *should* be absorbed by chain rearrangement is instead concentrated at a flaw, leading to **catastrophic brittle fracture**. This is precisely what impact failure analysis studies — *how fast loading, temperature, and polymer structure conspire to convert a tough material into a brittle one.*

## 1.4 Classification of Impact Failure Modes

| Failure Mode | Energy Absorption Mechanism | Typical Appearance |
