> **Why it matters in failure analysis:** Random scission causes gradual embrittlement; unzipping causes catastrophic mass loss with little warning — both are failure modes, but require different stabiliser strategies.

### 2.1.2 Thermo-Oxidative Degradation

Follows the classical **Bolen–Cullis–Battig (BSR) Autoxidation Scheme** — a radical chain mechanism:

- **Initiation:** Polymer–H → P• + H• (catalysed by heat, light, metal impurities)
- **Propagation:** P• + O₂ → POO•; POO• + P–H → POOH + P•
- **Branching:** POOH → PO• + •OH (rate-determining; catalysed by metal ions)
- **Termination:** P• + P• → P–P (crosslink); POO• + POO• → inert products

This is a **self-accelerating** process because hydroperoxides (POOH) accumulate and decompose exothermically, raising local temperature.

### 2.1.3 Photodegradation (UV-Induced)

- UV photons (290–400 nm) carry energy of **300–415 kJ/mol**, sufficient to break most C–C (347 kJ/mol) and C–H (413 kJ/mol) bonds.
- Chromophores (carbonyls, hydroperoxides, catalyst residues) absorb UV and initiate Norrish Type I (α-cleavage) and Norrish Type II (γ-H abstraction) reactions.
- Result: surface yellowing, chalking, microcracking, and loss of tensile strength.

### 2.1.4 Hydrolytic Degradation

Affects polymers with **heteroatoms in the backbone** — polyesters, polyamides, polycarbonates, polyurethanes.

$$
\text{–CO–O–} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{–COOH} + \text{HO–}
$$

Acid- or base-catalysed; rate is highly pH-dependent. Critical for medical implants (PLA, PGA) and marine applications.

## 2.2 Stabilisation Strategies

| Stabiliser Class | Function | Typical Examples | Loading |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Antioxidants** (radical scavengers) | Donate H• to P• / POO• | Hindered phenols (Irganox 1010, BHT) | 0.1–0.5 % |
| **Secondary Antioxidants** (peroxide decomposers) | Reduce POOH to inert alcohol | Phosphites (Irgafos 168), thioesters | 0.1–0.5 % |
| **HALS** (Hindered Amine Light Stabilisers) | Scavenge radicals via nitroxyl cycle | Tinuvin 770, Chimassorb 944 | 0.1–1.0 % |
| **UV Absorbers** | Convert UV → heat | Benzotriazoles, benzophenones | 0.2–0.5 % |
| **Quenchers** | Deactivate excited chromophores | Nickel complexes | 0.1–0.3 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Synergistic Effect:** Combining a primary antioxidant (Irganox 1010) with a secondary antioxidant (Irgafos 168) at 1:1 ratio typically **doubles** the OIT compared to either alone — a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T) \cdot f(\alpha)$ | General kinetic equation (TGA) | $s^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $k = A \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius rate constant | $s^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{m_0 - m_t}{m_0 - m_\infty}$ | Fractional conversion (mass loss) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\ln\left[\dfrac{-\ln(1-\alpha)}{T^2}\right] = \ln\left[\dfrac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right] - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | Coats–Redfern (1st order) | — |
| 5 | $E_a = \dfrac{2.303 \, R \, T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1} \log\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$ | Two-point $E_a$ from Arrhenius | kJ/mol |
| 6 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Half-life (1st order) | s |
| 7 | $T_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_p}{\Delta S_p}$ | Ceiling temperature (thermodynamic) | K |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{T_c} - \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{R}{\Delta H_p}\ln[\text{M}]$ | Equilibrium polymerisation ceiling temp | K |
| 9 | $L = L_0 \exp(-k t)$ | Property loss (exponential decay) | varies |
| 10 | $Q_{10} = 2^{\frac{E_a}{2.303 \, R} \cdot \frac{10}{T_1 T_2}}$ | Acceleration factor per 10 °C | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** In the table above, $\beta$ = heating rate (K/min), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), and $\alpha$ = extent of degradation. The Coats–Redfern equation is the **most frequently asked** derivation in KTU Module 3 examinations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Carbon-fibre/epoxy composites require $T_d > 400$ °C and char yield $> 50$ % for fire, smoke, toxicity (FST) compliance.
- **Medical Implants:** PLA/PGA sutures are designed to lose 50 % tensile strength in 2–4 weeks *in vivo* — controlled hydrolytic degradation.
- **Solar Modules:** EVA encapsulant must survive 25 years of UV/thermal cycling; HALS + UV absorber packages extend service life.
- **Automotive Under-the-Hood:** PP/EPDM blends with stabiliser packages rated to 150 °C continuous service.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Coats–Redfern Equation for Activation Energy of Thermal Degradation

The Coats–Redfern method is a **closed-form integral solution** of the Arrhenius kinetic equation applied to non-isothermal TGA data. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

### Step 1: Start with the Fundamental Kinetic Equation

For a solid-state thermal degradation, the rate of conversion is:

$$
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T) \cdot (1-\alpha)^n
$$

where $n$ is the reaction order. Assuming first-order kinetics ($n=1$, valid for most polymer degradations):

$$
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T) \cdot (1-\alpha)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Arrhenius Rate Constant

$$
\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) (1-\alpha)
$$

### Step 3: Convert to Temperature Domain

In TGA, the heating rate $\beta = dT/dt$ is constant. Therefore:

$$
d\alpha = \frac{A}{\beta} \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) (1-\alpha) \, dT
$$

### Step 4: Separate Variables and Integrate

$$
\int_0^{\alpha} \frac{d\alpha}{1-\alpha} = \frac{A}{\beta} \int_{T_0}^{T} \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) dT
$$

The LHS integrates to $-\ln(1-\alpha)$. The RHS is the **Arrhenius integral**, which has no closed-form elementary solution.

### Step 5: Apply the Coats–Redfern Asymptotic Expansion

The exponential is sharply peaked. Using the substitution $u = E_a/RT$ and the asymptotic expansion for the integral (valid when $E_a/RT \gg 1$):

$$
\int_{T_0}^{T} \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) dT \approx \frac{R T^2}{E_a} \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \left[ 1 - \frac{2RT}{E_a} \right]
$$

### Step 6: Neglect the Higher-Order Term and Rearrange

For $E_a \gg 2RT$ (true for most polymers where $E_a > 80$ kJ/mol and $T < 800$ K), the bracketed term reduces to 1. Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
\ln\left[\frac{-\ln(1-\alpha)}{T^2}\right] = \ln\left[\frac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right] - \frac{E_a}{RT}
$$

### Step 7: Recognise the Linear Form

This is a **straight-line equation** $y = mx + c$ when plotted as:

$$
\underbrace{\ln\!\left[\frac{-\ln(1-\alpha)}{T^2}\right]}_{y}
=
\underbrace{-\frac{E_a}{R}}_{m}
\cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{T}}_{x}
+
\underbrace{\ln\!\left[\frac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right]}_{c}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Use:** A TGA experiment is run at three heating rates (say 5, 10, 20 K/min). For each, the LHS is plotted vs $1/T$. The slope gives $-E_a/R$, hence $E_a = -(\text{slope}) \times 8.314$ J/mol. This is a **14-mark KTU favourite**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ceiling Temperature ($T_c$) from Thermodynamics

The ceiling temperature is the temperature above which **depolymerisation is thermodynamically favoured** over polymerisation. Derivation from Gibbs free energy:

### Step 1: Write the Equilibrium Reaction

$$
M_n \rightleftharpoons nM
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta H_p - T_c \Delta S_p = 0
$$

### Step 3: Solve for $T_c$

$$
T_c = \frac{\Delta H_p}{\Delta S_p}
$$

This is the standard form (Eq. 7 in the formula sheet).

### Step 4: Include Monomer Concentration Effect

If monomer concentration $[M]$ deviates from standard state:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta H_p - T \Delta S_p + RT \ln[M] = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{1}{T_c} - \frac{1}{T} = \frac{R}{\Delta H_p}\ln[M]
$$

This is Eq. 8 in the formula sheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** For PMMA, $\Delta H_p \approx -56$ kJ/mol, $\Delta S_p \approx -117$ J/(mol·K) → $T_c \approx 478$ K (205 °C). This explains why PMMA depolymerises quantitatively when heated above 220 °C.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Two-Point Activation Energy

**Problem:** A polymer loses 5 % mass at 380 °C and 50 % mass at 430 °C under TGA at 10 K/min in N₂. Assuming first-order kinetics, estimate the activation energy if the rate constant ratio is $k_{430}/k_{380} = 8.5$.

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 380 + 273 = 653$ K
- $T_2 = 430 + 273 = 703$ K
- $k_2/k_1 = 8.5$
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Solution (Equation 5):**

$$
E_a = \frac{2.303 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 653 \cdot 703}{703 - 653} \log_{10}(8.5)
$$

$$
E_a = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 459{,}059}{50} \times 0.9294
$$

$$
E_a = \frac{8{,}338{,}944}{50} \times 0.9294
$$

$$
E_a = 166{,}779 \times 0.9294 \approx 155{,}000 \text{ J/mol} = 155 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Interpretation:** $E_a = 155$ kJ/mol is typical for backbone C–C bond scission in polyolefins, confirming the proposed mechanism.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Stabiliser Based on Failure Mode

| Failure Mode (Observed Symptom) | Dominant Mechanism | Recommended Stabiliser Package |
|---|---|---|
| Yellowing + surface chalking (outdoor) | UV-induced Norrish reactions | HALS (0.5 %) + UV absorber (0.3 %) |
| Embrittlement after oven aging | Thermo-oxidative chain scission | Phenolic AO (0.2 %) + phosphite (0.2 %) |
| Black specks / gel formation | Crosslinking via radical recombination | Phenolic AO + lactone (carbon-black-free) |
| Loss of MW in moist environment | Hydrolytic ester cleavage | Carbodiimide chain extender or PU-grade stabiliser |
| Smoke + acid emission (PVC fire) | HCl elimination + zip dehydrochlorination | Zn/Ca stearate + $\text{Mg(OH)}_2$ smoke suppressant |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Thermo-Oxidative Degradation Cycle (BSR Autoxidation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Initiation: Polymer-H] --> nodeB[Alkyl Radical P-dot]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Add O2]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Peroxy Radical POO-dot]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Abstract H from P-H]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Hydroperoxide POOH]
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeF --> nodeG[Branch: POOH decomposes]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Alkoxy PO-dot + Hydroxyl OH-dot]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Carbonyls, chain scission, crosslinks]
    nodeB --> nodeJ[Termination: P-dot + P-dot]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Termination: POO-dot + POO-dot]
    nodeJ --> nodeI
    nodeK --> nodeI
```

**Reading the diagram:** The blue loop (B → C → D → E → B) is the **propagation cycle** — note that step E regenerates B, creating a self-sustaining radical chain. This is why stabilisers must *interrupt* this cycle (HALS and phenolic antioxidants).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of a Polymer Stabilisation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[ENVIRONMENTAL STRESSORS]
        nodeS1[Heat]
        nodeS2[UV Photons]
        nodeS3[Oxygen]
        nodeS4[Moisture]
    end
    subgraph POLY[POLYMER MATRIX]
        nodeP1[Backbone Bonds]
        nodeP2[Side Groups]
        nodeP3[Amorphous Regions]
    end
    subgraph STAB[STABILISER PACKAGE]
        nodeA1[Primary AO: H-donor]
        nodeA2[Secondary AO: POOH decomposer]
        nodeA3[HALS: Nitroxyl cycle]
        nodeA4[UV Absorber]
    end
    subgraph OUT[FAILURE MANIFESTATIONS]
        nodeF1[Embrittlement]
        nodeF2[Discolouration]
        nodeF3[Mass Loss]
        nodeF4[Cracking]
    end
    nodeS1 --> nodeP1
    nodeS2 --> nodeP2
    nodeS3 --> nodeP1
    nodeS4 --> nodeP3
    nodeA1 -. scavenges .-> nodeP1
    nodeA2 -. decomposes .-> nodeP1
    nodeA3 -. regenerates .-> nodeA1
    nodeA4 -. filters .-> nodeS2
    nodeP1 --> nodeF1
    nodeP1 --> nodeF3
    nodeP2 --> nodeF2
    nodeP3 --> nodeF4
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: TGA Curve Interpretation (Failure Analysis Tool)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Raw TGA Curve: mass percent vs T] --> node2[Identify Td onset 5 percent mass loss]
    node1 --> node3[Identify Td50 50 percent mass loss]
    node1 --> node4[Identify Char Yield at 800 C]
    node1 --> node5[Apply Coats-Redfern at 3 heating rates]
    node2 --> node6[Compare with database]
    node3 --> node6
    node4 --> node7[Fire performance rating]
    node5 --> node8[Activation Energy Ea]
    node8 --> node9[Mechanism identification]
    node6 --> node10[Failure Analysis Report]
    node7 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:** The TGA instrument outputs raw mass-loss data; the analyst extracts three characteristic temperatures and the char yield, runs the Coats–Redfern analysis on data from multiple heating rates, and synthesises a failure diagnosis report.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "Oxidative Induction Time (OIT)" and state its significance in assessing polymer stability. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (3 marks):** Oxidative Induction Time (OIT) is defined as the time elapsed between the onset of oxygen exposure (at a constant elevated temperature, typically 200 °C for polyolefins) and the beginning of the exothermic oxidation reaction, as detected by **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** under an oxygen atmosphere.
>
> **Significance:** OIT is a **direct measure of the effectiveness of antioxidant packages** in a polymer. A longer OIT indicates a more stable formulation. The test is standardised under **ASTM D3895** and is routinely used in quality control of cable insulation, pipe-grade HDPE, and food-contact packaging. A reduction in OIT of more than 20 % relative to a reference sample signals that the antioxidant has been depleted and the material is approaching end-of-service life.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between random chain scission and chain-end unzipping with one example for each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Random Chain Scission (1.5 marks):** Statistical breaking of backbone bonds at random positions, leading to a rapid drop in molecular weight and broadening of the molecular weight distribution. Example: Thermal degradation of **polyethylene (PE)** and **polypropylene (PP)** in inert atmosphere.
>
> **Chain-End Unzipping / Depolymerisation (1.5 marks):** Sequential, monomer-by-monomer release starting from the chain end (often an active radical or anion). Yields > 90 % monomer with little change in molecular weight of the residual polymer until late in the reaction. Example: Thermal degradation of **poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA)** which yields > 95 % methyl methacrylate monomer above 300 °C, and **polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)** which unzips to give tetrafluoroethylene monomer.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q A. (a)** With the help of a suitable kinetic equation, derive the **Coats–Redfern equation** for the determination of activation energy of polymer thermal degradation from TGA data. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Starting Equation (1 mark):** For first-order thermal degradation:
> $$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) (1-\alpha)$$
>
> **Step 2 — Convert to Temperature Domain (1 mark):** With constant heating rate $\beta = dT/dt$:
> $$\int_0^\alpha \frac{d\alpha}{1-\alpha} = \frac{A}{\beta}\int_{T_0}^{T}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) dT$$
>
> **Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral (2 marks):** Using the asymptotic expansion of the Arrhenius integral (valid when $E_a/RT \gg 1$):
> $$\int_{T_0}^{T}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) dT \approx \frac{RT^2}{E_a}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$
>
> **Step 4 — Rearrange to Linear Form (2 marks):** Taking the natural logarithm:
> $$\ln\!\left[\frac{-\ln(1-\alpha)}{T^2}\right] = \ln\!\left[\frac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right] - \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Plot and Extract (1 mark):** Plot LHS vs $1/T$ at a single heating rate; the slope $= -E_a/R$, hence $E_a = -\text{slope} \times 8.314$ J/mol. For greater accuracy, repeat at three heating rates (5, 10, 20 K/min).
>
> **Assumptions:** (i) first-order kinetics; (ii) $E_a \gg 2RT$ (allows higher-order term in the expansion to be dropped); (iii) $\alpha$ is well-defined from mass-loss data; (iv) single-step degradation mechanism in the temperature window analysed.

---

**Q A. (b)** Explain the **Bolen–Cullis–Battig autoxidation scheme** for thermo-oxidative degradation of polymers. Discuss the role of primary and secondary antioxidants in interrupting this cycle. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **The BSR Autoxidation Scheme (4 marks):** The mechanism comprises four phases:
>
> *(i) Initiation:* Thermal, photochemical, or metal-ion-catalysed homolysis of a C–H bond produces the first alkyl radical:
> $$\text{P–H} \xrightarrow{\Delta, h\nu, \text{M}^{n+}} \text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$$
>
> *(ii) Propagation:* The alkyl radical rapidly adds oxygen (rate constant $\approx 10^9$ L/mol·s) to form a peroxy radical, which then abstracts a hydrogen from another polymer chain, regenerating a new alkyl radical:
> $$\text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{POO}^{\bullet}$$
> $$\text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{P–H} \rightarrow \text{POOH} + \text{P}^{\bullet}$$
>
> *(iii) Branching:* Hydroperoxides (POOH) accumulate and decompose (rate-determining), producing alkoxy and hydroxyl radicals that initiate new chains:
> $$\text{POOH} \rightarrow \text{PO}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$
>
> *(iv) Termination:* Radical recombination gives inert (or crosslinked) products:
> $$\text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{P}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{P–P} \quad \text{(crosslink)}$$
> $$\text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{POO}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{POO–POO}$$
>
> **Role of Primary Antioxidants (1.5 marks):** Hindered phenols (e.g., Irganox 1010, BHT) donate a hydrogen atom to the propagating $\text{P}^{\bullet}$ or $\text{POO}^{\bullet}$, converting them to inert products and themselves becoming a **stable resonance-stabilised phenoxyl radical** that does not propagate the chain:
> $$\text{Ar–OH} + \text{POO}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{Ar–O}^{\bullet} + \text{POOH}$$
>
> **Role of Secondary Antioxidants (1.5 marks):** Phosphites (e.g., Irgafos 168) and thioesters **reduce hydroperoxides** to inert alcohols *before* they can undergo the branching reaction, thereby cutting off the auto-acceleration step:
> $$\text{P(OR)}_3 + \text{POOH} \rightarrow \text{P(OR)}_3{=}\text{O} + \text{POH}$$
>
> **Synergy:** Primary AOs intercept the radical chain; secondary AOs prevent radical generation at source. Used together, they produce a synergistic OIT enhancement of 2–4×.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q B. (a)** Define **ceiling temperature** and **floor temperature**. Derive the relationship between ceiling temperature, enthalpy of polymerisation, and entropy of polymerisation. How does monomer concentration affect $T_c$? **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions (1 mark):**
> - **Ceiling temperature ($T_c$):** The temperature above which the free energy of polymerisation becomes positive, i.e., depolymerisation is thermodynamically favoured.
> - **Floor temperature ($T_f$):** The temperature below which the free energy of depolymerisation becomes positive for chains that have already been formed — relevant for polymers formed by ring-opening or condensation equilibria.
>
> **Derivation (3 marks):** For the equilibrium $M_n \rightleftharpoons nM$, at equilibrium $\Delta G = 0$:
> $$\Delta G = \Delta H_p - T_c \Delta S_p = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_c = \frac{\Delta H_p}{\Delta S_p}$$
> For addition polymerisation, $\Delta H_p < 0$ and $\Delta S_p < 0$ (entropy decreases on polymerisation). Hence $T_c > 0$ K. For $\alpha$-methylstyrene, $\Delta H_p = -35$ kJ/mol, $\Delta S_p = -110$ J/(mol·K) gives $T_c = 318$ K (45 °C) — this is why $\alpha$-methylstyrene cannot be polymerised above 45 °C in bulk.
>
> **Effect of Monomer Concentration (3 marks):** When monomer concentration $[M]$ deviates from standard state (1 M), the activity term must be included:
> $$\Delta G = \Delta H_p - T\Delta S_p + RT\ln[M] = 0$$
> Solving for $T_c$ at concentration $[M]$:
> $$\frac{1}{T_c} - \frac{1}{T_{c,0}} = \frac{R}{\Delta H_p}\ln[M]$$
> where $T_{c,0}$ is the ceiling temperature at standard concentration. **Diluting the monomer reduces $T_c$** (makes polymerisation harder), while **concentrating or applying pressure raises $T_c$** — a principle exploited in industrial bulk polymerisation of styrene and methyl methacrylate.

---

**Q B. (b)** Compare the mechanisms of **thermal degradation** of (i) polyethylene, (ii) PVC, and (iii) PMMA. For each, identify the dominant mechanism, the major volatile products, and a suitable stabilisation strategy. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Polymer | Mechanism | Major Volatile Products | Stabilisation Strategy |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Polyethylene (PE)** | Random chain scission via free-radical β-scission of secondary C–C bonds | Wide range of alkanes, alkenes, waxes (C₁–C₅₀) | Primary phenolic AO (0.1–0.3 %) + secondary phosphite AO; carbon black for UV |
> | **Poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC)** | Side-group elimination (dehydrochlorination) → polyene sequences → crosslinking/aromatisation | HCl (dominant, > 60 %), benzene, toluene, alkyl aromatics | **Acid scavengers** (Zn/Ca stearate, 1–3 %); **primary AO** (bisphenol A); co-stabilisers (β-diketones, epoxidised soybean oil) |
> | **PMMA** | Chain-end unzipping (depolymerisation) initiated at unsaturated chain ends | Methyl methacrylate monomer (yield > 90 %) | Radical initiators removed during synthesis; chain-end-capping agents; for high-temp service, use of **acrylic-PMMA copolymer** with higher $T_d$ |
>
> **Engineering Inference (1 mark):** For PE, antioxidant depletion is the limiting factor; for PVC, HCl evolution and polyene discoloration are the failure signatures; for PMMA, monomer release (and consequent fire toxicity) is the concern. These distinctions are critical in failure analysis: a single stabiliser package cannot serve all three.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 — Stability of Polymer Structures:**
>
> 1. **Coats–Redfern Derivation (up to −3 marks):** Students often forget the **assumption $E_a \gg 2RT$** that justifies dropping the higher-order term in the asymptotic expansion. Always state the assumptions explicitly.
>
> 2. **Units Mismatch (up to −2 marks):** Activation energy must be reported in **kJ/mol**, not J/mol, unless otherwise specified. Forgetting to convert is a frequent error.
>
> 3. **Confusing OIT and OOT (Oxidative Onset Temperature):** OIT is **isothermal** (time-based, minutes). OOT is **temperature-ramped** (temperature-based, °C). Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Stabiliser Mis-Assignment:** UV absorbers (benzotriazoles) do *not* scavenge radicals; only HALS do. Phenolic antioxidants do *not* absorb UV; only benzophenones/benzotriazoles do.
>
> 5. **Ceiling Temperature Sign Convention:** $\Delta H_p$ is **negative** for exothermic polymerisation; $\Delta S_p$ is **negative** for entropy loss. If you write them positive, you will get a negative $T_c$, signalling a fundamental conceptual error.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Radical Symbol:** In BSR scheme diagrams, *every* radical must carry the dot (e.g., $P^{\bullet}$, $POO^{\bullet}$). Examiners deduct marks for sloppy notation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Stability is kinetic, not thermodynamic** — always use Arrhenius for rate prediction.
- [x] The **BSR autoxidation scheme** has 4 stages: Initiation → Propagation → Branching → Termination.
- [x] **Primary AOs** (phenols) trap radicals; **Secondary AOs** (phosphites) destroy hydroperoxides.
- [x] **HALS** work via the **Denisov cycle** of nitroxyl regeneration — they are catalytic, not stoichiometric.
- [x] **Coats–Redfern** is the standard TGA kinetic method — a $y = mx + c$ plot with slope $-E_a/R$.
- [x] **Ceiling temperature** $T_c = \Delta H_p / \Delta S_p$ governs the upper polymerisation limit.
- [x] **OIT** (isothermal, minutes) ≠ **OOT** (ramped, °C) — do not confuse.
- [x] **PMMA** unzips to monomer; **PE** random scissions; **PVC** eliminates HCl — three distinct mechanisms.
- [x] **TGA metrics to report:** $T_{d,5\%}$, $T_{d,50\%}$, **char yield at 800 °C**, and $E_a$ from Coats–Redfern.
- [x] **Rule of 10 °C:** Polymer lifetime approximately halves for every 10 °C rise in service temperature.
- [x] UV photons (290–400 nm) carry **300–415 kJ/mol** — sufficient to cleave C–C (347 kJ/mol) and C–H (413 kJ/mol) bonds.
- [x] **HCl evolution** from PVC is autocatalysed — must be scavenged with metal stearates or epoxides.
- [x] In KTU derivations, **always state assumptions** and **show all intermediate steps** — partial credit is awarded generously for correctly framed starting equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Effect on Polymer Failure - Weather
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT867 - FAILURE ANALYSIS OF POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Environmental Effect on Polymer Failure - Weather

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Weathering in Polymers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weathering (KTU 2024 Definition):** Weathering is the collective physico-chemical degradation of polymeric materials caused by prolonged exposure to the **combined and synergistic action** of environmental stressors — namely **ultraviolet (UV) radiation, atmospheric oxygen, moisture (humidity/rain), heat, and temperature fluctuations** — leading to irreversible changes in chemical structure, molecular weight, morphology, surface energy, color, and mechanical performance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, weathering is classified as a **passive environmental failure mode** (distinguished from *active* mechanical or thermal failure modes) because the polymer is typically not under significant external load during the degradation process; rather, the environment itself acts as the degradation agent.

### Weathering Triad

> [!NOTE]
> The three primary weathering vectors acting on polymers are:
> 1. **Photochemical Stress** (UV photons, typically **290–400 nm**)
> 2. **Hydrolytic Stress** (water vapor, rainfall, condensation)
> 3. **Thermal Cycling Stress** (diurnal and seasonal temperature swings, often **−40 °C to +85 °C**)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sun-Bleached Car Dashboard:**
> Imagine a car dashboard left in the open sun for three years. It cracks, fades, becomes brittle, and the surface feels chalky. The polymer did not "break" mechanically — the **sun (UV), heat, and humidity gradually attacked the molecular chains**. This is exactly what happens in polymer weathering. The macromolecular chains absorb UV photons, break at weak bonds, react with oxygen (oxidation), and absorb water (hydrolysis). The cumulative effect is the **same end result**: a polymer that looks, feels, and performs like a much older, inferior material.

The same phenomenon affects polyethylene greenhouse films, PVC pipes exposed outdoors, EPDM roofing membranes, automotive coatings, and polymer insulators in substations.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in Weathering Studies

The following metrics are universally used in KTU-validated weathering studies:

- **Carbonyl Index (CI)** — measured by FTIR at **~1715 cm⁻¹**, dimensionless
- **Yellowing Index (YI)** — measured per **ASTM D1925 / E313**
- **UV Dose** — units: **MJ/m²** or **kWh/m²**
- **Xenon Arc Irradiance** — **0.35 W/m² @ 340 nm** (per ASTM G155)
- ** Gloss Retention (%)** — measured at **60°** per **ASTM D523**
- **Molecular Weight Retention (Mn, Mw)** — via **GPC/SEC**
- **Elongation-at-Break Retention (%)** — per **ASTM D638 / D882**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** When asked to *define weathering*, students **must** mention the **synergistic** nature of the three stressors — UV, moisture, and heat acting together. A definition that only mentions UV or only mentions moisture will be marked incomplete by KTU evaluators.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanistic Classification of Weathering Degradation

Weathering failures arise from **three concurrent and inter-coupled chemical mechanisms**. In KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation, examiners expect students to **name the mechanism, write the initiation step, and identify the end-group signature in FTIR**.

### 2.1.1 Photo-Oxidation (The Dominant Mechanism)

Photo-oxidation is a **free-radical chain reaction** initiated by absorption of UV photons by chromophoric groups (C=O, C=C, peroxide residues, catalyst fragments) in the polymer.

**Step 1 — Initiation (Photolysis of chromophore):**

$$\text{PH} + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation (Oxygen incorporation):**

$$\text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{POO}^{\bullet}$$

$$\text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{PH} \longrightarrow \text{POOH} + \text{P}^{\bullet}$$

**Step 3 — Branching (Hydroperoxide decomposition):**

$$\text{POOH} + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{PO}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$

**Step 4 — Termination:**

$$\text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{POO}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{POOP} + \text{O}_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FTIR Signature of Photo-oxidation:** A broad carbonyl absorption band appears at **~1715 cm⁻¹**, hydroperoxide band at **~3400 cm⁻¹**, and trans-vinylene band at **~965 cm⁻¹**. The **carbonyl index** is the most widely cited quantitative marker in KTU exam answers.

### 2.1.2 Norrish Reactions (Chain Scission Pathways)

For polymers containing carbonyl groups in the backbone (e.g., **polyethylene, polypropylene, polycarbonate, PET**), the C=O acts as an internal UV chromophore and undergoes:

> [!NOTE]
> **Norrish Type I** — α-cleavage producing two radicals:
> $$\text{R–CO–R'} + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{R–CO}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{R'}$$
> This causes **direct chain scission** and rapid molecular weight loss.
>
> **Norrish Type II** — intramolecular H-transfer via a six-membered transition state producing a ketone, alkene, and chain scission. Dominant in **polyethylene** and **polypropylene**.

### 2.1.3 Hydrolytic Degradation

Hydrolysis affects polymers with hydrolyzable linkages: **polyesters (PET, PLA, PBAT), polyamides (Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6), polycarbonates, polyurethanes, and polyanhydrides**.

$$\text{–[CO–O–]}_n + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{–[CO–OH]} + \text{[HO–]–}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hydrolysis is **strongly autocatalytic** in polyesters because the liberated carboxylic acid end-group **catalyzes further ester cleavage** — a phenomenon that frequently appears in KTU 14-mark questions on packaging or biomedical polymer failure.

### 2.1.4 Thermal Cycling & Thermo-Oxidative Aging

Diurnal thermal cycling introduces **differential expansion**, microcrack propagation at the surface, and accelerated diffusion of oxygen and moisture into the bulk. Thermo-oxidation follows the same radical mechanism as photo-oxidation but is **initiated thermally** rather than photochemically.

### 2.1.5 Synergistic Effect — The Real-World Reality

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common mistake is to treat UV, moisture, and heat as independent variables. In practice, they are **highly synergistic**. Heat accelerates oxidation kinetics; UV photolyzes hydroperoxides; moisture swells the polymer and increases oxygen permeability. **Examiners specifically look for the word "synergistic" in 14-mark answers.**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a high-yield consolidation of every weathering-related equation a KTU 2024 Scheme student is expected to know. **Memorize these equations verbatim.**

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Beer–Lambert Law (UV penetration)** | $I(x) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon c x}$ | $I_0$ = incident intensity, $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity, $c$ = chromophore concentration, $x$ = depth into polymer |
| **Arrhenius Equation (Rate of degradation)** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $k$ = rate constant, $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| **Carbonyl Index (CI)** | $\text{CI} = \dfrac{A_{1715}}{A_{\text{ref}}}$ | $A_{1715}$ = absorbance at 1715 cm⁻¹, $A_{\text{ref}}$ = reference peak (e.g., 1370 cm⁻¹ for PE/PP) |
| **Degree of Hydrolysis (α)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{[\text{COOH}]_t}{[\text{COOH}]_{\infty}}$ | Used to track hydrolytic chain scission in polyesters |
| **Oxidation Induction Time (OIT)** | $\text{OIT} = \dfrac{t_{\text{onset}}}{\text{sample mass}}$ | DSC method per ASTM D3895; indicator of stabilizer effectiveness |
| **Accelerated Weathering Time Conversion** | $t_{\text{field}} = \text{AF} \cdot t_{\text{lab}}$ | AF = acceleration factor; xenon arc AF typically **5–15×** for Central European climate |
| **Time-Temperature Superposition** | $\log a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_{\text{ref}})}{C_2 + (T - T_{\text{ref}})}$ | WLF equation; $C_1 \approx 17.44$, $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K (universal constants) |
| **Degradation Half-Life (1st order)** | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{deg}}}$ | Applicable when CI grows exponentially with exposure time |
| **Effective UV Dose** | $D_{\text{UV}} = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} E(\lambda) \, d\lambda \, dt$ | Units: **MJ/m²**; xeno chamber: 0.35 W/m² @ 340 nm |
| **Elongation Retention** | $R_\varepsilon = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{t}}{\varepsilon_{0}} \times 100\%$ | $R_\varepsilon < 50\%$ typically defines **embrittlement failure** per ISO 4892 |

---

## 2.3 Why and How: Engineering Significance

**Why this matters in industry:**

1. **Polyolefin greenhouse films** in Kerala's tropical climate receive annual UV doses exceeding **250 MJ/m²** — equivalent to **2–3 years of Northern European exposure** in just one year. This is why KTU modules emphasize weathering extensively: Kerala's geography makes this a regional-industrial priority.
2. **Polymer insulators** in substations fail by tracking and erosion accelerated by UV + acid rain.
3. **Automotive polyurethane dashboards** fail by surface cracking within **2–4 years** in hot, humid climates.
4. **PLA and PHB biopolymers** used in packaging are hydrolytically unstable and lose molecular weight rapidly at **> 60% RH**.

**How the theory translates to practice:**

- Stabilizer packages (**HALS, UV absorbers, antioxidants**) are dosed based on Arrhenius-predicted lifetimes.
- **Q-Lab QUV** and **Atlas Xenon Arc** chambers simulate years of outdoor exposure in weeks.
- FTIR carbonyl index is the **routine QC metric** for incoming raw material verification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Arrhenius-Based Lifetime Prediction

The Arrhenius relationship is the cornerstone of all KTU weathering lifetime predictions. Below is the **exhaustive, line-by-line derivation** that examiners expect in a 14-mark answer.

### Step 1 — Start from the integrated first-order rate law

For a first-order degradation reaction where the property of interest (e.g., elongation, molecular weight) decays exponentially:

$$P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{-k_{\text{deg}} \cdot t}$$

where $P_0$ is the initial property value, $P(t)$ is the value at exposure time $t$, and $k_{\text{deg}}$ is the degradation rate constant.

### Step 2 — Rearrange for failure time

Define the **failure criterion** $P_{\text{fail}}$ (e.g., 50% elongation retention). Then:

$$P_{\text{fail}} = P_0 \cdot e^{-k_{\text{deg}} \cdot t_{\text{fail}}}$$

Dividing both sides by $P_0$:

$$\frac{P_{\text{fail}}}{P_0} = e^{-k_{\text{deg}} \cdot t_{\text{fail}}}$$

### Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{fail}}}{P_0}\right) = -k_{\text{deg}} \cdot t_{\text{fail}}$$

$$t_{\text{fail}} = \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_{\text{fail}}}\right)}{k_{\text{deg}}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Arrhenius expression for k

Since $k_{\text{deg}} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$:

$$t_{\text{fail}} = \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_{\text{fail}}}\right)}{A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}}$$

### Step 5 — Invert to obtain a form amenable to log-linear plotting

$$\frac{1}{t_{\text{fail}}} = \frac{A}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_{\text{fail}}}\right)} \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{t_{\text{fail}}}\right) = \ln A - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{P_0}{P_{\text{fail}}}\right)\right] - \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}$$

### Step 6 — Final working form

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{t_{\text{fail}}}\right) = \underbrace{\left\{\ln A - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_{\text{fail}}}\right)\right]\right\}}_{\text{constant}} \;-\; \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A plot of $\ln(1/t_{\text{fail}})$ versus $1/T$ yields a straight line with **slope $= -E_a/R$**. The activation energy $E_a$ can therefore be determined experimentally from accelerated aging at **three or more elevated temperatures**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (14-mark type):** A polypropylene garden chair is rated for a **5-year** outdoor service life in Kerala (average effective service temperature **35 °C = 308 K**). The carbonyl index growth follows first-order kinetics with an activation energy of **$E_a = 78 \text{ kJ/mol}$**. The failure criterion is **CI ≥ 0.30**. Accelerated aging at **85 °C (358 K)** indicates that CI reaches 0.30 in **250 hours**. Estimate the actual outdoor lifetime and comment on whether the chair design meets its 5-year warranty.

### Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin

$$T_1 = 308 \text{ K}, \qquad T_2 = 358 \text{ K}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the two-temperature Arrhenius ratio

Using $\ln(k_2/k_1) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right) = \frac{78{,}000}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{308} - \frac{1}{358}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the bracket

$$\frac{1}{308} - \frac{1}{358} = \frac{358 - 308}{308 \cdot 358} = \frac{50}{110{,}264} = 4.535 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the exponential argument

$$\frac{78{,}000}{8.314} \times 4.535 \times 10^{-4} = 9381.3 \times 4.535 \times 10^{-4} = 4.255$$

### Step 5 — Compute the rate constant ratio

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{4.255} = 70.31$$

### Step 6 — Compute the outdoor lifetime

Since $k \propto 1/t_{\text{fail}}$:

$$\frac{t_{1}}{t_{2}} = \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}} = 70.31$$

$$t_1 = 70.31 \times 250 = 17{,}578 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 7 — Convert to years

$$t_1 = \frac{17{,}578}{24 \times 365} = \frac{17{,}578}{8760} = 2.01 \text{ years}$$

### Step 8 — Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The predicted outdoor lifetime is approximately **2 years**, **substantially less than the 5-year warranty**. The chair will fail prematurely — most likely by surface chalking and embrittlement. The recommended corrective actions are: (i) addition of **0.3–0.5 wt% HALS** (e.g., Tinuvin 770), (ii) incorporation of **2–3 wt% carbon black** as a UV screen, and (iii) specification of a thicker section or a UV-stable topcoat. **The design as analyzed does not meet the 5-year warranty requirement.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Weathering Mechanisms by Polymer Class

| Polymer Class | Dominant Weathering Mechanism | Key Functional Group Attacked | Primary FTIR Marker (cm⁻¹) | Stabilizer Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyethylene (PE)** | Norrish II photo-oxidation | C=O formed from oxidation of C–H | 1715, 965 | HALS + UV absorber (benzotriazole) |
| **Polypropylene (PP)** | Photo-oxidation + chain scission | Tertiary C–H | 1715, 3400 | HALS + hindered phenol AO |
| **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** | Dehydrochlorination + photo-oxidation | C–Cl, allylic C–H | 1730, 1610 | Tin stabilizer + TiO₂ pigment |
| **Polycarbonate (PC)** | Photo-Fries rearrangement | Carbonate C–O | 3490 (OH), 1680 | UV absorber + aromatic stabilizer |
| **PET / Polyester** | Hydrolysis + Norrish I | Ester C–O | 1715, 3270 (OH) | Carbodiimide hydrolytic stabilizer |
| **Polyamide (Nylon 6,6)** | Hydrolysis + photo-oxidation | Amide C–N | 1640, 3300 | HALS + phenolic AO |
| **Polyurethane (PU)** | Photo-oxidation of ester/ether segment | Urethane C–O | 1730, 1530 | HALS + antioxidant package |
| **EPDM Rubber** | Crosslink scission by ozone + UV | C=C backbone | 965, 1710 | Antiozonant (wax + PPD) |
| **PLA (biopolymer)** | Hydrolysis (ester cleavage) | C–O | 1715 | Crystallinity control + blending |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Carbonyl Index Determination (FTIR Workflow)

The carbonyl index is **the single most important quantitative weathering marker** in KTU assessments.

### Step 1 — Baseline Correction

The FTIR spectrum is baseline-corrected in the region **1660–1800 cm⁻¹** by drawing a straight line between the absorbance minima.

### Step 2 — Peak Area Integration

The area under the carbonyl band is integrated:

$$A_{1715} = \int_{1660}^{1800} A(\tilde{\nu}) \, d\tilde{\nu}$$

### Step 3 — Reference Peak Area

A reference band that does **not change during degradation** is used. For polyethylene, the **1370 cm⁻¹** band (CH₃ bending) is standard:

$$A_{\text{ref}} = \int_{1330}^{1410} A(\tilde{\nu}) \, d\tilde{\nu}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate CI

$$\text{CI} = \frac{A_{1715}}{A_{\text{ref}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked "Why use a reference peak?", the answer is: *to normalize for variations in sample thickness, contact pressure, and instrument path length, ensuring that CI changes reflect only true chemical modification.*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Weathering Failure Cascade

The following diagram illustrates the **cascading failure pathway** from environmental exposure to macroscopic mechanical failure. This is the exact framework KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark answer.

```mermaid

    B --> C[Radical Formation Initiation Step]
    C --> D[Oxygen Incorporation Propagation]
    D --> E[Hydroperoxide Buildup Branching]
    E --> F[Chain Scission + Crosslinking]
    F --> G[Molecular Weight Reduction Mw drop]
    G --> H[Crystallinity Increase Embrittlement]
    H --> I[Surface Microcracking]
    I --> J[Gloss Loss + Color Shift]
    J --> K[Macroscopic Failure]
    K --> L[Mechanical Property Loss Elongation drop below 50%]
    L --> M[Service Life Exceeded]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define polymer weathering and list the three primary environmental stressors responsible for it.**

**Model Answer:**

> Polymer weathering is the irreversible physico-chemical degradation of a polymeric material resulting from prolonged exposure to outdoor environmental conditions. The **three primary environmental stressors** are:
> 1. **Ultraviolet (UV) radiation** in the range **290–400 nm** (photochemical stress)
> 2. **Moisture** in the form of humidity, rainfall, dew, and condensation (hydrolytic stress)
> 3. **Thermal cycling** from diurnal and seasonal temperature variations (thermo-oxidative stress)
>
> These three factors act **synergistically**, accelerating each other's degradation pathways, and their combined action leads to changes in molecular weight, surface appearance, color, and mechanical properties.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the carbonyl index, and why is it considered the most reliable indicator of polymer photo-oxidation?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **carbonyl index (CI)** is a dimensionless FTIR-derived metric defined as the ratio of the absorbance (or integrated peak area) of the carbonyl stretching band at **~1715 cm⁻¹** to that of a reference band that is unaffected by degradation (e.g., **1370 cm⁻¹** for PE/PP).
>
> **Why CI is the most reliable indicator:**
> 1. It directly quantifies the **concentration of C=O groups**, which are produced stoichiometrically by photo-oxidation.
> 2. It is **insensitive to sample thickness** and instrumental drift because of the internal reference normalization.
> 3. It **correlates linearly** with the loss of mechanical properties (elongation, tensile strength).
> 4. It is **non-destructive** and can be monitored on the same specimen over time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain with relevant equations the **photo-oxidation mechanism** of polyethylene, including the role of **Norrish Type II** reactions. Identify the characteristic FTIR signatures that confirm this mechanism.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A **HDPE geomembrane** used in a landfill capping system in Kerala is expected to last **50 years** under field conditions (average service temperature **33 °C = 306 K**). Laboratory accelerated aging at **80 °C (353 K)** yields an **OIT (Oxidation Induction Time) reduction to 50%** in **1800 hours**. The activation energy for HDPE oxidative degradation is **$E_a = 83 \text{ kJ/mol}$**. Using the Arrhenius approach, determine whether the geomembrane will meet its 50-year design life. Suggest **two stabilizer interventions** to extend the life if it falls short.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Photo-oxidation of Polyethylene (7 Marks)

Polyethylene, although structurally simple (–CH₂–CH₂–), contains residual chromophoric impurities from polymerization — typically **carbonyl groups, hydroperoxides, and catalyst residues**. These absorb UV photons in the **290–400 nm** range and initiate the radical chain.

**Initiation Step:**

$$\text{PH} + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$$

**Propagation Step (oxygen incorporation):**

$$\text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{POO}^{\bullet}$$

$$\text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{PH} \longrightarrow \text{POOH} + \text{P}^{\bullet}$$

**Norrish Type II Reaction** (dominant in PE):
Once a carbonyl is introduced into the backbone, it acts as an internal chromophore. Absorption of UV by the C=O triggers a six-membered cyclic transition state:

$$\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_3 + \text{CH}_2\text{=CH–} + \text{CO}$$

This produces a **terminal methyl group**, a **terminal vinyl (trans-vinylene) group**, and a **chain scission event** — all three are experimentally verifiable.

**Branching (Hydroperoxide photolysis):**

$$\text{POOH} + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{PO}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$

**Termination (radical coupling):**

$$\text{POO}^{\bullet} + \text{POO}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{POOP} + \text{O}_2$$

**Characteristic FTIR Signatures:**
| Band (cm⁻¹) | Assignment | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1715** | C=O stretch (ketone/aldehyde/acid) | Carbonyl index |
| **965** | Trans-vinylene (–CH=CH–) out-of-plane bend | **Direct evidence of Norrish II** |
| **3400** | O–H stretch of hydroperoxides and alcohols | Branching intermediate |
| **910** | Terminal vinyl (CH₂=CH–) | Chain end from Norrish II |

> The simultaneous appearance of bands at **1715** and **965 cm⁻¹** is the **fingerprint** of Norrish II-dominated photo-oxidation, and this is the precise combination that KTU examiners reward.

---

#### Part (b) — Arrhenius Lifetime Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**
$T_1 = 306 \text{ K}$, $T_2 = 353 \text{ K}$

**Step 2 — Compute the inverse temperature difference:**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{306} - \frac{1}{353} = \frac{353 - 306}{306 \times 353} = \frac{47}{108{,}018} = 4.351 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Arrhenius exponential term:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{83{,}000}{8.314} \times 4.351 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 9983.2 \times 4.351 \times 10^{-4} = 4.344$$

**Step 4 — Compute the rate constant ratio:**

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{4.344} = 77.16$$

**Step 5 — Compute the field lifetime:**

Since $k \propto 1/t_{\text{fail}}$:

$$t_{\text{field}} = t_{\text{lab}} \times \frac{k_2}{k_1} = 1800 \times 77.16 = 138{,}888 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to years:**

$$t_{\text{field}} = \frac{138{,}888}{8760} = 15.86 \text{ years}$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion and Recommendation:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Result:** The predicted field lifetime is approximately **16 years**, **far short of the 50-year design requirement**. The geomembrane as currently formulated **does NOT meet the service life target**.
>
> **Recommended stabilizer interventions:**
> 1. **Add 2.5–3.0 wt% carbon black** — provides excellent UV screening and antioxidant activity (the standard practice in HDPE geomembranes per GRI-GM13).
> 2. **Add a HALS package (e.g., 0.3 wt% Chimassorb 944) combined with a hindered phenolic antioxidant (0.15 wt% Irganox 1010)** — HALS scavenges propagating radicals in a regenerative cycle, while the phenolic AO decomposes hydroperoxides.

With these interventions, the activation energy effectively increases (often to **120–130 kJ/mol**), and lifetime extension by a factor of **5–10×** is achievable, comfortably meeting the 50-year requirement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) (7 Marks)** Discuss the **hydrolytic degradation mechanism** of **PET** used in outdoor applications. Explain why hydrolysis is **autocatalytic** in polyesters and identify the **end-group signatures** in FTIR and NMR spectra.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the **WLF (Williams–Landel–Ferry) equation** and its use in **time-temperature superposition** for predicting the long-term weathering behavior of polymer films. State the typical values of the WLF constants and discuss the **limitations** of this approach.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Hydrolytic Degradation of PET (7 Marks)

PET (poly(ethylene terephthalate)) contains the ester linkage **–O–CO–C₆H₄–CO–O–CH₂–CH₂–**, which is susceptible to hydrolytic cleavage:

**Mechanism:**

$$\text{–[CO–O–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–]}_n + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{–[COOH]} + \text{[HO–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–]–}$$

**Why hydrolysis is autocatalytic:**
The reaction produces a **carboxylic acid (–COOH) end-group**, which is itself a catalyst for ester hydrolysis. The carbonyl oxygen of the carboxyl group is protonated, activating the carbonyl carbon for nucleophilic attack by water. This **acid-catalyzed pathway** is far faster than the neutral hydrolysis pathway, so as more ester bonds cleave, more acid is liberated, accelerating further cleavage — a positive-feedback loop.

**Quantitatively**, the degradation follows autocatalytic kinetics often modeled as:

$$[\text{COOH}](t) = [\text{COOH}]_0 + k_{\text{acid}} \cdot \int_0^t [\text{COOH}](\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Factors accelerating PET hydrolysis:**
- **High humidity (> 80% RH)**
- **Temperature > 60 °C**
- **Low crystallinity** (amorphous regions hydrolyze 10–100× faster than crystalline regions)
- **Alkaline pH** (saponification)

**Spectroscopic Signatures:**
- **FTIR:** Appearance of broad **–OH stretch at 3200–3500 cm⁻¹** and **carboxylic acid C=O at 1680–1700 cm⁻¹** (shifted from the original ester C=O at 1715 cm⁻¹).
- **¹H NMR:** New peaks corresponding to **–COOH** (≈ 8–10 ppm), **–CH₂–OH** (≈ 3.7 ppm), and **–CH₂–OCO–** next to free acid (≈ 4.4 ppm).
- **¹³C NMR:** Ester carbonyl carbon at **~165 ppm** shifts to **~170 ppm** in the free acid.
- **SEC/GPC:** Progressive decrease in $M_n$ and $M_w$, often with broadening of the dispersity $(M_w/M_n)$.

> **Practical implication:** PET bottles stored in hot, humid warehouses can lose **30–50% of molecular weight in 6 months**, severely compromising mechanical performance. This is the basis of KTU 14-mark questions on packaging failures.

---

#### Part (b) — WLF Equation and Time-Temperature Superposition (7 Marks)

**The WLF Equation:**

$$\log a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_{\text{ref}})}{C_2 + (T - T_{\text{ref}})}$$

where:
- $a_T$ = horizontal shift factor required to superpose a curve measured at temperature $T$ onto a reference curve measured at $T_{\text{ref}}$
- $C_1$, $C_2$ = **WLF constants**
- **Universal (apparent) values at $T_{\text{ref}} = T_g$:** $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6 \text{ K}$

**Engineering Application in Weathering:**

Time-temperature superposition (TTS) allows the **long-term weathering behavior** of a polymer to be predicted from **short-term measurements at elevated temperatures**. A master curve of modulus or elongation is constructed at a reference temperature by horizontally shifting curves obtained at other temperatures by $\log a_T$.

For example, if a polymer film's 1-year outdoor aging data at 35 °C is needed but only 7-day laboratory data at 75 °C is available, the WLF equation predicts the shift factor:

$$a_T = 10^{-\frac{17.44 \times (75 - 35)}{51.6 + (75 - 35)}} = 10^{-\frac{17.44 \times 40}{91.6}} = 10^{-7.61} \approx 2.45 \times 10^{-8}$$

Wait — actually, in TTS for aging, $a_T$ is typically computed to map laboratory aging time onto field aging time. The **shift factor gives the time-scaling equivalence**: $t_{\text{field}} = a_T \cdot t_{\text{lab}}$ when $T_{\text{lab}} > T_{\text{field}}$. So 7 days at 75 °C corresponds to $7 / 2.45 \times 10^{-8}$ ≈ **3,260 years** — an apparent result that is unrealistic because **TTS breaks down at very large shifts**.

**Limitations of the WLF / TTS Approach:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitations to mention in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Valid only above $T_g$** (for amorphous polymers). Below $T_g$, the WLF equation loses physical meaning.
> 2. **Only one dominant relaxation mode** is assumed. Polymers with multiple transitions (e.g., semicrystalline polymers like HDPE) require modification.
> 3. **Thermo-rheologically simple** behavior is assumed — no change in the chemical structure during the experiment. In weathering, **chemical degradation is occurring**, so the polymer is **not** thermorheologically simple. Pure TTS without accounting for chemistry over-predicts life.
> 4. **Moisture coupling is not addressed** — humidity-dependent shifts require the **Knauss–Emri** or **fiber-bundle model** extensions.
> 5. **Extrapolation beyond $T_{\text{ref}} \pm 30 \text{ K}$** is unreliable.

> **KTU Examiner's expectation:** A student who lists 3+ limitations and recommends combining WLF with Arrhenius for chemical aging is rewarded with **full 7 marks**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the word "synergistic"** when describing UV + moisture + heat: **−2 marks** in 14-mark answers.
> - **Confusing Norrish I and Norrish II**: Norrish I = α-cleavage into two radicals (chain scission); Norrish II = intramolecular H-transfer (also chain scission but produces vinyl + ketone). Examiners want **both** named and **distinguished**.
> - **Not identifying the FTIR signature of weathering**: Any 14-mark answer without a table of FTIR peaks will lose **at least 2 marks**.
> - **Mixing up Arrhenius with WLF**: Arrhenius governs **chemical degradation** (rate constants); WLF governs **physical relaxation** (viscoelastic shift). Conflating the two is a **fatal** conceptual error.
> - **Omitting the reference peak** when defining carbonyl index: Always state that a **non-degrading reference band** is used.
> - **Forgetting to convert to Kelvin**: Many students plug °C into Arrhenius directly. This is the **#1 numerical error** in KTU exam scripts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- **Weathering = Synergistic action of UV (290–400 nm) + moisture + thermal cycling.**
- **Photo-oxidation** is a **free-radical chain** reaction: **Initiation → Propagation → Branching → Termination.**
- **Carbonyl index** at **1715 cm⁻¹** is the gold-standard weathering metric; reference band at **1370 cm⁻¹** (PE/PP).
- **Norrish II** dominates in PE/PP; produces **trans-vinylene (965 cm⁻¹)** — a diagnostic marker.
- **Norrish I** = α-cleavage into radicals; **Norrish II** = 6-membered cyclic H-transfer producing alkene + ketone.
- **Hydrolysis** is **autocatalytic** in polyesters because –COOH groups catalyze further ester cleavage.
- **Arrhenius** governs chemical degradation: $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$; **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
- **WLF** governs physical relaxation above $T_g$: $\log a_T = -C_1 (T - T_{\text{ref}})/[C_2 + (T - T_{\text{ref}})]$ with **C₁ = 17.44, C₂ = 51.6 K**.
- **HALS** = cyclic secondary amines that scavenge radicals in a regenerative cycle — the most effective modern stabilizer class.
- **Carbon black (2–3 wt%)** is the most cost-effective UV screen for HDPE and EPDM.
- **Kerala's tropical climate** delivers **~250 MJ/m²** annual UV dose — among the highest in India.
- **QUV (ASTM G154)** uses fluorescent UV lamps; **Xenon Arc (ASTM G155)** simulates full solar spectrum.
- **Failure criterion for embrittlement**: typically **50% retention of elongation-at-break**.
- **Activation energies**: PE/PP photo-oxidation **~75–90 kJ/mol**; hydrolysis of PET **~80–100 kJ/mol**; oxidation with carbon black + HALS **~120–140 kJ/mol**.
- **Accelerated-to-field time conversion**: $t_{\text{field}} = \text{AF} \cdot t_{\text{lab}}$, where AF (acceleration factor) is typically **5–15×** for xenon arc chambers.
- **Yellowness Index** (ASTM E313) tracks discoloration — increases during UV aging of polyolefins.
- **OIT** (Oxidation Induction Time, ASTM D3895) measures residual stabilizer capacity via DSC.
- **Synergistic weathering × stabilizer** understanding is **the most testable KTU 2024 concept** for 14-mark answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT867 - FAILURE ANALYSIS OF POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Thermal Failure of Polymers

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-Equivalent)**
> **Thermal failure of polymers** refers to the irreversible, time-dependent loss of mechanical, chemical, rheological, or electrical functionality of a polymeric material when subjected to elevated temperatures (or thermal cycling) in the presence or absence of an oxidative atmosphere. It encompasses thermal degradation, thermal oxidation, thermal aging, and thermally-induced physical transitions (e.g., loss of dimensional stability above the glass-transition temperature, $T_g$, or heat-deflection temperature, HDT).

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based framework, this topic is mapped to **CO3** of the *Failure Analysis of Polymers* course:
> *"Apply thermogravimetric and calorimetric techniques to characterize the thermal failure thresholds of polymers and predict service-life using kinetic models."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cooking a piece of chicken in a pan. At **low heat**, nothing dramatic happens — proteins just slowly denature. At **medium heat**, the surface browns (Maillard reaction) and the meat shrinks (water loss + protein coagulation). At **very high heat**, it chars, releases smoke, and eventually burns into carbon. Polymers behave in a remarkably similar way, except the "cooking" is happening at the molecular level:

- **Low temperature ($<$ $T_g$)**: Polymer chains vibrate but remain locked in their glassy state — serviceable.
- **Around $T_g$**: Chain segments gain mobility — material softens, dimensions change — this is the **Heat-Deflection-Temperature (HDT)** region.
- **Above $T_m$ (for semi-crystalline polymers)**: Crystallites melt — the part loses all load-bearing capacity.
- **Above the *onset of decomposition*** ($T_d^{onset}$): C–C and C–H bonds break — irreversible chemical failure (chain scission, cross-linking, charring, depolymerisation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Answers**
> Physical thermal failure (softening, distortion) is **reversible** upon cooling (if no chemistry has occurred). Chemical thermal failure (chain scission, oxidation, charring) is **permanent and irreversible**. Examiners always reward students who explicitly state this reversibility contrast.

### Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following metrics are universally required in any KTU 2024 answer on thermal failure:

- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** — unit: **K** or **°C**
- **Melting Temperature ($T_m$)** — unit: **K** or **°C**
- **Onset Decomposition Temperature ($T_d^{onset}$)** — typically measured at **5 % mass loss** in TGA
- **Maximum Decomposition Temperature ($T_d^{max}$)** — peak of DTG curve
- **Heat Distortion Temperature (HDT)** — measured under a flexural load of **0.45 MPa** or **1.82 MPa** (ASTM D648)
- **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI)** — minimum oxygen fraction (%) to sustain combustion
- **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314$ **J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Standard Reference Heating Rates** in TGA: $\beta = 5, 10, 20$ **K·min⁻¹**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Thermal Failure

For KTU 2024 board answers, the examiner expects a **layered** explanation. The hierarchy is:

| Layer | Mechanism | Reversibility | Typical Diagnostic |
|-------|-----------|---------------|--------------------|
| L1 | Sub-$T_g$ physical aging (enthalpy relaxation) | Reversible by re-heating above $T_g$ | DSC (sub-$T_g$ endotherm) |
| L2 | Thermo-mechanical failure (softening, creep above $T_g$) | Reversible (geometric) | DMA, HDT test |
| L3 | Thermo-oxidative failure (radical chain scission at moderate T with O₂) | Irreversible | FTIR (carbonyl peak), OIT |
| L4 | Pyrolytic / anaerobic thermal degradation (high T, inert atm.) | Irreversible | TGA / DTG under N₂ |
| L5 | Flammability / combustion (LOI, HRR) | Irreversible | Cone calorimeter |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Thermal Degradation

### Step 1 — Initiation
A C–C or C–H bond absorbs thermal energy $h\nu_{thermal}$ and undergoes **homolytic scission**, producing two free radicals:

$$ \text{Polymer–R–R'–Polymer} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Polymer–R}^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}\text{R'–Polymer} $$

### Step 2 — Propagation
The radical abstracts hydrogen or undergoes **β-scission**, generating smaller radicals and often a monomer (in the case of depolymerisation, e.g., PMMA → methyl methacrylate).

### Step 3 — Branching (especially under O₂)
Oxygen reacts with alkyl radicals to form peroxyl radicals, leading to an **auto-accelerating** cycle (the *Bolland–Gee* mechanism for hydrocarbon oxidation).

### Step 4 — Termination
Radicals couple or disproportionate, yielding stable end products — often including **char**, **volatile oligomers**, and **low-MW gases** (CO, CO₂, H₂O, HCl from PVC, HCN from acrylonitriles, SO₂ from sulfonated polymers).

### Step 5 — Mass-Loss Manifestation in TGA
The cumulative mass-loss curve $m(T)$ is the integrated result of all the above elementary steps; differentiating yields the **DTG curve**, whose peak defines $T_d^{max}$.

## 2.3 Why and How Each Step Matters

- **Why initiation matters**: The bond dissociation energy (BDE) dictates the threshold temperature. C–C BDE ≈ **347 kJ·mol⁻¹**, C–H BDE ≈ **413 kJ·mol⁻¹** — therefore, *C–C backbone scission precedes C–H abstraction* in most thermoplastics.
- **Why propagation matters**: Unzipping (depolymerisation) preserves the repeat unit — useful for chemical recycling (e.g., polystyrene back to styrene at 400 °C).
- **Why oxygen matters**: O₂ lowers the apparent activation energy by ~80–100 kJ·mol⁻¹, so **thermo-oxidative failure occurs at temperatures 100–150 K below pyrolytic failure**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|----------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius rate constant, $A$ pre-exponential factor (s⁻¹), $E_a$ activation energy (J·mol⁻¹), $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ | Predict lifetime at lower T from accelerated TGA data |
| 2 | $\ln\!\left(-\ln(1-\alpha)\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right) - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | $\alpha$ fractional mass loss, $\beta$ heating rate | **Coats–Redfern** method (integral method, $n=1$) |
| 3 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{T_{max}^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{E_a}\right) - \dfrac{E_a}{RT_{max}}$ | $T_{max}$ peak DTG temperature | **Kissinger** method (multiple heating rates) |
| 4 | $\ln \beta = \text{const} - 1.052\,\dfrac{E_a}{RT_{max}}$ | Ozawa–Flynn–Wall (OFW) method | Independent kinetic analysis |
| 5 | $\ln\!\left(-\ln(1-\alpha)\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{RT} + \text{const}$ | Broido's method for first-order | Quick $E_a$ from single TGA curve |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{R}{E_a}\ln A + \dfrac{R}{E_a}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{E_a}\right)$ | — | Derived from Kissinger rearrangement |
| 7 | $\text{Service Life } t_f = \dfrac{\ln(\alpha_f) - \ln(\alpha_0)}{A \exp(-E_a/RT)}$ | $t_f$ time-to-failure at service $T$ | Lifetime extrapolation |
| 8 | $\text{Char Yield} = \dfrac{m_{900\,^{\circ}\text{C}}}{m_{30\,^{\circ}\text{C}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Mass remaining at 900 °C (TGA, N₂) | Indicator of flame retardancy |
| 9 | $\eta_{eff}(T) = \eta_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_{\eta}}{RT}\right)$ | Viscosity temperature dependence | WLF / Arrhenius flow above $T_g$ |
| 10 | $\text{LOI} = 17.5 + 0.4 \cdot \text{CY}$ | Van Krevelen empirical relation; CY = char yield (%) | Estimating flame retardancy from TGA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any **14-mark derivations** in KTU, the **Coats–Redfern** and **Kissinger** methods are the two most-likely derivations. Memorize the assumptions (first-order, single-step, $n=1$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Carbon-fibre / PEEK composites must retain $E > 100$ GPa up to 250 °C; thermal failure analysis determines the upper service ceiling.
- **Electrical/Electronics**: Epoxy moulding compounds in IC packaging must survive 260 °C solder reflow; TGA verifies $T_d^{onset} > 350$ °C.
- **Automotive under-hood**: Polyamide 6,6 (PA66) sustains 180 °C continuous; lifetime is extrapolated from Arrhenius plots.
- **Pipeline coatings**: HDPE used at 60 °C for 50 years — service life predicted from Arrhenius extrapolation of OIT data.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The Arrhenius Lifetime Equation

**Statement (KTU 2024 standard):**
The fractional mass loss $\alpha$ of a polymer undergoing first-order thermal degradation follows
$$ \dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A\,(1-\alpha)\,e^{-E_a/RT} $$
Show that the **time-to-failure** $t_f$ at a constant service temperature $T_s$ is
$$ t_f = \dfrac{-\ln(1-\alpha_f)}{A\,e^{-E_a/RT_s}} $$

### Derivation

We treat $\alpha$ as the fraction of bonds broken; $(1-\alpha)$ is the fraction remaining, and $A$ carries units of s⁻¹. Integrating from $\alpha = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $\alpha = \alpha_f$ at $t = t_f$, with $T = T_s$ constant:

$$ \int_{0}^{\alpha_f}\!\dfrac{d\alpha}{1-\alpha} \;=\; A\,e^{-E_a/RT_s}\int_{0}^{t_f}\! dt $$

Evaluate the left-hand integral:

$$ \Bigl[-\ln(1-\alpha)\Bigr]_{0}^{\alpha_f} = -\ln(1-\alpha_f) - (-\ln 1) = -\ln(1-\alpha_f) $$

The right-hand integral gives $A\,e^{-E_a/RT_s}\cdot t_f$. Equating:

$$ -\ln(1-\alpha_f) = A\,e^{-E_a/RT_s}\cdot t_f $$

Rearrange for $t_f$:

$$ t_f = \dfrac{-\ln(1-\alpha_f)}{A\,e^{-E_a/RT_s}} $$

which is the **result claimed**. Taking $\ln$ of both sides gives the *linear* form used to plot $\ln t_f$ vs $1/T$ — this is the standard KTU Arrhenius master plot for **service-life extrapolation**.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Kissinger Equation

**Statement:**
For a non-isothermal TGA experiment at multiple heating rates $\beta_i$, the peak DTG temperature $T_{max,i}$ satisfies
$$ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta_i}{T_{max,i}^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{E_a}\right) - \dfrac{E_a}{RT_{max,i}} $$

### Derivation

The general rate equation for a single-step solid-state reaction (applicable to polymer decomposition) is

$$ \dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T)\,f(\alpha) = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}\,f(\alpha) $$

with $f(\alpha)$ the kinetic model function. For a constant heating rate, $\beta = dT/dt$, we change variables:

$$ \dfrac{d\alpha}{dT} = \dfrac{A}{\beta}\,e^{-E_a/RT}\,f(\alpha) $$

The peak of the DTG curve occurs at $T_{max}$, which by definition is where $d^{2}\alpha/dt^{2} = 0$. Differentiating the rate equation with respect to $t$ and applying the chain rule $d/dt = \beta\,d/dT$:

$$ \dfrac{d^{2}\alpha}{dt^{2}} = \beta\!\left[\dfrac{d}{dT}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{\beta}e^{-E_a/RT}f(\alpha)\right)\right] $$

Applying the product rule and noting that at the peak $\dfrac{d^{2}\alpha}{dt^{2}}=0$ and (by definition of $f(\alpha)$ peak condition) $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT_{max}^{2}}\right) = \dfrac{A}{\beta}e^{-E_a/RT_{max}}f'(\alpha_{max})$:

$$ \dfrac{E_a\,A}{RT_{max}^{2}}\,e^{-E_a/RT_{max}}f(\alpha_{max}) = \dfrac{A}{\beta}\,e^{-E_a/RT_{max}}f'(\alpha_{max}) $$

Cancelling $A\,e^{-E_a/RT_{max}}$ on both sides:

$$ \dfrac{E_a}{RT_{max}^{2}}\,f(\alpha_{max}) = \dfrac{f'(\alpha_{max})}{\beta} $$

For a first-order reaction, $f(\alpha) = (1-\alpha)$, so $f'(\alpha) = -1$. Also, at the peak of DTG (which corresponds to the inflection of $\alpha$ vs $T$), $(1-\alpha_{max})$ cancels out, yielding:

$$ \dfrac{E_a}{RT_{max}^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\beta}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \dfrac{\beta}{T_{max}^{2}} = \dfrac{E_a}{RT_{max}} $$

Wait — strictly Kissinger's *full* result requires an additional assumption. Substituting into the original derivative and taking logarithms, one obtains:

$$ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta_i}{T_{max,i}^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{E_a}\right) - \dfrac{E_a}{RT_{max,i}} $$

Plotting $\ln(\beta/T_{max}^{2})$ against $1/T_{max}$ for several $\beta$ values gives a straight line with slope $-E_a/R$, from which **$E_a$** is extracted, and intercept $\ln(AR/E_a)$ gives **$A$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **always** ask for the slope = $-E_a/R$ and intercept = $\ln(AR/E_a)$. Memorize these — they are 2 easy marks.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Coats–Redfern Integral Method

The integral form of the rate equation is

$$ g(\alpha) \equiv \int_{0}^{\alpha}\!\dfrac{d\alpha}{f(\alpha)} = \dfrac{A}{\beta}\!\int_{T_0}^{T}\!e^{-E_a/RT}\,dT $$

Using the asymptotic expansion $\int e^{-E_a/RT}dT \approx \dfrac{RT^{2}}{E_a}\!\left(1-\dfrac{2RT}{E_a}\right)e^{-E_a/RT}$ and dropping the small second-order term, for first-order $g(\alpha) = -\ln(1-\alpha)$:

$$ -\ln(1-\alpha) = \dfrac{ART^{2}}{\beta E_a}\,e^{-E_a/RT}\!\left(1-\dfrac{2RT}{E_a}\right) $$

Since $2RT/E_a \ll 1$ for typical values ($E_a \sim 150$ kJ·mol⁻¹), the correction term is dropped:

$$ -\ln(1-\alpha) \approx \dfrac{ART^{2}}{\beta E_a}\,e^{-E_a/RT} $$

Taking $\ln$ of both sides:

$$ \ln\!\left[-\ln(1-\alpha)\right] = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right) + 2\ln T - \dfrac{E_a}{RT} $$

Since $2\ln T$ varies much more slowly than $1/T$, treating it as part of the intercept yields:

$$ \ln\!\left[-\ln(1-\alpha)\right] \approx \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right) - \dfrac{E_a}{RT} $$

This is the **Coats–Redfern linearised form**: a plot of $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)]$ vs $1/T$ yields slope $-E_a/R$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A polymer sample shows the following TGA data at $\beta = 10$ K·min⁻¹:

| $T$ (K) | Mass remaining (%) |
|---------|--------------------|
| 600 | 96.5 |
| 650 | 88.2 |
| 700 | 71.4 |
| 750 | 41.6 |
| 800 | 12.3 |

Calculate $E_a$ using the **Coats–Redfern** method.

**Solution:**

Compute $\alpha = 1 - m/m_0$ and $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)]$:

| $T$ (K) | $\alpha$ | $1/T$ (×10⁻³ K⁻¹) | $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)]$ |
|---------|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 600 | 0.035 | 1.6667 | $\ln[-\ln 0.965] = \ln(0.0356) = -3.336$ |
| 650 | 0.118 | 1.5385 | $\ln[-\ln 0.882] = \ln(0.1257) = -2.073$ |
| 700 | 0.286 | 1.4286 | $\ln[-\ln 0.714] = \ln(0.3366) = -1.089$ |
| 750 | 0.584 | 1.3333 | $\ln[-\ln 0.416] = \ln(0.8770) = -0.131$ |
| 800 | 0.877 | 1.2500 | $\ln[-\ln 0.123] = \ln(2.096) = 0.740$ |

Linear regression of $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)]$ vs $1/T$:

$$ \text{slope} = \dfrac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^{2}} $$

$\bar{x} = 1.4434\times 10^{-3}$, $\bar{y} = -1.178$.

Numerator (in units of 10⁻³ K⁻¹):
$(1.6667-1.4434)(-3.336+1.178) = (0.2233)(-2.158) = -0.4819$
$(1.5385-1.4434)(-2.073+1.178) = (0.0951)(-0.895) = -0.0851$
$(1.4286-1.4434)(-1.089+1.178) = (-0.0148)(0.089) = -0.00132$
$(1.3333-1.4434)(-0.131+1.178) = (-0.1101)(1.047) = -0.1153$
$(1.2500-1.4434)(0.740+1.178) = (-0.1934)(1.918) = -0.3709$
**Sum ≈ −1.054**

Denominator (in units of 10⁻⁶ K⁻²):
$(0.2233)^{2} + (0.0951)^{2} + (0.0148)^{2} + (0.1101)^{2} + (0.1934)^{2}$
$= 0.0499 + 0.00904 + 0.00022 + 0.01212 + 0.03740 = 0.1087$

So slope $= -1.054/0.1087 = -9.696$ (in units of K, since numerator was 10⁻³·unitless and denominator 10⁻⁶·K² → units of K).

Wait — let me recheck the units. $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)]$ is dimensionless, $1/T$ has units K⁻¹. So:

$$ \text{slope} = \dfrac{-1.054}{0.1087\times 10^{-3}} = -9696 \text{ K} $$

Therefore:

$$ -E_a/R = -9696 \implies E_a = 9696 \times 8.314 = 80{,}613 \text{ J·mol}^{-1} \approx \mathbf{80.6 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical $E_a$ for thermo-oxidative degradation of a polyolefin (PE/PP). Pure pyrolytic PE has $E_a \approx 200{-}250$ kJ·mol⁻¹.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use?

| Method | Data Required | Heating Rates | Order Assumption | KTU 2024 Difficulty |
|--------|---------------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Broido | Single TGA curve | 1 | First order | Easy |
| Coats–Redfern | Single TGA curve | 1 | First order (or $n$) | Moderate |
| Kissinger | Multiple $\beta$ → $T_{max}$ | ≥ 3 | None specific | Moderate–Hard |
| Ozawa–Flynn–Wall | Multiple $\beta$ | ≥ 3 | None specific | Hard |
| Friedman (differential) | Multiple $\beta$ | ≥ 3 | None | Hard |
| **ASTM E1640** (Tonset 5%) | Single TGA | 1 | — | Empirical |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Thermal Failure Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[In-Service Polymer Part] --> B{Failure Observed}
    B -- Visual/Mechanical --> C[Visual + Microscopy]
    B -- Thermal Symptom --> D[TGA Sample Prep]
    C --> E[FTIR + SEM + DSC]
    D --> F[TGA under N2]
    D --> G[TGA under Air]
    F --> H[DTG Curve Analysis]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Thermal Mechanism?}
    I -- Single sharp DTG peak --> J[Pyrolytic Scission]
    I -- Broad multi-step DTG --> K[Thermo-Oxidative]
    I -- Residue at 900 C --> L[Charring / Aromatic Backbone]
    E --> M[FTIR confirms carbonyl 1715 cm-1]
    H --> N[Apply Kissinger / Coats Redfern]
    N --> O[Compute Ea and A]
    O --> P[Service Life via Arrhenius Extrapolation]
    P --> Q[Failure Analysis Report]
    M --> Q
    J --> Q
    K --> Q
    L --> Q
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Thermo-Oxidative Chain Mechanism (Bolland–Gee)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Init[Initiation: RH --> R. + H.] --> P1[Propagation 1: R. + O2 --> ROO.]
    P1 --> P2[Propagation 2: ROO. + RH --> ROOH + R.]
    P2 --> P1
    P2 --> Br[Branching: ROOH --> RO. + .OH]
    Br --> P1
    P1 --> T1[Termination: ROO. + ROO. --> ROOR + O2]
    P1 --> T2[Termination: R. + R. --> R-R]
    P1 --> T3[Termination: R. + ROO. --> ROOR]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — TGA + DTG Interpretation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start TGA Run] --> A[Record mass m vs T]
    A --> B[m decreases monotonically]
    B --> C[Compute dm/dT = DTG]
    C --> D{DTG Shape}
    D -- Single peak --> E1[One-step degradation e.g. PS]
    D -- Two peaks --> E2[Two-step e.g. PVC: HCl loss then backbone]
    D -- Shouldered --> E3[Complex multi-mechanism e.g. polyurethanes]
    D -- Gradual plateau --> E4[Char forming e.g. phenolic, PEEK]
    E1 --> F[Report Td onset and Td max]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Compare with literature for polymer family]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Polymer Degradation Pathway Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heated Polymer] --> B{Backbone Type}
    B -- Vinyl addition --> C{Substituent}
    C -- H --> C1[PE: random scission + crosslinking]
    C -- CH3 --> C2[PP: chain scission + tertiary radicals]
    C -- Cl --> C3[PVC: HCl elimination then polyene]
    C -- C6H5 --> C4[PS: unzip depolymerisation]
    B -- Condensation --> D{Linkage}
    D -- Ester --> D1[PET: beta scission yielding vinyl ester + COOH]
    D -- Amide --> D2[PA66: amide hydrolysis + cyclisation]
    D -- Urethane --> D3[PU: dissociation to polyol + isocyanate]
    C1 --> E[Failure Mode Identified]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 board answers, **always include a labelled schematic** for the degradation pathway of the specific polymer under question. This alone fetches **2–3 easy marks**.
