# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between thermal degradation and thermo-oxidative degradation of polymers.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Thermal (Pyrolytic) Degradation | Thermo-Oxidative Degradation |
|-----------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Atmosphere | Inert (N₂ / Ar / vacuum) | Air / O₂ |
| Temperature range | Higher (typically > 350 °C for C–C scission) | Lower (typically 150–250 °C) |
| Activation energy $E_a$ | High (200–300 kJ·mol⁻¹ for PE/PP) | Lower (80–120 kJ·mol⁻¹) |
| Mechanism | Random scission, unzipping, cross-linking | Radical chain with peroxyl intermediates |
| Diagnostic | DTG single peak, FTIR no carbonyl | FTIR peak at 1715 cm⁻¹ (C=O), broad DTG |
| Reversibility | Irreversible | Irreversible |

(3 marks — *tabular form with at least 4 distinct parameters*)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define the term "char yield" in polymer TGA analysis. Mention its significance with one example.**

**Model Answer:**
Char yield is defined as the **percentage residual mass of a polymer remaining after heating to a defined high temperature** (typically 700–900 °C) in an inert atmosphere, as measured by TGA.

$$ \text{Char Yield} = \dfrac{m_{900\,^{\circ}\text{C}}}{m_{30\,^{\circ}\text{C}}}\times 100\% $$

**Significance:** A high char yield (e.g., phenolic resins ≈ 50–60 %, PEEK ≈ 50 %) indicates superior flame retardancy, because the char acts as a physical barrier that limits heat and mass transfer to the underlying polymer. It is therefore a **semi-quantitative proxy for fire performance** and is correlated empirically with LOI by the **Van Krevelen relation**: $\text{LOI} \approx 17.5 + 0.4\cdot\text{CY}$.

(3 marks — definition 1, equation 1, example + significance 1)

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a suitable block diagram, explain the **hierarchical classification of polymer thermal failure** (physical aging, thermo-mechanical, thermo-oxidative, pyrolytic, and combustion). For each level, mention the diagnostic technique used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Kissinger equation** for the determination of activation energy from multiple-heating-rate TGA experiments, clearly stating the assumptions made.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The five-layer hierarchy of polymer thermal failure is:

1. **Physical Aging (Sub-$T_g$):** Slow densification by enthalpy relaxation below $T_g$. *Diagnostic:* DSC sub-$T_g$ endotherm, density gradient column.
2. **Thermo-mechanical Failure (above $T_g$):** Loss of modulus and dimensional stability. *Diagnostic:* Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA) showing storage modulus drop, HDT test per ASTM D648.
3. **Thermo-oxidative Failure (150–250 °C, O₂ present):** Auto-accelerating radical chain reaction with oxygen (Bolland–Gee). *Diagnostic:* FTIR — carbonyl peak at 1715 cm⁻¹, hydroperoxide peak at 3450 cm⁻¹; Oxidation Induction Time (OIT) by DSC.
4. **Pyrolytic Failure (> 350 °C, inert atmosphere):** Homolytic C–C bond cleavage. *Diagnostic:* TGA under N₂ showing sharp DTG peak; GC-MS of evolved gases.
5. **Combustion (> 500 °C, oxidative atmosphere, piloted):** Sustained flaming with release of heat, smoke, toxic gases. *Diagnostic:* Cone calorimeter (Heat Release Rate HRR), LOI per ASTM D2863.

A block diagram showing these five layers with corresponding diagnostic techniques (similar to Section 4.1) should be drawn (2 marks). Connecting arrows showing reversibility/irreversibility should be labelled (1 mark).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Refer to **Section 3.2** above. The derivation proceeds in five steps:

1. Start from the general rate law: $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}\,f(\alpha)$.
2. Change variable using $\beta = dT/dt$ to get $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dT} = \dfrac{A}{\beta}\,e^{-E_a/RT}\,f(\alpha)$.
3. Differentiate w.r.t. $t$ and apply the **peak condition** $\dfrac{d^{2}\alpha}{dt^{2}} = 0$.
4. Use the **first-order assumption** $f(\alpha) = (1-\alpha)$, so $f'(\alpha) = -1$.
5. Take $\ln$ of both sides after simplification, yielding the final Kissinger linear form:

$$ \boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{T_{max}^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{E_a}\right) - \dfrac{E_a}{RT_{max}}\;} $$

Plot $\ln(\beta/T_{max}^{2})$ vs $1/T_{max}$ for at least three heating rates; **slope $= -E_a/R$** gives $E_a$; **intercept $= \ln(AR/E_a)$** gives $A$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert heating rate units to **K·s⁻¹** (not K·min⁻¹) when $A$ is reported in s⁻¹. Failure to do so yields a wrong $A$ by a factor of 60 — losing 2 marks silently.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A polymer sample undergoes first-order thermal degradation. Using the **Arrhenius equation**, derive the expression for **time-to-failure** $t_f$ in terms of service temperature $T_s$, activation energy $E_a$, pre-exponential factor $A$, and fractional conversion $\alpha_f$. Show how this can be used to predict the 50-year service life of a polymer from accelerated TGA data.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following TGA data were recorded at $\beta = 10$ K·min⁻¹ for a polymer under nitrogen. Compute the **activation energy** using the **Coats–Redfern method**, assuming first-order kinetics.

| $T$ (K) | 650 | 700 | 750 | 800 | 850 |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $\alpha$ | 0.06 | 0.18 | 0.42 | 0.72 | 0.92 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

See **Section 3.1** above. The derivation yields:

$$ t_f = \dfrac{-\ln(1-\alpha_f)}{A\,e^{-E_a/RT_s}} $$

To predict a 50-year service life:

1. Run TGA at three or more elevated temperatures $T_i$ (e.g., 200, 220, 240 °C) and record $t_{f,i}$.
2. Plot $\ln t_{f,i}$ vs $1/T_i$; the slope is $E_a/R$ and the intercept is $\ln[1/(A)] - \ln[-\ln(1-\alpha_f)]$.
3. Extrapolate the linear plot to the **service temperature** $T_s$ (e.g., 60 °C pipeline operation) and read off $t_f$.
4. Verify $t_f \geq 50 \text{ years} = 1.577 \times 10^9$ s.

This is the basis of **ASTM E1877** and **ISO 2578** standards for polymer endurance testing.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Compute $-\ln(1-\alpha)$ and $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)]$:

| $T$ (K) | $\alpha$ | $-\ln(1-\alpha)$ | $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)]$ | $1/T$ (×10⁻³ K⁻¹) |
|---------|----------|------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 650 | 0.06 | 0.0619 | −2.781 | 1.5385 |
| 700 | 0.18 | 0.1985 | −1.617 | 1.4286 |
| 750 | 0.42 | 0.5447 | −0.607 | 1.3333 |
| 800 | 0.72 | 1.2730 | 0.2413 | 1.2500 |
| 850 | 0.92 | 2.5257 | 0.9264 | 1.1765 |

Linear regression of $y = \ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)]$ vs $x = 1/T$:

$\bar{x} = 1.3454 \times 10^{-3}$, $\bar{y} = -0.7676$

| $x_i - \bar{x}$ (10⁻³) | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | product (10⁻³) | $(x_i-\bar{x})^{2}$ (10⁻⁶) |
|-----------------------|-----------------|----------------|----------------------------|
| 0.1931 | −2.0134 | −0.3888 | 37.29 |
| 0.0832 | −0.8494 | −0.0707 | 6.92 |
| −0.0121 | 0.1606 | −0.00194 | 0.146 |
| −0.0954 | 1.0089 | −0.0962 | 9.10 |
| −0.1689 | 1.6940 | −0.2861 | 28.53 |

Sum of products $= -0.8438 \times 10^{-3}$  
Sum of $(x_i - \bar{x})^{2} = 81.99 \times 10^{-6}$

$$ \text{slope} = \dfrac{-0.8438\times 10^{-3}}{81.99\times 10^{-6}} = -10.29 \text{ K} \times 1000 = -10290 \text{ K} $$

Therefore:

$$ -E_a/R = -10290 \implies E_a = 10290 \times 8.314 = 85{,}551 \text{ J·mol}^{-1} \approx \mathbf{85.6 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}} $$

This value is consistent with a **medium-temperature degradation** regime — typical of a **polyurethane soft-segment decomposition** or a **polyolefin with antioxidant additives**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When $A$ is computed from Coats–Redfern, students often forget the $\beta$ term and the units. Recall: $A = \dfrac{\beta\,E_a}{R}\,e^{\text{intercept}}$ with $\beta$ in K·s⁻¹. Marks lost: 2 out of 7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Thermal failure** = irreversible (chemical) OR reversible (physical) loss of function due to temperature.
- ✅ Always distinguish **pyrolytic** (inert) from **thermo-oxidative** (air) degradation.
- ✅ **$T_g$, $T_m$, HDT, $T_d^{onset}$** are the four cardinal thermal temperatures — define all four in any 14-mark answer.
- ✅ **Arrhenius equation** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ is the cornerstone for **lifetime prediction**.
- ✅ **Kissinger** method uses multiple $\beta$; slope = $-E_a/R$.
- ✅ **Coats–Redfern** uses a single $\beta$ curve; plot $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)]$ vs $1/T$.
- ✅ Char yield at 900 °C is correlated to LOI: $\text{LOI} \approx 17.5 + 0.4\cdot\text{CY}$ (Van Krevelen).
- ✅ **$R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** — always quote.
- ✅ Convert heating rates to **K·s⁻¹** before using in formulas expecting s⁻¹.
- ✅ Diagnostic triad: **TGA** (mass loss) + **DSC** (transitions) + **FTIR** (chemistry).
- ✅ **Bolland–Gee mechanism** is the standard answer for thermo-oxidative chain reactions.
- ✅ Common exam pitfall: confusing *fractional mass loss* $\alpha$ with *fractional mass remaining* $m/m_0$ — they sum to 1.
- ✅ A **schematic / block diagram** in any thermal-failure answer earns 2–3 easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photo and Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT867 - FAILURE ANALYSIS OF POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Photo and Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photo-oxidation of Polymers** is defined as the simultaneous, synergistic degradation process where photochemical (UV/visible light initiated) reactions and oxidative (oxygen-driven free radical) reactions occur concurrently on a polymer substrate, leading to irreversible changes in chemical structure, molecular weight distribution, morphology, and macroscopic mechanical/optical properties. The process is formally classified under the **Bolt-Bolland-Gee free-radical chain mechanism** and is the dominant failure mode for polymers exposed to outdoor service environments.

**Oxidation of Polymers** more broadly refers to the autoxidation reaction at the polymer–oxygen interface, encompassing **thermo-oxidation** (heat-driven, no light required), **photo-oxidation** (light-driven, ambient temperature), and **chemiluminescence-driven oxidation** during the service life or processing stages of a polymer.

### 1.2 Syllabus Highlights

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 3 Coverage:**
> - Photodegradation mechanism (Norrish I, Norrish II, zipper reactions)
> - UV–Visible energy thresholds vs. polymer bond dissociation energies
> - Bolland–Gee kinetic scheme (Initiation → Propagation → Branching → Termination)
> - Hydroperoxide (POOH) formation and decomposition
> - Carbonyl Index measurement by FTIR
> - Stabilization strategies: UV absorbers, quenchers, HALS, antioxidants
> - Weathering (artificial vs. natural) and service-life prediction

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rust of Plastics"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Think of photo-oxidation as the **"biological aging" of plastics** — analogous to how human skin wrinkles and develops spots under chronic sun exposure. The sun's invisible ultraviolet rays act as tiny molecular "surgical knives" that sever long polymer chains (chain scission), while atmospheric oxygen acts like a "stitch" that creates new crosslinks in random places, making the once-flexible plastic brittle, yellowed, and cracked. Just as sunscreen (a stabilizer) protects skin, additives like HALS and UV absorbers protect polymers from this inevitable damage.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^{8} \, m/s}$ | Used in wavelength–energy conversion |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, J \cdot s}$ | Quantum energy of a photon |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | $\mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23} \, mol^{-1}}$ | Convert Einstein to mol |
| Wavelength threshold for C–C cleavage | $\lambda_{max}$ | $\mathbf{\le 290 \, nm}$ | Polyethylene type bonds |
| Threshold for C–H cleavage | $\lambda_{max}$ | $\mathbf{\le 350 \, nm}$ | Aliphatic C–H bond |
| Threshold for C=O cleavage (Norrish I) | $\lambda_{max}$ | $\mathbf{\le 400 \, nm}$ | Carbonyl n→π* |
| Carbonyl Index (CI) | $CI$ | Dimensionless ratio | FTIR diagnostic tool |
| Oxidation Induction Time | OIT | **minutes** | DSC/OIT technique |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy–Wavelength Relationship:** The photon energy required to break a chemical bond is inversely proportional to wavelength. **Shorter wavelength (UV) = Higher energy = More damaging.**

$$E = h \nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

where $E$ is photon energy in joules, $h$ is Planck's constant, $\nu$ is frequency (Hz), $c$ is speed of light, and $\lambda$ is wavelength in meters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Photophysical and Photochemical Primaries

A polymer absorbs a photon only when the photon energy matches the energy gap between its electronic ground state ($S_0$) and an excited state ($S_1, S_2, \ldots$). Once excited, the chromophore (a C=O, C=C, aromatic ring, or trace impurity) undergoes one of several deactivation pathways:

- **Radiative deactivation (fluorescence/phosphorescence)** — emits light, no chemical change
- **Non-radiative deactivation (heat release)** — no chemical change
- **Energy transfer to a quencher** — stabilised, no change
- **Photochemical reaction (bond cleavage)** — **causes degradation**

> [!NOTE]
> **Kasha's Rule:** Regardless of which higher excited state is reached, the molecule rapidly relaxes (via internal conversion) to the *lowest* excited state of that multiplicity before undergoing a photochemical process. Hence, degradation pathways depend on the **lowest excited state** of the chromophore.

### 2.2 The Photo-oxidation Pathway — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Initiation (Photolytic generation of the first radical):**

A carbonyl chromophore (formed during processing or service) absorbs a UV photon and cleaves via **Norrish Type I** reaction:

$$P-C(=O)-CH_2-P' \xrightarrow{h\nu} P-C(=O)^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}CH_2-P'$$

Two radicals are formed. The acyl radical (P–CO•) rapidly decarboxylates releasing **CO**.

**Step 2 — Propagation (Oxygen attack and chain transfer):**

The alkyl radical (P•) reacts almost instantaneously with dissolved or atmospheric O₂ (rate constant $\sim 10^{9} \, L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}$, diffusion-controlled):

$$P^{\bullet} + O_2 \longrightarrow POO^{\bullet}$$

The resulting peroxy radical abstracts a hydrogen from a neighbouring chain:

$$POO^{\bullet} + PH \longrightarrow POOH + P^{\bullet}$$

A new alkyl radical is regenerated — **the chain reaction has begun**.

**Step 3 — Branching (Hydroperoxide decomposition, the "kinetic engine" of oxidation):**

The hydroperoxide (POOH) is thermally and photolytically unstable:

$$POOH \xrightarrow{\Delta \, or \, h\nu} PO^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}OH$$

Two new radicals → two new chains → **autocatalytic acceleration**. This is why oxidation exhibits an **induction period** followed by rapid deterioration.

**Step 4 — Termination (Radical–radical annihilation):**

- Combination: $P^{\bullet} + P^{\bullet} \rightarrow P-P$
- Disproportionation: $P^{\bullet} + P^{\bullet} \rightarrow P{=}P + PH$
- Peroxy–peroxy: $POO^{\bullet} + POO^{\bullet} \rightarrow POOP + O_2$
- Alkyl–peroxy: $P^{\bullet} + POO^{\bullet} \rightarrow POOP$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Closed-Loop Autoxidation:** Termination does not *stop* oxidation — it merely *slows* it. As long as POOH is present, new radicals are generated. The **Oxidation Induction Time (OIT)** ends when the radical flux from POOH decomposition exceeds the stabilization capacity of the antioxidant system.

### 2.3 Effects of Photo-oxidation on Polymer Properties

- **Molecular weight:** Decreases via chain scission (dominant in PP, PE) or increases via crosslinking (dominant in elastomers, PVC)
- **Mechanical properties:** Tensile strength, elongation-at-break, and impact strength all decrease — embrittlement
- **Optical properties:** Yellowing, hazing, loss of gloss, discoloration
- **Surface:** Craze, microcracks, chalking (in filled systems)
- **Chemical structure:** Appearance of carbonyl (C=O, ~1715 cm⁻¹), hydroxyl (–OH, ~3400 cm⁻¹), and vinyl (C=C, ~1640 cm⁻¹) groups in FTIR spectra

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are KTU high-yield — appear in derivations, numericals, and short-answer questions.**

| # | Equation | Application | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | $h, c, \lambda$ | J/photon |
| 2 | $E \, (kJ/mol) = \dfrac{1.196 \times 10^{5}}{\lambda \, (nm)}$ | Per-mole energy | $\lambda$ | kJ/mol |
| 3 | $\lambda_{max}(nm) = \dfrac{1.196 \times 10^{5}}{BDE \, (kJ/mol)}$ | Threshold wavelength for bond cleavage | BDE | nm |
| 4 | $CI = \dfrac{I_{1715}}{I_{1375}}$ | Carbonyl Index (FTIR, internal reference 1375 cm⁻¹) | Absorbance | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{OIT} = t_{\text{onset}} - t_{0}$ | Oxidation Induction Time | time | minutes |
| 6 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -kt$ | First-order carbonyl accumulation | rate constant $k$ | min⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\tau_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Half-life of carbonyl growth | $k$ | min |
| 8 | $r_{ox} = k_2[POO^{\bullet}][PH]$ | Rate of oxidation (steady state) | concentrations | mol/L·s |
| 9 | $A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$ | Beer–Lambert law for FTIR absorbance | molar absorptivity | L/(mol·cm) |
| 10 | $\text{Arrhenius: } k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Service-life acceleration by temperature | $A, E_a$ | various |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical: Pipe Symbol Rule in KTU Tables** — For absolute value expressions in any KTU submission, use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` inside the table to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Carbon-fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP) components must pass 25-year UV exposure tests
- **Solar PV modules**: Ethylene-vinyl acetate (EVA) encapsulant must survive 25 years of UV + thermal cycling
- **Automotive**: Headlamp lenses (polycarbonate) require UV-stabilized top-coats
- **Medical implants**: Polyethylene liners for hip joints — oxidative degradation is a known failure mode
- **Geotextiles**: HDPE liners in landfills — environmental stress cracking accelerated by UV

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Wavelength for a Chemical Bond

**Problem Statement:** A polymer has a C–H bond with a Bond Dissociation Energy (BDE) of **410 kJ/mol**. Determine the maximum wavelength of light that can cleave this bond.

**Step 1 — Convert BDE to photon energy per mole:**

$$E_{\text{molar}} = 410 \, kJ/mol = 410 \times 10^{3} \, J/mol$$

**Step 2 — Convert molar photon energy to energy per photon:**

$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{E_{\text{molar}}}{N_A} = \frac{410 \times 10^{3}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}$$

$$E_{\text{photon}} = 6.808 \times 10^{-19} \, J \text{ per photon}$$

**Step 3 — Use $E = hc/\lambda$ and rearrange for $\lambda$:**

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_{\text{photon}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute known values ($h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, J \cdot s$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \, m/s$):**

$$\lambda = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{6.808 \times 10^{-19}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 19.878 \times 10^{-26} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \, J \cdot m$$

**Step 6 — Divide by the denominator:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{6.808 \times 10^{-19}} \, m$$

$$\lambda = 0.2919 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 291.9 \, nm$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result:**

> Since the sunlight reaching Earth's surface contains UV-B and UV-A components (≈ 280–400 nm), a wavelength of 291.9 nm is achievable in solar radiation. **Therefore, the C–H bond IS susceptible to direct photolytic cleavage** in outdoor exposure.

**Step 8 — Useful shortcut formula (KTU-friendly):**

$$\lambda_{\max}(nm) = \frac{1.196 \times 10^{5}}{BDE(kJ/mol)}$$

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{1.196 \times 10^{5}}{410} = 291.7 \, nm \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Carbonyl Index from FTIR Spectroscopy

**Given:** A polyethylene sample aged for 60 days in a Xenon arc weatherometer. FTIR shows absorbance at 1715 cm⁻¹ (carbonyl) of **0.184** and at 1375 cm⁻¹ (CH₃ bending reference) of **0.095**.

**Step 1 — Write the formula:**

$$CI = \frac{I_{1715}}{I_{1375}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$CI = \frac{0.184}{0.095}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$CI = 1.937$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation per KTU grading rubric:**

> A CI > 1.5 indicates **significant oxidative degradation** — the polymer has likely lost > 40% of its elongation-at-break and is approaching end-of-service life.

**Step 5 — Rate constant assuming first-order growth:**

$$CI_t = CI_0 \cdot e^{kt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{1}{t} \ln\left(\frac{CI_t}{CI_0}\right)$$

Assuming $CI_0 = 0.02$ (pristine PE) and $t = 60$ days:

$$k = \frac{1}{60} \ln\left(\frac{1.937}{0.02}\right) = \frac{1}{60} \ln(96.85)$$

$$k = \frac{4.573}{60} = 0.0762 \, \text{day}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Time to reach end-of-service CI = 2.5:**

$$t_{2.5} = \frac{1}{0.0762} \ln\left(\frac{2.5}{0.02}\right) = \frac{1}{0.0762} \times 4.828 = 63.4 \, \text{days}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** The polymer reaches end-of-service in ≈ 63 days under accelerated aging, corresponding to approximately **3–5 years** of outdoor exposure (acceleration factor ≈ 25–30x typical for Xenon arc).

### 3.3 Derivation: Oxidation Induction Time (OIT) from DSC

**Definition:** OIT is the time elapsed between reaching isothermal test temperature and the onset of oxidation exotherm in a DSC at a given temperature under oxygen atmosphere.

**Analytical Model (ASTM E1858, KTU reference standard):**

For an Arrhenius-controlled induction process:

$$\text{OIT}(T) = \frac{E_a}{2.303 \, R \cdot T} + \text{const}$$

**Step 1 — Two OIT measurements at different T:**

- $T_1 = 423 \, K$, $OIT_1 = 87 \, min$
- $T_2 = 453 \, K$, $OIT_2 = 24 \, min$

**Step 2 — Compute $\log(\text{OIT})$ at each temperature:**

$$\log(87) = 1.940$$
$$\log(24) = 1.380$$

**Step 3 — Compute $1/T$ values:**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{423} = 2.364 \times 10^{-3} \, K^{-1}$$
$$\frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{453} = 2.208 \times 10^{-3} \, K^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Arrhenius form:**

$$\log(\text{OIT}) = A' + \frac{B'}{T}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $B'$ (slope):**

$$B' = \frac{\log(OIT_1) - \log(OIT_2)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)} = \frac{1.940 - 1.380}{(2.364 - 2.208) \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$B' = \frac{0.560}{0.156 \times 10^{-3}} = 3590 \, K$$

**Step 6 — Compute $E_a$:**

$$E_a = 2.303 \cdot R \cdot B' = 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 3590$$

$$E_a = 68{,}738 \, J/mol \approx 68.7 \, kJ/mol$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical **un-stabilized polypropylene** has $E_a \approx 60$–$80 \, kJ/mol$ — consistent with our calculated value. A **HALS-stabilized PP** typically shows $E_a \approx 100$–$120 \, kJ/mol$.

### 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the *single most important* KTU reference summary for Module 3 — memorize for the exam.**

| Degradation Feature | Diagnostic Signature | Affected Polymers | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chain scission (random) | $\bar{M}_n \downarrow$, $\bar{M}_w$ slight $\downarrow$ | PP, PE, PS | HALS, phenolic AO |
| Crosslinking | Gel fraction $\uparrow$, insoluble | EPDM, PVC, XLPE | Synergist + co-AO |
| Discoloration (yellowing) | UV–Vis 400–450 nm absorbance | PS, PVC, PC, PMMA | Benzotriazoles |
| Surface cracking (crazing) | Optical/SEM microscopy | PP, ABS, ASA | Top-coat, carbon black |
| Carbonyl build-up | FTIR 1715 cm⁻¹, CI > 1.5 | PE, PP, PB | Phenolic AO, phosphite |
| Hydroperoxide | FTIR 3400 cm⁻¹ (broad), iodometric titration | All polyolefins | Phosphite (secondary AO) |
| Embrittlement | Charpy impact $\downarrow > 50\%$ | HDPE pipes, PP | Stabilizer package |
| Chalking | TiO₂ release at surface | TiO₂-pigmented systems | Surface-treated TiO₂ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Photo-oxidation Closed-Loop Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Polymer P-H] -->|UV photon h nu| startB[Excited state P-H*]
    startB -->|Norrish I cleavage| radA[Alkyl radical P*]
    radA -->|O2 attack diffusion-limited k ~ 10^9| peroxy[Peroxy radical POO*]
    peroxy -->|H-abstraction| hydro[Hydroperoxide POOH]
    hydro -->|photolysis or thermolysis| branch1[Alkoxy radical PO*]
    hydro -->|photolysis or thermolysis| branch2[Hydroxyl radical OH*]
    branch1 -->|Beta-scission| radA
    branch2 -->|H-abstraction| startA
    peroxy -->|combination termination| termA[Stable product POOP]
    radA -->|combination termination| termB[Crosslink P-P]
    hydro -->|stabilized by phosphite| stabA[Non-radical product]
    peroxy -->|Denisov cycle with HALS| stabB[Inactive nitroxyl]
    startA -->|UV absorber competes for photon| stabC[Excitation energy lost as heat]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stabilizer Action Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sunlight [Sunlight Source]
        uvA[UV-A 315-400 nm]
        uvB[UV-B 280-315 nm]
        uvC[UV-C 100-280 nm]
    end

    subgraph polymer1 [Unstabilized Polymer]
        chrom[Chromophore C=O]
        chain[Polymer backbone PH]
    end

    subgraph polymer2 [Stabilized Polymer with HALS]
        chrom2[Chromophore C=O]
        chain2[Polymer backbone PH]
        uva[UV Absorber benzotriazole]
        hals[HALS Nitroxyl]
        phos[Phosphite secondary AO]
    end

    sunlight --> uvA
    sunlight --> uvB
    uvB -->|absorbed| chrom
    chrom -->|Norrish I| chain

    uvA --> uva
    uva -->|converts to heat| hals
    hals -->|Denisov cycle| chain2
    chain2 -->|H-donation| phos
    phos -->|POOH neutralization| hals
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Diagram: Closed-Loop Autoxidation with Inhibition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init[Initiation: P-H + h nu -> P* + H*] --> prop1[Propagation 1: P* + O2 -> POO*]
    prop1 --> prop2[Propagation 2: POO* + P-H -> POOH + P*]
    prop2 --> brnch[Branching: POOH -> PO* + *OH]
    brnch --> prop1
    brnch --> prop2
    prop1 --> term[Termination: radical combination]
    prop2 --> term
    term --> end1[Stable products]
    
    init -.->|competitively absorbed| inhiA[UV Absorber UA]
    prop2 -.->|donates H to POO*| inhiB[Primary Antioxidant ArOH]
    brnch -.->|reduces POOH non-radically| inhiC[Phosphite PIII]
    prop1 -.->|radical scavenging| inhiD[HALS nitroxyl >NO*]
    inhiD -.->|regenerated via Denisov| prop2
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Failure Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    obs[Observed failure: yellowing embrittlement cracking] --> age[Step 1: Determine exposure history]
    age -->|outdoor service| agechk[Yes, sunlight exposure]
    agechk --> ftir[Step 2: FTIR analysis]
    ftir -->|1715 cm-1 peak high| carb[Step 3: Carbonyl Index calculation]
    ftir -->|3400 cm-1 broad peak| hyd[Step 4: Hydroperoxide confirmation iodometric]
    carb --> ci[Carbonyl Index > 1.5 = significant oxidation]
    ci --> oit[Step 5: DSC OIT measurement]
    oit -->|OIT less than 5 min| end1[AO package depleted]
    oit -->|OIT greater than 20 min| end2[AO package intact]
    hyd -->|conc > 10 mmol per kg| end3[Active oxidation ongoing]
    end1 --> reco1[Recommendation: reformulate with HALS + UV absorber]
    end2 --> reco2[Recommendation: investigate other failure modes mechanical thermal]
    end3 --> reco3[Recommendation: replace immediately extend life via topcoat]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**

> **Differentiate between Norrish Type I and Norrish Type II reactions in the photodegradation of carbonyl-containing polymers. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Norrish Type I (α-cleavage):** The excited carbonyl chromophore cleaves the **adjacent C–C bond** (α to the carbonyl), producing two radicals: an acyl radical and an alkyl radical. The acyl radical subsequently loses CO. The reaction is dominant at **short UV wavelengths (< 300 nm)** and is the *primary* initiation step in photo-oxidation of **polyethylene** (containing trace C=O) and **polypropylene** (tertiary C–H readily oxidized to C=O during processing).

- **Norrish Type II (β-cleavage via 6-membered cyclic transition state):** The excited carbonyl abstracts a hydrogen atom from the **γ-carbon (3 atoms away)** through a six-membered cyclic intermediate, producing an **enol** that tautomerises to a different ketone + an **alkene (vinyl group)**. The reaction does **not produce free radicals** directly and is dominant at **longer UV wavelengths (300–400 nm)**. Characteristic in **poly(methyl methacrylate)** and **poly(ethylene-co-carbon monoxide)**.

- **Key Diagnostic:** FTIR detection of a **vinyl (C=C) peak near 1640 cm⁻¹** is the hallmark of Norrish II chemistry. Absence of vinyl with strong C=O loss indicates Norrish I is dominant.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023, Supplementary]**

> **Explain the role of Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers (HALS) in polymer stabilization. Why is the 'Denisov Cycle' important?**

**Model Answer:**

HALS do **not** absorb UV light — they act via a regenerative radical-scavenging cycle. The active species is the **nitroxyl radical (>N–O•)** which traps alkyl radicals (P•) to form **aminoether (NOR)**:

$$P^{\bullet} + >N-O^{\bullet} \rightarrow >N-OR$$

The aminoether is then **regenerated** to nitroxyl by reaction with a peroxy radical:

$$>N-OR + POO^{\bullet} \rightarrow >N-O^{\bullet} + POOR$$

This **Denisov cycle** makes HALS a *catalytic* stabilizer (one molecule can neutralize > 50 radicals). This is fundamentally different from phenolic antioxidants (consumed in 1:1 stoichiometry). HALS are particularly effective in **polyolefins (PP, PE)** and are typically used at 0.1–0.5 wt% loadings.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer ONE of the two choices, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Suggested Question]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Bolland–Gee kinetic scheme** for the autoxidation of polymers. Identify the rate-determining step and explain why oxidation exhibits an induction period.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks)** A polyethylene sample is subjected to accelerated UV aging. After 200 hours, the carbonyl index (CI) is 0.85. After 400 hours, CI is 1.70. Determine: (i) the order of reaction, (ii) the rate constant, and (iii) the time required to reach CI = 3.0 (the failure threshold for outdoor service).

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The Bolland–Gee scheme (1946) formalizes the autoxidation cycle into four elementary steps:

**Step 1 — Initiation:**
$$\text{In} \xrightarrow{k_i} 2R^{\bullet}$$
For a polymer (no external initiator), the radical source is photolysis of C=O or POOH.

**Step 2 — Propagation:**
$$R^{\bullet} + O_2 \xrightarrow{k_2} ROO^{\bullet} \quad (\text{very fast})$$
$$ROO^{\bullet} + RH \xrightarrow{k_p} ROOH + R^{\bullet} \quad (\text{rate-determining})$$

**Step 3 — Branching:**
$$ROOH \xrightarrow{k_b} RO^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}OH$$

**Step 4 — Termination:**
$$2ROO^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_t} \text{non-radical products}$$

> The **rate-determining step** is the **H-abstraction** by POO•, because it requires a specific C–H bond (tertiary C–H in PP is most susceptible, $k_p \approx 10 \, L \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}$, vs. $k_p \approx 1$ for primary C–H in PE).
>
> **Induction period explanation:** During the initial stage, the rate of radical generation is low and matches the rate of termination by residual stabilizer. The antioxidant is progressively consumed; once depleted, [POOH] rises autocatalytically — the OIT ends. The slope of [POOH] vs. time is initially near zero (flat) and then rises steeply.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Order of reaction:**

- Test 1st order: $CI_2/CI_1 = 1.70/0.85 = 2.0$, and time doubled (200→400 hr). For 1st order: $CI = CI_0 \cdot e^{kt}$, so $CI_2/CI_1 = e^{k\Delta t}$. Here $\Delta t = 200 \, hr$, and ratio = 2 → $e^{k \cdot 200} = 2$.
- Test 0th order: $CI = CI_0 + kt$. If 0th order, CI should increase linearly: $0.85 + k(200) = 1.70$ → $k = 0.00425/hr$. Check: at 400 hr, $0.85 + 0.00425 \times 400 = 0.85 + 1.70 = 2.55 \neq 1.70$. **Not 0th order.**
- **Test 1st order:** $e^{k \cdot 200} = 2$ → $k \cdot 200 = \ln 2 = 0.693$ → $k = 3.467 \times 10^{-3} \, hr^{-1}$. Verify at 400 hr: $0.85 \cdot e^{3.467 \times 10^{-3} \times 400} = 0.85 \cdot e^{1.386} = 0.85 \cdot 4.0 = 3.40 \neq 1.70$. **Not 1st order either!**
- **Test 2nd order:** $1/CI = 1/CI_0 + kt$. At 200 hr: $1/0.85 = 1.176$. At 400 hr: $1/1.70 = 0.588$. Difference = $0.588/k$ for $\Delta t = 200$. So $k = (0.588 - 1.176)/200 \cdot$ … wait, $1/CI_2 = 1/CI_1 - k \cdot t$ doesn't work either.
- **Correct approach:** The data fits $CI$ doubling every 200 hr — this is **exponential** growth characteristic of **autocatalytic** oxidation. Best fit: $CI(t) = CI_0 \cdot e^{kt}$ where data shows factor of 2 per 200 hr. Therefore $k = \ln 2 / 200 = 3.47 \times 10^{-3} \, hr^{-1}$ (apparent 1st-order in the autocatalytic regime).

**Reconciliation (revised approach):** Using $CI_0 = 0$ is not valid here. Treat as $CI(t) = A(e^{kt} - 1)$ for autocatalytic. From two points:

$$0.85 = A(e^{200k} - 1)$$
$$1.70 = A(e^{400k} - 1)$$

Dividing: $2 = (e^{400k} - 1)/(e^{200k} - 1)$. Let $x = e^{200k}$: $2 = (x^2 - 1)/(x - 1) = x + 1$ → $x = 1$ → $k = 0$? Not consistent.

> **Correct standard interpretation:** For a linear-in-time plot of CI vs. t (zero-order), at $t=200, CI=0.85$ and at $t=400, CI=1.70$ — *these would be perfectly linear* with slope 0.00425/hr, intercept 0. **Therefore the reaction is 0th order** in the carbonyl build-up during this regime. The earlier "doubling" interpretation is a coincidence. **Conclusion: 0th order, $k = 4.25 \times 10^{-3} \, hr^{-1}$.**

**Time to reach CI = 3.0:**

$$t = \frac{CI - CI_0}{k} = \frac{3.0 - 0}{0.00425} = 706 \, \text{hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students confuse *rate of carbonyl growth* (0th order in this regime) with *rate of oxygen consumption* (which is 1st order). State your assumption **clearly**. Also, do not mix $CI_0$ (the *initial* CI of pristine material — usually taken as 0 for analytical simplicity) with $CI$ at any aging time.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Model Question]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the major **stabilizers** used in polymer formulations for photo-oxidative resistance. Discuss the working principle of each class with specific chemical examples.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks)** A polyolefin is used in an outdoor application. Justify the choice of a **multi-component stabilizer package** (HALS + UV absorber + phenolic antioxidant + phosphite) using the **synergistic** stabilization concept. Why is a single stabilizer generally inadequate?

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Class | Function | Chemical Example | Typical Loading |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UV Absorbers** | Competitive photon absorption, harmless deactivation | 2-(2-Hydroxyphenyl)benzotriazole (Tinuvin P) | 0.1–1.0 wt% |
| **Quenchers** | Deactivate excited chromophores via energy transfer | Ni(II) bis(phenolate) | 0.1–0.5 wt% |
| **HALS** | Radical scavenger via Denisov cycle | Bis(2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidinyl) sebacate (Tinuvin 770) | 0.1–0.5 wt% |
| **Primary Antioxidants** | H-donors to peroxy radicals | Irganox 1010 (hindered phenol) | 0.05–0.3 wt% |
| **Secondary Antioxidants** | POOH decomposers | Tris(nonylphenyl) phosphite | 0.05–0.3 wt% |

**Working principles:**

- **UV Absorbers** (benzophenones, benzotriazoles, triazines) absorb UV and dissipate the energy as heat via *intramolecular proton transfer* (ESIPT mechanism). They protect the **bulk** of the polymer (depth-dependent — effective only above a critical thickness).
- **Quenchers** de-activate the *excited state* of the chromophore before bond cleavage, by transferring the energy to themselves (which then dissipate as heat). Effective at the **surface**.
- **HALS** scavenge radicals and are regenerated (Denisov cycle) — catalytic, high efficiency.
- **Primary AOs** donate H to POO•, terminating one chain each — *stoichiometric* (1:1 consumption).
- **Secondary AOs** convert POOH to non-radical alcohols, preventing branching.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

A single stabilizer class cannot address all four failure pathways simultaneously:

1. **UV Absorber alone** — reduces photon flux but cannot stop radicals that have already been formed at the surface or in pigmented systems (where UV absorber efficiency drops).
2. **HALS alone** — excellent radical scavenger, but cannot prevent the *initial* photon absorption by chromophores; in pigmented systems, HALS effectiveness is reduced.
3. **Phenolic AO alone** — consumed rapidly during processing (high-temperature oxidation) and does not provide long-term UV protection.
4. **Phosphite alone** — only stabilizes POOH, cannot scavenge radicals.

**Synergistic mechanism of the 4-component package:**

- **Phenolic AO** protects the polymer during **high-temperature processing** (extrusion, injection molding) where the HALS nitroxyl would not be regenerated (Denisov cycle requires radicals).
- **Phosphite** decomposes POOH *formed during processing* into alcohols, preventing early-stage POOH build-up.
- **UV Absorber** reduces UV photon flux reaching the chromophore, especially effective in the bulk.
- **HALS** scavenges any radicals that escape the first two barriers during **long-term service**.

> **The result:** A polyolefin with this 4-component package can survive **> 10 years of outdoor exposure** (Florida or Arizona weathering) without significant embrittlement, while the unstabilized control fails in < 6 months.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Students often write "stabilizers prevent UV light from entering the polymer" — **this is wrong for HALS**. HALS do not absorb UV. Also, do not list HALS as "antioxidant" alone — they are a *light* stabilizer (radical scavenger with regenerative cycle). Failing to distinguish HALS from phenolic AO is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — must be at your fingertips for the KTU exam:**

- [ ] **Photo-oxidation** = light + O₂ + polymer → radicals → chain scission / crosslinking / discoloration
- [ ] The **Bolland–Gee scheme** has 4 steps: Initiation, Propagation, Branching, Termination
- [ ] **Rate-determining step** is H-abstraction by POO• — most selective to tertiary C–H (PP > PE in susceptibility)
- [ ] **Induction period** ends when AO is depleted and POOH starts autocatalytic decomposition
- [ ] **Norrish I** → radical products (C–C cleavage α to C=O)
- [ ] **Norrish II** → non-radical products (intramolecular H-abstraction, 6-membered TS, generates alkene)
- [ ] Photon energy $E = hc/\lambda$; per-mole: $E(kJ/mol) = 1.196 \times 10^{5}/\lambda(nm)$
- [ ] C–H bond (BDE 410 kJ/mol) cleaved at $\lambda \le 291 \, nm$ — present in solar UV
- [ ] **Carbonyl Index** $CI = I_{1715}/I_{1375}$ — diagnostic of carbonyl build-up
- [ ] **CI > 1.5** indicates significant degradation
- [ ] **OIT** measured by DSC; ASTM E1858 standard
- [ ] **HALS mechanism** = Denisov cycle (NOR + POO• → >N–O• + POOR)
- [ ] HALS are **catalytic** (regenerative) — not consumed
- [ ] **Phenolic AOs** are **stoichiometric** (1:1 with radicals)
- [ ] **Phosphites** = secondary AOs, decompose POOH
- [ ] **UV absorbers** (benzotriazoles) work by **ESIPT** mechanism
- [ ] **Quenchers** deactivate excited chromophores via energy transfer
- [ ] **Multi-component synergy** is mandatory for > 5-year outdoor service
- [ ] **Yellowing** is most pronounced in PS, PVC, PC, PMMA
- [ ] **Chain scission** dominates in PP, PE; **crosslinking** dominates in PVC, elastomers
- [ ] **Service life prediction** uses Arrhenius extrapolation: $\ln t = A + E_a/RT$
- [ ] **Weathering acceleration factor** for Xenon arc ≈ 25–30× Florida outdoor

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For numerical problems, always state the **assumption of reaction order** (0th, 1st, or autocatalytic) and verify it with at least 2 data points before solving. For descriptive answers, use **mechanistic arrows** (radical formation → propagation → branching → termination) and cite the **specific chemical class** of the stabilizer with a chemical example. This consistently scores 12+ / 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degradation Mechanisms - Ionizing Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT867 - FAILURE ANALYSIS OF POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Degradation Mechanisms - Ionizing Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ionizing Radiation Degradation** is the irreversible alteration of a polymer's chemical structure and physical-mechanical properties caused by exposure to high-energy electromagnetic or particulate radiation possessing energy sufficient to ionize atoms and molecules along its trajectory.

By the KTU 2024 definition, ionizing radiation is characterized by photon or particle energy exceeding the ionization threshold of matter, typically **> 10 eV**, with practical polymer-processing energies ranging from **10 keV to 10 MeV**.

The principal categories are:

| Radiation Type | Nature | Typical Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Gamma (γ) rays | Electromagnetic | 0.1 – 10 MeV |
| X-rays | Electromagnetic | 1 keV – 1 MeV |
| Electron beam (β) | Particulate (e⁻) | 0.1 – 10 MeV |
| Alpha (α) particles | Particulate (He²⁺) | 2 – 10 MeV |
| Neutron (n) | Particulate (neutral) | 0.025 eV – 20 MeV |
| Proton (p⁺) | Particulate (H⁺) | 1 – 200 MeV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The radiation dose absorbed by the polymer is measured in **Gray (Gy)**, where 1 Gy = 1 J/kg = 100 rad. Industrial sterilization doses are typically 25 – 50 kGy.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Picture)

Imagine you are throwing bowling balls (radiation) at a delicate LEGO castle (polymer chains). Each bowling ball strike can:
- **Break apart individual LEGO bricks** → this is *chain scission* (molecular-weight reduction)
- **Knit two separate castles together** → this is *crosslinking* (molecular-weight increase, gel formation)
- **Shatter pieces into dust that flies away as gas (H₂, CH₄, CO₂)** → this is *outgassing* (mass loss)

The "bowling ball" is ionizing radiation — invisible, weightless, yet carrying enormous energy in each packet. The castle's reaction depends on **what kind of plastic** it is made from. Polyethylene and rubber *want* to crosslink when hit. PTFE and PMMA *want* to shatter into smaller chains. Polystyrene is a tough customer — it crosslinks but resists scission.

The presence of **oxygen** is like throwing the bowling balls during a rainstorm (O₂) — instead of clean breakage, you get messy oxidation products (carbonyls, hydroperoxides), accelerating failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Photodegradation* (UV light) only excites electrons in the outer shell. *Ionizing radiation* ejects electrons entirely, breaking primary covalent bonds and creating radical cations, free radicals, and secondary electrons with energies of 10 – 100 eV. This is why γ-irradiation damages a polymer's *bulk*, not just its *surface*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Speed of light:** c = **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Planck's constant:** h = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Ionization energy of C–C bond:** ≈ **3.6 eV** (well below γ-ray energies)
- **Ionization energy of C–H bond:** ≈ **11.3 eV**
- **G-value (radiation-chemical yield):** events per **100 eV** absorbed, or per Joule (SI: μmol/J)
- **Conversion:** 1 Gy = **6.24 × 10¹⁸ eV/kg** = **6.24 × 10¹⁵ eV/g**

## 1.4 KTU-Standard Categorization of Polymer Response

A foundational classification students must memorize:

> **Crosslinking-dominant polymers (G(X) > G(S)):**
> Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), Polystyrene (PS), Polyvinyl chloride (PVC), Nylon-6,6, Natural rubber, Silicone rubber, Polyacrylamide.

> **Chain-scission-dominant polymers (G(S) > G(X)):**
> Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), Polyisobutylene (PIB), Cellulose, Polypropylene oxide, Poly-α-methylstyrene.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** "All polymers degrade under radiation." This is FALSE. Some polymers (notably PE and PS) become *stronger and tougher* with moderate radiation doses because crosslinking dominates. The key is the **G(S)/G(X) ratio**, not the absolute dose.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism — Three-Stage Cascade

When an ionizing photon or particle enters a polymer, damage proceeds in **three distinct time-stages**:

### Stage 1: Physical Stage (10⁻¹⁸ – 10⁻¹⁵ s)
- High-energy photon/particle collides with orbital electrons.
- **Three competing mechanisms** dictate energy transfer:

  **(a) Photoelectric Effect** — dominant at **E < 100 keV**
  $$\sigma_{pe} \propto \frac{Z^{4-5}}{E^{3}}$$
  Photon transfers *all* its energy to a bound electron; the atom becomes ionized, the photon disappears.

  **(b) Compton Scattering** — dominant at **100 keV < E < 10 MeV**
  $$\lambda' - \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos\theta)$$
  Photon scatters off a "free" (loosely bound) electron, transferring *part* of its energy. This is the **dominant mechanism for ⁶⁰Co γ-rays (1.17 and 1.33 MeV)**.

  **(c) Pair Production** — dominant at **E > 1.022 MeV**
  $$\gamma \rightarrow e^- + e^+$$
  Photon converts into an electron-positron pair in the Coulomb field of a nucleus. Threshold = **2 m_e c² = 1.022 MeV**.

### Stage 2: Physico-Chemical Stage (10⁻¹⁵ – 10⁻¹² s)
- Secondary electrons (10 – 100 eV) lose energy through repeated ionization and excitation, creating a **thermal "spike"**.
- Excited molecules relax, generating **free radicals** (R•, •R'•), **radical ions** (R•⁺), and **secondary electrons**.
- Vibrational/rotational relaxation converts excess energy to local heat (thermal spike radius ≈ 1 – 10 nm, temperature spike 10³ – 10⁴ K for < 1 ps).

### Stage 3: Chemical Stage (10⁻¹² s – hours)
- Free radicals undergo:
  - **Chain scission:**  
    $$\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} \xrightarrow{h\nu,\, e^-} \text{–CH}_2\text{–}\dot{\text{C}}\text{H}_2 + \dot{\text{C}}\text{H}_2\text{–}$$
  - **Crosslinking:**  
    $$2\,\text{–CH}_2\text{–}\dot{\text{C}}\text{H}\text{–} \longrightarrow \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH(–)–CH(–)–CH}_2\text{–}$$
  - **Hydrogen abstraction:**  
    $$\text{R}^\bullet + \text{–CH}_2\text{–R'} \longrightarrow \text{R–H} + \text{–}\dot{\text{C}}\text{H}\text{–R'}$$
  - **Double-bond formation** (in vinyl-type polymers)
  - **Gas evolution:** H₂, CH₄, CO, CO₂ (H₂ is the dominant evolved gas in most hydrocarbons)

## 2.2 G-Value — The Central Quantitative Descriptor

**G(X)** is the number of events of type X produced per 100 eV (or per Joule in SI) of energy absorbed.

$$G(X) = \frac{\text{Number of events of type } X}{E_{absorbed} \,[\text{in 100 eV or 1 J}]}$$

**SI conversion:**  
$$G_{SI}\left[\frac{\mu mol}{J}\right] = \frac{G_{old}}{9.648}$$

> **Why 100 eV?** This historical unit was chosen because it is roughly the energy of a single ionizing event (one ionization ≈ 30 eV, one radical formation ≈ 10 – 30 eV). A G-value of 3 means 3 events per 100 eV absorbed.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use / Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absorbed dose | $D = E_{abs} / m$ | Total energy per unit mass | Gy (J/kg) |
| Dose rate | $\dot{D} = dD/dt$ | Energy deposition rate | Gy/s, kGy/h |
| G-value (old) | $G = N_{events} / (E_{abs}/100\,eV)$ | Radiation chemical yield | events / 100 eV |
| G-value (SI) | $G_{SI} = G_{old}/9.648$ | SI form | μmol / J |
| Scission events | $N_s = G(S) \cdot M_n \cdot D / (9.648 \times 100)$ | Number of chain breaks per gram | dimensionless |
| Crosslink events | $N_x = G(X) \cdot M_n \cdot D / (9.648 \times 100)$ | Number of crosslinks per gram | dimensionless |
| Charlesby–Pinner | $s + \sqrt{s} = p_0/q_0 + 1/(q_0 \cdot u_1 \cdot D)$ | Sol-gel analysis | s = sol fraction |
| Charlesby–Pinner (general) | $s + \sqrt{s} = \dfrac{G(S)}{2 G(X)} + \dfrac{9.65 \times 10^6}{G(X) \cdot M_n \cdot D}$ | Random initial distribution | dimensionless |
| Mass loss | $\Delta m = m_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-G_{gas} \cdot D / 9.648})$ | Outgassing fraction | kg, fraction |
| Mean energy per ion pair (W) | $W \approx 25$ eV for PE | Energy to create one ion pair | eV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Numerical Problems:** Always carry units. Convert rad → Gy by dividing by 100, and convert eV/g → μmol/J by dividing by 9.648 × 10⁶. Failure to convert is the #1 cause of 14-mark calculation errors.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Ionizing-radiation engineering finds use in:

1. **Radiation Sterilization of Medical Devices:** 25 kGy dose destroys microbial DNA; KTU frequently asks why PE and PP are preferred for syringes/tubing (they crosslink slightly, gaining strength).
2. **Wire & Cable Crosslinking:** PE insulation is e-beam crosslinked in continuous lines; crosslinked PE (XLPE) rated to 90 °C vs. 70 °C for thermoplastic PE.
3. **Shrink-Film Production:** Radiation crosslinked PE/PP films have "memory" — they shrink back to their pre-irradiation shape on reheating.
4. **Radiation-Resistant Materials Selection:** For nuclear-plant and space applications, polymers with high **G(X) − G(S)** values and aromatic backbones (PS, polyimide, PEEK) are chosen.
5. **Dosimetry:** Polymer dosimeters (e.g., FWT-60, radiochromic films) exploit the linear color change with dose.

## 2.5 Factors Influencing Radiation Response

- **Polymer structure:** Aliphatic > aromatic in radiation resistance (aromatic rings dissipate absorbed energy as heat via π-electron delocalization).
- **Presence of oxygen:** O₂ converts alkyl radicals to peroxy radicals (ROO•), accelerating oxidative chain scission. **In vacuum or inert atmosphere, "cleaner" chemistry prevails.**
- **Dose rate:** Low dose rates allow radical recombination → less net damage per unit dose (oxygen-diffusion-controlled regime).
- **Temperature:** Higher T increases radical mobility, may favor either crosslink (radical recombination) or scission (β-scission of tertiary radicals).
- **Additives:** Anti-rad (antioxidants like hindered phenols) and crosslinking promoters (multifunctional monomers, e.g., triallyl isocyanurate) alter response dramatically.
- **Crystallinity:** Crystalline regions are more radiation-resistant than amorphous regions (e.g., HDPE > LDPE in radiation tolerance for the same dose).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Absorbed Dose to Number of Molecular Events

**Starting premise:** A polymer of mass $m$ absorbs a dose $D$ (in Gy = J/kg). We want to find the number of chain-scission events $N_s$ produced.

**Step 1 — Total energy absorbed (Joules):**
$$E_{abs} = D \times m$$

**Step 2 — Convert to eV (knowing 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J):**
$$E_{abs} \,[\text{eV}] = \frac{D \times m}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

**Step 3 — Number of "100 eV packets" (G-value denominator):**
$$N_{packets} = \frac{E_{abs} \,[\text{eV}]}{100}$$

**Step 4 — Number of scission events from G(S):**
$$N_s = G(S) \times N_{packets} = \frac{G(S) \cdot D \cdot m}{100 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify numerical constant:**
$$N_s = \frac{G(S) \cdot D \cdot m}{1.602 \times 10^{-17}} = 6.243 \times 10^{17} \cdot G(S) \cdot D \cdot m$$

**Step 6 — Per unit mass (number of events per gram, with D in Gy):**
$$n_s \left[\frac{\text{events}}{g}\right] = 6.243 \times 10^{17} \cdot G(S) \cdot D$$

**Step 7 — Express in moles per gram (dividing by Avogadro's number 6.022 × 10²³):**
$$n_s \left[\frac{mol}{g}\right] = 1.037 \times 10^{-6} \cdot G(S) \cdot D$$

This last form is often used in textbooks:  
$$\boxed{\,n_s \left[\mu mol/g\right] = 1.037 \times G(S) \cdot D\,[\text{kGy}]\,}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A 50 g sample of polyethylene is irradiated with a ⁶⁰Co source at a dose of 100 kGy. Given G(S) = 0.3 and G(X) = 2.0 (in units of events per 100 eV), calculate:
(a) Number of chain-scission events
(b) Number of crosslink events  
(c) Net effect on molecular weight

**Step (a) — Scission events:**

First convert D to Gy: $D = 100 \text{ kGy} = 1.00 \times 10^5 \text{ Gy}$.

$$N_s = 6.243 \times 10^{17} \times G(S) \times D \times m$$

$$N_s = 6.243 \times 10^{17} \times 0.3 \times (1.00 \times 10^5) \times 50$$

$$N_s = 6.243 \times 10^{17} \times 0.3 \times 5.0 \times 10^6$$

$$N_s = 6.243 \times 0.3 \times 5.0 \times 10^{23}$$

$$N_s = 9.365 \times 10^{23} \text{ events}$$

**Step (b) — Crosslink events:**

$$N_x = 6.243 \times 10^{17} \times 2.0 \times (1.00 \times 10^5) \times 50$$

$$N_x = 6.243 \times 2.0 \times 5.0 \times 10^{23}$$

$$N_x = 6.243 \times 10^{24} \text{ events}$$

**Step (c) — Net effect:**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{N_x}{N_s} = \frac{6.243 \times 10^{24}}{9.365 \times 10^{23}} = 6.67$$

Since crosslink events exceed scission events by a factor of 6.67, **the polymer will crosslink, form a gel, and increase in molecular weight** (until a limiting crosslink density is reached).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** If $G(X) > 4 \cdot G(S)$, crosslinking is strongly dominant. If $G(S) > 4 \cdot G(X)$, chain scission dominates. In between, both effects occur and the Charlesby–Pinner plot is required.

## 3.3 Derivation Outline: Charlesby–Pinner Equation

For a polymer with the *most-probable* (Schulz–Flory) initial molecular-weight distribution ($M_w / M_n = 2$), the sol fraction $s$ after a dose $D$ obeys:

$$s + \sqrt{s} = \frac{p_0}{q_0} + \frac{1}{q_0 u_1 D}$$

where:
- $p_0$ = scission density per unit dose
- $q_0$ = crosslink density per unit dose
- $u_1$ = initial number-average degree of polymerization

For an *arbitrary* initial distribution, the G-value form is:

$$s + \sqrt{s} = \frac{G(S)}{2 G(X)} + \frac{9.65 \times 10^6}{G(X) \cdot M_n \cdot D}$$

**Logical steps (for exam understanding):**

1. Radiation creates crosslinks at rate $q_0 D$ and scissions at rate $p_0 D$ per monomer unit.
2. A chain becomes part of the infinite gel network when its effective functionality exceeds 2.
3. Statistical mechanics (Flory–Stockmayer theory) gives the gel-point condition.
4. The sol fraction is the fraction of chains *not* incorporated into the gel.
5. Substituting the radiation-induced crosslink and scission densities into the gel fraction yields the equation above.

**Plotting procedure (KTU exam favorite):**
- Measure $s$ experimentally at multiple doses.
- Plot $s + \sqrt{s}$ on y-axis vs. $1/D$ on x-axis.
- Slope = $\dfrac{9.65 \times 10^6}{G(X) \cdot M_n}$ → solve for $G(X)$.
- Intercept = $\dfrac{G(S)}{2 G(X)}$ → solve for $G(S)$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Mass Loss from Outgassing

Assume a polymer of initial mass $m_0$ loses mass as gas with G-value $G_{gas}$ (events per 100 eV).

**Step 1 — Mass lost per unit dose per unit mass:**
$$\frac{dm}{dD} = -\frac{G_{gas} \cdot m \cdot M_{gas}}{9.648 \times 10^6}$$

where $M_{gas}$ is the molecular weight of evolved gas in g/mol.

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$\int_{m_0}^{m} \frac{dm}{m} = -\frac{G_{gas} \cdot M_{gas}}{9.648 \times 10^6} \int_{0}^{D} dD$$

**Step 3 — Exponential decay solution:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{m}{m_0}\right) = -\frac{G_{gas} \cdot M_{gas} \cdot D}{9.648 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for final mass:**

$$m = m_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{G_{gas} \cdot M_{gas} \cdot D}{9.648 \times 10^6}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Fractional mass loss:**

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\Delta m}{m_0} = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{G_{gas} \cdot M_{gas} \cdot D}{9.648 \times 10^6}\right)\,}$$

**Numerical sanity check (PTFE in vacuum):** $G_{gas} \approx 5$, $M_{gas} \approx 2$ g/mol (H₂), $D = 100$ kGy $= 10^5$ Gy.

$$\frac{\Delta m}{m_0} = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{5 \times 2 \times 10^5}{9.648 \times 10^6}\right) = 1 - \exp(-0.1036) = 0.098 \approx 9.8\%$$

This matches literature values for PTFE mass loss in vacuum at 100 kGy, confirming the derivation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Polymer Family vs. Dominant Mechanism

| Polymer | Repeat Unit | G(S) | G(X) | Dominant Effect | Industrial Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE) | –CH₂–CH₂– | 0.3 | 2.0 | Crosslink | XLPE wire insulation |
| High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE) | –CH₂–CH₂– | 0.2 | 1.9 | Crosslink | Radiation-sterilized implants |
| Polypropylene (PP) | –CH₂–CH(CH₃)– | 0.3 | 0.6 | Mixed (oxidative scission) | Stabilizers mandatory |
| Polystyrene (PS) | –CH₂–CH(C₆H₅)– | 0.02 | 0.05 | Crosslink | Radiation-resistant |
| Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) | –CH₂–CHCl– | 0.3 | 0.5 | Crosslink + HCl loss | Discoloration, must add stabilizers |
| Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) | –CH₂–C(CH₃)(COOCH₃)– | 1.7 | 0.5 | Scission | Optical parts degrade |
| Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) | –CF₂–CF₂– | 5.0 | 0.3 | Strong scission | Avoid in radiation fields |
| Polyisobutylene (PIB) | –CH₂–C(CH₃)₂– | 5.0 | 0.5 | Strong scission | Avoid in radiation fields |
| Natural Rubber (cis-Polyisoprene) | –CH₂–C(CH₃)=CH–CH₂– | 0.2 | 1.5 | Crosslink | Radiation-vulcanized latex |
| Polyimide (Kapton) | Aromatic/Imide | < 0.01 | < 0.01 | Highly resistant | Spacecraft insulation |
| Cellulose | C₆H₇O₂(OH)₃ | 2.5 | 0.5 | Scission | Paper embrittlement in archives |
| Nylon-6,6 | –(CH₂)₆–NH–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO–NH– | 0.4 | 0.8 | Crosslink | Limited radiation use |
| Polyethylene oxide (PEO) | –CH₂–CH₂–O– | 1.5 | 0.8 | Scission | Drug delivery matrix degrades |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 14-mark KTU problems:** You will be given a polymer and asked to predict whether it will crosslink or scission, and to recommend an application. Memorize the G(S)/G(X) ratio for the top 6 polymers above.

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Three-Stage Radiation Degradation Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 0: Incident Radiation] --> B{Stage 1: Physical Interaction}
    B --> B1[Photoelectric Effect below 100 keV]
    B --> B2[Compton Scattering 100 keV to 10 MeV]
    B --> B3[Pair Production above 1.022 MeV]
    
    B1 --> C[Stage 2: Energy Deposition]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    
    C --> C1[Secondary Electrons 10 to 100 eV]
    C --> C2[Excitation and Ionization Events]
    C --> C3[Local Thermal Spike 1 to 10 nm]
    
    C1 --> D[Stage 3: Chemical Reactions]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    
    D --> D1[Free Radical Formation R dot]

    D3 --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Polymer Response Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Polymer Exposed to Ionizing Radiation] --> Q1{Is G of X greater than G of S?}
    
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is Oxygen Present?}
    Q1 -->|No| R1[Scission Dominant Path]
    
    Q2 -->|No| S1[Clean Crosslinking]
```

```mermaid
    P2 --> P3[Calculate s plus square root s]
    P3 --> P4[Plot y-axis s plus square root s versus x-axis 1 divided by D]
    P4 --> P5{Determine Slope and Intercept}
    P5 --> P6[Slope equals 9.65e6 divided by G of X times M_n]
    P5 --> P7[Intercept equals G of S divided by 2 times G of X]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper PEP867]

**Define G-value in the context of polymer radiation chemistry. How is it used to distinguish between crosslinking and chain scission behavior?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
G-value (radiation-chemical yield) is defined as the number of specific chemical events (such as crosslinks, chain scissions, or gas molecules evolved) produced per 100 eV of radiation energy absorbed by the polymer. It is denoted G(X), where X is the event type. The two most important G-values are **G(S)** for chain scission and **G(X)** for crosslinking.

- If **G(X) > G(S)**, the polymer is **crosslinking-dominant**: molecular weight increases, gel forms at a critical dose, and the polymer becomes insoluble.
- If **G(S) > G(X)**, the polymer is **chain-scission-dominant**: molecular weight decreases, the polymer becomes brittle, and eventually disintegrates.

The **ratio G(S)/G(X)** is the diagnostic parameter: a value less than 0.25 indicates strong crosslinking; greater than 4 indicates strong scission. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]

**Explain the three interaction mechanisms by which ionizing radiation loses energy in polymers. Indicate the energy range where each dominates.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three mechanisms, in order of increasing photon energy, are:

1. **Photoelectric Effect (E < 100 keV):** The photon transfers all its energy to a bound inner-shell electron, which is ejected with kinetic energy $E_e = h\nu - E_b$. The photon is completely absorbed. Cross-section scales as $Z^{4-5}/E^{3}$.

2. **Compton Scattering (100 keV < E < 10 MeV):** The photon scatters off a loosely bound (effectively free) outer electron, transferring part of its energy. The scattered photon continues with reduced energy and a deflected direction. This is the **dominant mechanism for ⁶⁰Co γ-rays (1.17 and 1.33 MeV)**.

3. **Pair Production (E > 1.022 MeV):** The photon converts into an electron-positron pair in the Coulomb field of an atomic nucleus, conserving both energy and momentum. The positron subsequently annihilates, producing two 511 keV photons.

These mechanisms together determine the linear energy transfer (LET) and depth-dose profile in the polymer. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **three-stage mechanism** by which ionizing radiation causes chemical changes in a polymer, including the specific free-radical reactions that lead to chain scission and crosslinking. Discuss the role of oxygen in modifying the radiation response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample of mass 25 g is irradiated with γ-rays at a total dose of 80 kGy. Given G(S) = 1.5 and G(X) = 0.4 (per 100 eV), calculate (i) the number of chain-scission events, (ii) the number of crosslink events, and (iii) predict the dominant macroscopic effect on the polymer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Stage 1: Physical Stage (10⁻¹⁸ to 10⁻¹⁵ s) [2 marks]**
A high-energy photon (γ-ray or X-ray) or accelerated particle (electron, proton, α) enters the polymer. It loses energy through three mechanisms — photoelectric absorption, Compton scattering, and (above 1.022 MeV) pair production. The dominant result is the ejection of fast secondary electrons with energies typically 10 to 100 eV, plus ionized (R•⁺) and excited (R*) polymer molecules. The polymer's electrons — particularly those in C–C and C–H bonds — are the principal targets.

**Stage 2: Physico-Chemical Stage (10⁻¹⁵ to 10⁻¹² s) [2 marks]**
The secondary electrons thermalize within 1 to 10 nm of their point of origin, creating localized "thermal spikes" with peak temperatures of 10³ to 10⁴ K lasting about 1 picosecond. During this interval, ions dissociate into free radicals (R•⁺ + e⁻ → R• + R"•), excited molecules dissociate, and bond homolysis breaks the polymer backbone.

**Stage 3: Chemical Stage (10⁻¹² s to hours) [2 marks]**
Free radicals diffuse and react:
- **Crosslinking:** Two alkyl radicals on adjacent chains combine: 2 –CH₂–ĊH– → –CH₂–CH(–)–CH(–)–CH₂– (forms a C–C bridge between chains).
- **Chain scission:** A backbone radical undergoes β-scission: –CH₂–ĊH–CH₂–CH₂– → –CH₂–CH=CH₂ + •CH₂– (breaks the main chain).
- **Hydrogen evolution:** Most prevalent: 2 –CH₂–ĊH– → –CH₂–CH=CH– + H₂↑.

**Role of oxygen [1 mark]**
In the presence of O₂, alkyl radicals (R•) rapidly react to form peroxy radicals (ROO•), which abstract hydrogen to form hydroperoxides (ROOH). Hydroperoxides decompose to alkoxy (RO•) and hydroxyl (•OH) radicals, propagating oxidative chain scission. Thus, irradiation in air causes **oxidative degradation** far more aggressive than irradiation in vacuum or inert atmosphere. This is why radiation sterilization of polymers is often performed in nitrogen-purged packaging.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Mass: $m = 25$ g
- Dose: $D = 80 \text{ kGy} = 8.0 \times 10^4$ Gy
- $G(S) = 1.5$, $G(X) = 0.4$ (per 100 eV)

**(i) Number of chain-scission events [2 marks]**

$$N_s = 6.243 \times 10^{17} \times G(S) \times D \times m$$

$$N_s = 6.243 \times 10^{17} \times 1.5 \times (8.0 \times 10^4) \times 25$$

$$N_s = 6.243 \times 1.5 \times 8.0 \times 25 \times 10^{21}$$

$$N_s = 1873 \times 10^{21} = 1.873 \times 10^{24} \text{ events}$$

**(ii) Number of crosslink events [2 marks]**

$$N_x = 6.243 \times 10^{17} \times G(X) \times D \times m$$

$$N_x = 6.243 \times 10^{17} \times 0.4 \times (8.0 \times 10^4) \times 25$$

$$N_x = 4.994 \times 10^{23} \text{ events}$$

**(iii) Predicted dominant effect [3 marks]**

$$\frac{N_s}{N_x} = \frac{1.873 \times 10^{24}}{4.994 \times 10^{23}} = 3.75$$

$$\frac{G(S)}{G(X)} = \frac{1.5}{0.4} = 3.75$$

Since $G(S) > G(X)$ (ratio of 3.75), **chain scission is the dominant mechanism**. The polymer will:

- Undergo a **decrease in number-average molecular weight** ($M_n$).
- Become **brittle and lose ductility** (reduction in elongation at break).
- Show **mass loss** if volatile fragments escape.
- Eventually form a **low-molecular-weight powder** at very high doses.

The polymer is unsuitable for radiation-sterilized medical devices and would NOT be recommended for use in nuclear or space radiation environments. The properties resemble those of **PMMA, PTFE, or PIB** (all of which are scission-dominant).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam, Supplementary]

**(a)** Discuss the **Charlesby–Pinner equation** as applied to radiation-crosslinkable polymers. Explain how a plot of $s + \sqrt{s}$ versus $1/D$ yields the G-values for crosslinking and scission, and outline the assumptions of the model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the radiation response of **polyethylene (PE)** and **polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)**. Use specific G-values and structural reasoning to justify why one polymer is used in radiation-sterilized medical products while the other is strictly avoided in radiation environments. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Charlesby–Pinner equation (general form) [2 marks]**

For a polymer with the *most-probable* (Schulz–Flory, $M_w / M_n = 2$) initial molecular-weight distribution, the sol fraction $s$ at a given dose $D$ obeys:

$$s + \sqrt{s} = \frac{p_0}{q_0} + \frac{1}{q_0 u_1 D}$$

In terms of radiation-chemical yields, this is written:

$$s + \sqrt{s} = \frac{G(S)}{2 G(X)} + \frac{9.65 \times 10^{6}}{G(X) \cdot M_n \cdot D}$$

**Plotting procedure [2 marks]**
A series of samples is irradiated at different doses $D_i$, and the sol fraction $s_i$ is measured (e.g., by Soxhlet extraction in a suitable solvent). Then:
- Compute $s_i + \sqrt{s_i}$ (y-axis values).
- Compute $1/D_i$ (x-axis values).
- Plot y vs. x. According to the equation, the result is a **straight line** with:
  - **Slope** = $\dfrac{9.65 \times 10^{6}}{G(X) \cdot M_n}$ → solve for $G(X)$.
  - **Intercept** = $\dfrac{G(S)}{2 G(X)}$ → solve for $G(S)$ once $G(X)$ is known.

**Assumptions of the model [2 marks]**
1. Crosslinks and scissions occur **randomly and uniformly** along the chain.
2. The initial molecular-weight distribution is the *most-probable* distribution ($M_w / M_n = 2$); for other distributions a correction is needed.
3. The G-values are **independent of dose** (no dose-rate effects).
4. **No end-linking** or long-chain branching is present initially.
5. Crosslinks and scissions occur **independently** of each other.
6. Chain ends are not considered (infinite-chain approximation).

**Limitation:** The model fails at very low doses where the chain population is far from random, and at very high doses where crosslinks cluster (intramolecular crosslinks that do not contribute to gel).

**Why this is useful:** The Charlesby–Pinner plot is the standard experimental technique to extract both G-values from a single set of sol-fraction measurements. It is the **only** practical method to obtain G(S) and G(X) for insoluble or crosslinked polymers.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Polyethylene (PE) [3.5 marks]**
- Repeat unit: –CH₂–CH₂–
- G(S) ≈ 0.2 to 0.3
- G(X) ≈ 1.8 to 2.0
- Ratio G(S)/G(X) ≈ 0.10 to 0.17
- **Mechanism:** When a secondary radical forms on a PE chain, it is a primary alkyl radical. Two such radicals on adjacent chains can recombine to form a stable C–C crosslink. There is no labile bond (e.g., no quaternary carbon) that promotes β-scission. The result is rapid gel formation at doses as low as 10 to 50 kGy.
- **Use:** PE is the **polymer of choice** for γ-sterilized medical devices (syringes, catheters, IV tubing) and for e-beam-crosslinked wire insulation (XLPE) rated to 90 °C.

**Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) [3.5 marks]**
- Repeat unit: –CF₂–CF₂–
- G(S) ≈ 3.0 to 5.0
- G(X) ≈ 0.2 to 0.3
- Ratio G(S)/G(X) ≈ 10 to 25
- **Mechanism:** The C–F bond is highly polarized, and the perfluorinated backbone is mechanically and electronically distinct from hydrocarbon polymers. Radiation causes massive chain scission because:
  1. The main-chain C–C bond is weakened by the strongly electron-withdrawing CF₂ groups.
  2. F• radicals formed are extremely reactive and abstract fluorine, propagating chain scission.
  3. C–C bond dissociation energy in PTFE is reduced from ~3.6 eV (PE) to ~2.5 eV.
- **Use:** PTFE is **strictly avoided** in radiation environments. A dose of 10 to 100 kGy causes a 90% reduction in tensile strength and embrittlement. It cannot be sterilized by γ-radiation; manufacturers use ethylene oxide gas instead.

**Summary Table:**

| Property | PE | PTFE |
|---|---|---|
| Dominant response | Crosslink | Scission |
| G(X) | ~2.0 | ~0.3 |
| G(S) | ~0.3 | ~5.0 |
| Behavior at 50 kGy | Stronger, insoluble | Brittle, powdery |
| Sterilization method | γ or e-beam | Ethylene oxide gas |
| Industrial use | XLPE, syringes | Non-stick coatings (no radiation) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Failing to convert kGy → Gy or eV/100eV → μmol/J is the single largest source of calculation mistakes. Always state conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing "degradation" with "scission only":** Radiation-induced *crosslinking* is also a form of "degradation" because it destroys processability and may cause stress cracking. KTU expects you to mention *both* directions of property change.
> 3. **Ignoring oxygen effects:** A 14-mark question on radiation effects that does not discuss the role of O₂ (oxidative degradation) is incomplete. Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Forgetting the dose-rate effect:** Low dose rates (extended exposure) allow radical-radical recombination, reducing net damage. Students frequently miss this in design problems.
> 5. **Memorizing G-values without understanding structure:** The G(S)/G(X) ratio is governed by *why* radicals prefer one pathway. A mechanistic answer beats a memorized number.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Ultra-Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Night Revision):**

- [x] **Ionizing radiation threshold:** > 10 eV; practical polymer range 10 keV – 10 MeV.
- [x] **Three energy-loss mechanisms:** Photoelectric (E < 100 keV), Compton (100 keV – 10 MeV), Pair production (E > 1.022 MeV).
- [x] **Three degradation stages:** Physical (10⁻¹⁸ – 10⁻¹⁵ s) → Physico-chemical (10⁻¹⁵ – 10⁻¹² s) → Chemical (10⁻¹² s – hours).
- [x] **G-value unit:** events per 100 eV (old) or μmol/J (SI); conversion factor 9.648.
- [x] **Dose unit:** Gray (Gy) = 1 J/kg = 100 rad.
- [x] **Crosslinking-dominant polymers (memorize):** PE, PP, PS, PVC, Nylon-6,6, Natural rubber.
- [x] **Scission-dominant polymers (memorize):** PMMA, PTFE, PIB, Cellulose, Poly-α-methylstyrene.
- [x] **Why aromatic polymers resist radiation:** π-electron delocalization dissipates absorbed energy as heat.
- [x] **Why PTFE is radiation-sensitive:** Strong electron-withdrawing CF₂ groups weaken backbone; high G(S).
- [x] **Oxygen effect:** Converts R• to ROO• → ROOH → RO• + •OH → oxidative chain scission.
- [x] **Charlesby–Pinner equation:** $s + \sqrt{s} = \dfrac{G(S)}{2 G(X)} + \dfrac{9.65 \times 10^6}{G(X) \cdot M_n \cdot D}$.
- [x] **Slope gives G(X); intercept gives G(S)/[2 G(X)].**
- [x] **Critical dose for gel formation:** $D_{gel} \cdot M_n = 9.65 \times 10^6 / [2 G(X)]$ for a most-probable distribution.
- [x] **Sterilization dose:** 25 kGy is the international standard for medical-device sterilization.
- [x] **H₂ is the dominant evolved gas** in most hydrocarbon polymers under irradiation in vacuum.
- [x] **G(S)/G(X) classification:** < 0.25 → strong crosslinker; > 4 → strong scissioner.
- [x] **Industrial examples to remember:** XLPE wire insulation (crosslinking); PTFE seals in nuclear plants are forbidden (scission); γ-sterilized PE syringes are standard; PMMA aircraft windows degrade and yellow under cosmic radiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Emphasis:** When asked "predict the dominant effect of radiation on polymer X," the structured answer is: (1) State G(S) and G(X); (2) Compute the ratio; (3) State which effect dominates; (4) Explain mechanistically *why* (radical structure, backbone stability, presence of labile groups); (5) Discuss oxygen effect; (6) Suggest one industrial implication. This 6-step structure scores full marks every time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
