topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT867 - FAILURE ANALYSIS OF POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — HYDROLYSIS
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HYDROLYSIS (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> *Hydrolysis* is a **chemical degradation mechanism** in which water molecules (or aqueous media) cleave the labile bonds in a polymer chain — typically **ester, amide, urethane, carbonate, or acetal linkages** — leading to a reduction in **molecular weight**, deterioration of **mechanical properties**, and ultimately **in-service failure** of the polymeric component.

The process is fundamentally a **reverse condensation reaction**. Every condensation polymer (those formed with the elimination of a small molecule such as H₂O) can, under appropriate thermodynamic and kinetic conditions, be **broken back down** by hydrolysis. This is the *reversibility* principle of condensation polymerization.

### 1.2 Polymers Most Vulnerable to Hydrolysis

| Polymer Family | Labile Bond | Typical Industrial Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Polyesters (PET, PBT, PEN) | –COO– (ester) | Bottles, fibres, films |
| Polyamides (Nylon-6, Nylon-6,6) | –CONH– (amide) | Gears, ropes, filaments |
| Polycarbonates (PC) | –O–CO–O– (carbonate) | Safety glasses, CDs |
| Polyurethanes (PU) | –NH–CO–O– (urethane) | Foams, coatings |
| Polyimides, Polyacetals | Various | Engineering thermoplastics |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🔍 The "Rust of Plastics" Analogy**
> Think of hydrolysis as the **"rust" of polymers**. Just as iron rusts when exposed to moisture, polymers — especially those made by condensation — slowly disintegrate when exposed to water. Imagine a **zipper (the polymer chain)** made of plastic teeth held together by weak clips (the ester/amide bonds). Each raindrop (water molecule) works like a pair of pliers, prying open one clip at a time. After months or years of this microscopic attack, the zipper fails catastrophically even though the bulk water exposure looked trivial. *This is exactly how a Nylon-6,6 gear tooth shears off in a humid chemical plant, or how a PET bottle cracks after long-term storage in a warehouse.*

### 1.4 Classification of Hydrolytic Degradation

1. **Bulk Hydrolysis** — Water diffuses throughout the entire polymer volume; degradation is uniform. Common in **thin films, fibres, foams**.
2. **Surface (Heterogeneous) Hydrolysis** — Reaction occurs predominantly at the polymer-water interface. Common in **thick moulded parts** where diffusion is slow.
3. **Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC) combined with hydrolysis** — Applied stress accelerates chain scission at the crack tip.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ):** **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- **Standard Temperature for accelerated testing:** **23 °C (296 K)** and **85 °C / 85 % RH** (industry standard accelerated aging condition)
- **Hydrolytic stability ranking metric:** *Time to 50 % molecular weight loss (t₁/₂)* at given T and RH.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" — Mechanistic Logic Steps

### Step 1: Water Sorption (Mass Transfer Stage)
Before hydrolysis can begin, water molecules must **diffuse into** the polymer matrix. This obeys **Fick's Second Law**:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

where $C$ is the water concentration, $D$ is the diffusion coefficient (m²/s), $x$ is the spatial coordinate, and $t$ is time.

> **Why this matters:** Even if the polymer is *thermodynamically* susceptible, *kinetically* hydrolysis is limited by how fast water can get inside. Glassy polymers (PC, PET) are slower to hydrate than rubbery ones (PU, PA elastomers).

### Step 2: Nucleophilic Attack on the Labile Carbonyl Carbon
The **oxygen lone pair of H₂O** attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon (C=O) of the ester or amide bond. This forms a **tetrahedral intermediate**.

### Step 3: Proton Transfer & Bond Cleavage
The tetrahedral intermediate collapses, expelling the alcohol/amine leaving group and regenerating a carboxylic acid (or amide-derived acid + amine).

### Step 4: Catalytic Acceleration
- **Acid catalysis:** H⁺ protonates the carbonyl oxygen, making carbon more electrophilic.
- **Base (alkaline) catalysis:** OH⁻ directly attacks carbonyl — *saponification* (especially aggressive for polyesters).
- **Neutral hydrolysis:** Uncatalysed; slowest but unavoidable in service.

### Step 5: Property Loss Manifestation
Each chain scission event reduces the **number-average molecular weight (Mₙ)**. Tensile strength and impact resistance scale roughly as:

$$\sigma \propto M_n^{0.5 \text{ to } 1}$$

so a small loss in Mₙ translates to **catastrophic property collapse** at critical service life.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Equation / Concept | Expression | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First-order hydrolysis rate | $\displaystyle -\frac{d[ester]}{dt} = k_{hyd}[H_2O][Ester]$ | $k_{hyd}$ = rate constant (M⁻¹s⁻¹ or s⁻¹ for pseudo-first-order) |
| 2 | Pseudo-first-order form (excess water) | $\displaystyle \ln\!\left(\frac{[E]_0}{[E]}\right) = k' \cdot t$ | $[E]_0$ = initial concentration |
| 3 | Half-life of polymer bonds | $\displaystyle t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k'} = \frac{0.693}{k'}$ | Time for 50 % bond cleavage (s, h, days) |
| 4 | Arrhenius temperature dependence | $\displaystyle k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol); $A$ = pre-exponential factor |
| 5 | Fickian diffusion of water | $\displaystyle \frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s) |
| 6 | Molecular weight vs. scissions | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{M_n(t)} - \frac{1}{M_{n,0}} = \frac{n_s}{w}$ | $n_s$ = number of scissions, $w$ = sample mass |
| 7 | Acid-catalysed rate | $\displaystyle k_{acid} = k_{H^+}[H^+]$ | Catalysed by [H⁺] |
| 8 | Base-catalysed rate (saponification) | $\displaystyle k_{base} = k_{OH^-}[OH^-]$ | Catalysed by [OH⁻] — dominant for polyesters |
| 9 | Relative humidity effect | $\displaystyle k \propto \left(\frac{RH}{100}\right)^n, \quad n \approx 1$ | Empirical; $n$ varies 0.5–2 |
| 10 | Tensile strength vs Mₙ | $\displaystyle \sigma \propto M_n^{\alpha}, \quad 0.5 \le \alpha \le 1$ | Flory's strength–MW relation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Equations (3) and (4) are the *two most-tested formulas* in KTU exams on hydrolysis. Master them and you master the numerical problems.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

Hydrolysis prediction is critical in:

- **🏭 Chemical Process Industries** — Nylon pump housings, PET bottle failures in hot climates.
- **🚗 Automotive** — Polyurethane foam degradation in car seats; ester-based thermoplastic polyester elastomers in under-hood applications.
- **💊 Biomedical** — Hydrolytic degradation of PLA, PGA, PCL, PLGA sutures and scaffolds is *desirable* — this is the principle behind bioresorbable implants.
- **⚡ Electronics** — Polyimide and PET film insulation under humid operating conditions.
- **🌊 Marine** — Ester-based polyurethane coatings on ship hulls.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pseudo-First-Order Hydrolysis Kinetics

**Starting premise:** A polymer contains $N_0$ hydrolyzable ester bonds per gram at time $t = 0$. At time $t$, the number of *remaining* (uncleaved) bonds is $N(t)$.

**Mechanistic Step 1 — Rate law (bimolecular, elementary reaction):**

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} = k \cdot [H_2O] \cdot N$$

**Mechanistic Step 2 — Water is in vast excess (bulk hydrolysis of thin samples in humid air or immersed in water), so $[H_2O]$ is effectively constant. We define a pseudo-first-order constant:**

$$k' = k \cdot [H_2O]$$

Substituting:

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} = k' \cdot N$$

**Mechanistic Step 3 — Separation of variables:**

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k' \cdot dt$$

**Mechanistic Step 4 — Integration from $N_0$ to $N(t)$:**

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = -k' \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right) = -k' \cdot t$$

**Mechanistic Step 5 — Exponential form:**

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{-k' t}$$

**Mechanistic Step 6 — For half-life, set $N(t) = 0.5\,N_0$:**

$$0.5 = e^{-k' t_{1/2}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k'} = \frac{0.693}{k'}$$

> ✅ **Result:** The hydrolysis follows an exponential decay of bond concentration with characteristic time $t_{1/2}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Connection Between Chain Scissions and Molecular Weight

**Starting premise:** Each hydrolytic cleavage creates one additional chain. If a sample of mass $w$ initially has $N_0$ chains, and $n_s$ scissions have occurred by time $t$, then the new number of chains is:

$$N(t) = N_0 + n_s$$

**Step 1 — Number-average molecular weight is defined as:**

$$M_n = \frac{w}{N}$$

Therefore:

$$M_n(t) = \frac{w}{N_0 + n_s}$$

$$M_{n,0} = \frac{w}{N_0}$$

**Step 2 — Take reciprocals:**

$$\frac{1}{M_n(t)} = \frac{N_0 + n_s}{w} = \frac{N_0}{w} + \frac{n_s}{w} = \frac{1}{M_{n,0}} + \frac{n_s}{w}$$

**Step 3 — Rearranged (this is the canonical "scission equation"):**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{M_n(t)} - \frac{1}{M_{n,0}} = \frac{n_s}{w}\;}$$

> **Utility:** By measuring $M_n(t)$ (e.g., via GPC / viscometry) and knowing $w$, the experimenter can directly count the number of hydrolytic chain breaks — the most fundamental measure of degradation extent.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Arrhenius Acceleration for Lifetime Prediction

**Step 1 — Define service life as the time $t_f$ when $M_n$ drops to a critical value $M_n^{crit}$:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{M_{n,0}}{M_n^{crit}}\right) = k'(T) \cdot t_f$$

Solving for $t_f$:

$$t_f = \frac{1}{k'(T)} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{M_{n,0}}{M_n^{crit}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Arrhenius form for $k'(T)$:**

$$t_f = \frac{e^{E_a/RT}}{A \cdot [H_2O]} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{M_{n,0}}{M_n^{crit}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration Factor (AF) between two test temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:**

$$AF = \frac{t_{f,\,T_1}}{t_{f,\,T_2}} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

> ✅ **Worked numerical insight:** For $E_a = 90$ kJ/mol (typical for polyester hydrolysis), going from 25 °C (298 K) to 85 °C (358 K) accelerates degradation by approximately **80–120×**. This is why the **85 °C / 85 % RH** test is universally adopted.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A PET bottle grade polymer has $M_{n,0} = 30{,}000$ g/mol. After 6 months in 85 °C water, $M_n$ drops to 18,000 g/mol. Estimate the additional time until $M_n$ reaches 12,000 g/mol, assuming first-order hydrolysis.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate pseudo-first-order constant from data:**

$$k' = \frac{1}{t}\ln\!\left(\frac{M_{n,0}}{M_n(t)}\right) = \frac{1}{6}\ln\!\left(\frac{30{,}000}{18{,}000}\right) = \frac{1}{6}(0.5108) \approx 0.0851 \;\text{month}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute additional time $t_2$ from current state to target:**

$$t_2 = \frac{1}{k'}\ln\!\left(\frac{18{,}000}{12{,}000}\right) = \frac{1}{0.0851}\ln(1.5) = \frac{0.4055}{0.0851} \approx 4.77 \text{ months}$$

**Step 3 — Total service life from start:**

$$t_{total} = 6 + 4.77 = 10.77 \text{ months}$$

> ✅ **Engineering verdict:** This PET grade is unsuitable for hot, humid long-term storage beyond ~11 months under these conditions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mechanism Flowchart — Hydrolytic Degradation of an Ester Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Water Sorption - Fickian diffusion into polymer matrix] --> B[Step 2: Nucleophilic attack of H2O oxygen on carbonyl carbon]
    B --> C[Step 3: Tetrahedral intermediate formation]
    C --> D[Step 4: Proton transfer - leaving group departure]
    D --> E[Step 5: Carboxylic acid fragment + Alcohol fragment generated]
    E --> F[Step 6: Reduction in M_n and chain length]
    F --> G[Step 7: Mechanical property loss - tensile, impact]
    G --> H[Step 8: Macroscopic failure - cracking, fracture, embrittlement]
```

## 4.2 Catalytic Pathway Diagram — Acid, Base, and Neutral Hydrolysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Polymer - COO - linkage] --> B{Catalytic Environment}
    B -- Acidic pH < 7 --> C[Acid Catalysed - H+ protonates carbonyl O]
    B -- Neutral pH equals 7 --> D[Neutral Hydrolysis - uncatalysed slow]
    B -- Alkaline pH greater than 7 --> E[Base Catalysed Saponification - OH- direct attack]
    C --> F[Chain scission products]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Failure]
```

## 4.3 Polymer-Failure Onset Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer in Service] --> B{Water Present}
    B -- No --> Z[Stable - No Hydrolysis]
    B -- Yes --> C{Labile Bonds present}
    C -- No --> Z
    C -- Yes --> D{Temperature High}
    D -- Low --> E[Slow Hydrolysis - Long Life]
    D -- High --> F[Rapid Hydrolysis]
    E --> G{M_n below critical}
    F --> G
    G -- No --> H[Service Continues]
    G -- Yes --> I[Mechanical Failure]
```

## 4.4 Polymer Susceptibility Classification Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[All Polymers] --> B{Condensation Polymer}
    B -- Yes --> C[SUSCEPTIBLE to Hydrolysis]
    B -- No --> D{Additive Polymer with hydrolysable groups}
    D -- Yes --> C
    D -- No --> E[RESISTANT - e.g. Polyethylene, Polypropylene]
    C --> F1[Polyesters]
    C --> F2[Polyamides]
    C --> F3[Polycarbonates]
    C --> F4[Polyurethanes]
    C --> F5[Polyimides]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define hydrolysis as a polymer failure mechanism. Name any four polymer families that are particularly susceptible to hydrolytic degradation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Hydrolysis is a chemical degradation process in which water (or aqueous solutions) cleaves the labile functional groups — primarily **ester, amide, urethane, carbonate, or acetal bonds** — in a polymer chain, reducing its molecular weight and mechanical integrity. It is essentially the **reverse of condensation polymerization**.
>
> **Susceptible polymers (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Polyesters** (e.g., PET, PBT — contain ester linkages)
> 2. **Polyamides** (e.g., Nylon-6, Nylon-6,6 — contain amide linkages)
> 3. **Polycarbonates** (contain carbonate linkages)
> 4. **Polyurethanes** (contain urethane linkages)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain how temperature and pH influence the rate of hydrolytic degradation in polymers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Temperature effect (1.5 marks):** Hydrolysis rate obeys the **Arrhenius equation**, $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$. Higher temperature exponentially accelerates bond cleavage. For a typical polyester ($E_a \approx 80$–100 kJ/mol), a 60 °C rise can increase the rate by **two orders of magnitude**. This is the basis of the standard 85 °C / 85 % RH accelerated aging test.
>
> **pH effect (1.5 marks):**
> - **Acidic medium (pH < 7):** H⁺ ions catalyse hydrolysis by protonating the carbonyl oxygen, increasing its electrophilicity.
> - **Alkaline medium (pH > 7):** OH⁻ ions cause **saponification** — particularly aggressive on polyesters, where ester bonds are rapidly converted to carboxylate salts and alcohols.
> - **Neutral medium:** Slowest but thermodynamically inevitable; long-term failures still occur in pure water.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat mechanism, explain the **hydrolytic degradation of an ester linkage** in a polyester. Discuss the role of **acid and base catalysis**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **number-average molecular weight ($M_n$)** and the number of hydrolytic chain scissions ($n_s$). A PET sample has initial $M_{n,0} = 40{,}000$ g/mol. After 8 months in hot water at 85 °C, the $M_n$ drops to 25,000 g/mol. Calculate the number of chain scissions per gram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

**Part (a) — Mechanism of Ester Hydrolysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Water sorption:** Water diffuses into the polymer (Fickian diffusion), with concentration $C(x,t)$ governed by Fick's second law.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Nucleophilic attack:**
The lone pair on water oxygen attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the ester group:

$$\text{R–CO–O–R'} + H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–C(OH)(OH)–O–R'} \;\;(\text{tetrahedral intermediate})$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Collapse & bond cleavage:**
The intermediate collapses, expelling R'–OH (alcohol) and forming R–COOH (carboxylic acid):

$$\text{R–C(OH)(OH)–O–R'} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–COOH} + \text{R'–OH}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Acid catalysis:**
H⁺ protonates the carbonyl oxygen (–C=O → –C=OH⁺), making the carbon **more electrophilic**, accelerating nucleophilic attack.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Base catalysis (saponification):**
OH⁻ attacks the carbonyl directly, forming a tetrahedral intermediate that expels R'O⁻, yielding RCOO⁻ (carboxylate) + R'OH. Saponification is **irreversible** (carboxylate is stable) and is the most aggressive form of polyester hydrolysis.

---

**Part (b) — Derivation & Calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from $M_n = w/N$, where $w$ = sample mass, $N$ = number of chains:

$$M_{n,0} = \frac{w}{N_0}, \quad M_n(t) = \frac{w}{N_0 + n_s}$$

Taking reciprocals:

$$\frac{1}{M_{n,0}} = \frac{N_0}{w}, \quad \frac{1}{M_n(t)} = \frac{N_0 + n_s}{w}$$

Subtracting:

$$\frac{1}{M_n(t)} - \frac{1}{M_{n,0}} = \frac{n_s}{w}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{M_n(t)} - \frac{1}{M_{n,0}} = \frac{1}{25{,}000} - \frac{1}{40{,}000}$$

Computing each term:

$$\frac{1}{25{,}000} = 4.00 \times 10^{-5}, \quad \frac{1}{40{,}000} = 2.50 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{n_s}{w} = (4.00 - 2.50) \times 10^{-5} = 1.50 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{mol/g}$$

> ✅ **Final answer:** $n_s/w = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ moles of chain scissions per gram of polymer = $9.03 \times 10^{18}$ scissions per gram.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Supplementary]**

**(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting the rate of hydrolytic degradation** in polymers, with emphasis on **temperature, humidity, pH, and polymer morphology**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Arrhenius equation**, calculate the **Acceleration Factor (AF)** between 25 °C and 85 °C for a polyester with activation energy $E_a = 90$ kJ/mol. Comment on its engineering significance. **Given R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

**Part (a) — Factors Affecting Hydrolysis (7 marks):**

1. **Temperature (2 marks):** Higher temperature accelerates both water diffusion and bond cleavage via Arrhenius kinetics. Typical doubling of rate every 7–10 °C.

2. **Humidity / Water Activity (2 marks):** Higher relative humidity increases equilibrium water uptake. Empirically, $k \propto (RH/100)^n$. Saturated steam (100 % RH at high T) is the most aggressive environment.

3. **pH of Environment (1.5 marks):**
   - Acid: catalysis via carbonyl protonation.
   - Base: saponification (esp. polyesters).
   - Neutral: baseline uncatalysed rate.

4. **Polymer Morphology (1.5 marks):** Amorphous regions absorb water more readily than crystalline regions. Hydrolysis preferentially attacks amorphous domains first. Hydrophilic groups (–OH, –CONH–) in the backbone increase water uptake and thus hydrolysis rate.

---

**Part (b) — Arrhenius Acceleration Factor (7 marks):**

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures (0.5 mark):**

$$T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 85 + 273 = 358 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute reciprocal difference (1 mark):**

$$\frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{358} = \frac{358 - 298}{298 \times 358} = \frac{60}{106{,}684} = 5.624 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute E_a/R (1 mark):**

$$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{90{,}000}{8.314} = 10{,}824.6 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Compute exponent and AF (2 marks):**

$$\text{Exponent} = 10{,}824.6 \times 5.624 \times 10^{-4} = 6.088$$

$$AF = e^{6.088} \approx 442$$

**Step 5 — Engineering significance (0.5 mark):**
> ✅ One year of real-time exposure at 25 °C is equivalent to ~24 hours of accelerated exposure at 85 °C under the same humidity. This allows **laboratory testing in days** instead of years — critical for product qualification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Arrhenius problems:** Always use $E_a$ in **J/mol** (not kJ/mol) when $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. Convert first.
> 2. **Confusing $M_n$ loss with mass loss:** Hydrolysis reduces molecular weight **without significant mass loss** (small molecules diffuse away only at advanced stages). Do not write "polymer dissolves" unless fully degraded.
> 3. **Forgetting catalyst dependence:** A common error is to treat hydrolysis as *uncatalysed* even in acidic or alkaline service — examiners specifically look for the *catalytic pathway*.
> 4. **Sign errors in half-life derivation:** $\ln(N/N_0) = -k't$, NOT $+k't$.
> 5. **Crystalline vs. amorphous:** Marks are reserved for mentioning that hydrolysis preferentially attacks amorphous regions — a high-yield point frequently missed.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Hydrolysis = reverse condensation; cleaves ester/amide/urethane/carbonate bonds.**
- ✅ **Susceptible polymers:** Polyesters, polyamides, polycarbonates, polyurethanes, polyimides.
- ✅ **Resistant polymers:** Polyolefins (PE, PP), PTFE — no hydrolysable backbone bonds.
- ✅ **Mechanism:** Water sorption → nucleophilic attack → tetrahedral intermediate → bond scission → carboxylic acid + alcohol/amine.
- ✅ **Catalysis:** Acid (protonation), base (saponification — strongest for polyesters), neutral (slow).
- ✅ **Pseudo-first-order kinetics:** $\ln(N_0/N) = k't$; $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k'$.
- ✅ **Scission equation:** $1/M_n(t) - 1/M_{n,0} = n_s/w$ — **most important equation of the topic.**
- ✅ **Arrhenius dependence:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$; typical $E_a = 80$–100 kJ/mol for polyesters.
- ✅ **Acceleration factor:** $AF = \exp[(E_a/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)]$ — basis of accelerated aging.
- ✅ **85 °C / 85 % RH** is the global standard accelerated test condition.
- ✅ **Morphology effect:** Amorphous regions degrade first; crystallinity confers partial resistance.
- ✅ **Property manifestation:** Tensile strength, impact resistance, elongation — all drop as $M_n$ decreases.
- ✅ **Engineering uses:** Failure prediction in bottles, gears, films, biomedical scaffolds (where it is *designed* to occur).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Stress Cracking - Crazing and Cracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT867 - FAILURE ANALYSIS OF POLYMERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental Stress Cracking - Crazing and Cracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC) — Formal Definition

**Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC)** is a long-term, premature, brittle failure mode of a polymeric material caused by the *simultaneous* and *synergistic* action of two factors:
1. A **tensile (applied) stress** — usually well below the short-term yield or ultimate tensile strength of the polymer.
2. A **physically aggressive (chemical) environment** — a fluid that by itself is chemically inert toward the polymer, but physically interacts with its surface and bulk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (verbatim from syllabus):** ESC is the catastrophic failure of a stressed polymer exposed to a "weak" chemical agent (a stress-cracking agent) that would not normally attack the polymer in the absence of stress.

**Standard Metric:** *ESCR* — **Environmental Stress Crack Resistance**, expressed as the **time-to-failure (hours)** under a fixed strain and environment (ASTM D1693, ASTM D5393).

### 1.2 Crazing — Formal Definition

A **craze** is a narrow, planar, crack-like region in a polymer, oriented **perpendicular to the maximum principal tensile stress**, which consists of:
- **~50 % interconnected microvoids**
- **~50 % highly drawn polymer fibrils** bridging the void space
- A typical **craze width** of *1–5 µm* and **craze length** of *10–1000 µm*

> [!NOTE]
> A craze is *not* a crack — it still transmits load through the fibrils. This is the central reason crazing acts as a precursor, not the final failure event.

### 1.3 Cracking — Formal Definition

**Stress cracking** is the propagation of a *true crack* (load-free surfaces) that originates from the rupture of craze fibrils. Once the crack forms, it advances with very little additional plastic work — a **brittle**, often catastrophic, failure mode.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — The Wet Rope Trick:** Imagine pulling a dry rope — it holds a moderate load. Now wet the rope (the "environment") and apply the *same* load. The wet rope snaps at a much lower force because water has plasticized the fibres. ESC behaves the same way — the fluid *plasticizes* the polymer chains near the surface, allowing them to slide past each other easily.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — The Wet Paper Tear:** Try tearing a dry sheet of paper vs. a paper towel wetted at its edge. The dry paper resists tearing (cra fibrils hold). The wet paper tears cleanly (fibrils rupture → true crack). This is exactly the craze-to-crack transition.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 3 — The Sponge Under Load:** A sponge (craze) can still carry water (load) through its ligaments. But if you keep squeezing, the ligaments rupture one by one and a channel (crack) opens up. Now the sponge cannot carry any load.

### 1.5 Key Stress-Cracking Agents (SCAs)

| Class | Examples | Affected Polymers |
|---|---|---|
| Surfactants | Igepal CO-630, Triton X-100, soap solutions | Polyethylene, Polypropylene |
| Oils & Greases | ASTM Oil No. 3, silicone oil | Nitrile rubber, Polyamide |
| Alcohols | Methanol, Ethanol, Isopropanol | Polycarbonate, PMMA, Polysulfone |
| Acids / Bases (dilute) | H₂SO₄, NaOH | Polyamides, POM |
| Halogenated fluids | TCE, perchloroethylene | PVC, PMMA |
| Liquid metals | Mercury, Gallium | Aluminium alloys (for analogy) |

### 1.6 Governing Physical Constants

- **Boltzmann constant** *k* = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **Gas constant** *R* = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard reference temperature** *T₀* = **298 K (25 °C)**
- **Surface tension of water** *γ* ≈ **72 mN/m**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Steps of the ESC Failure Mechanism

The ESC process is a **five-stage sequence** (each stage accelerated by the environment):

1. **Wetting & Sorption Stage:** The fluid contacts the polymer surface, wets it (Young's equation), and begins to diffuse into the amorphous regions following Fickian or Case-II kinetics.
2. **Plasticization Stage:** The sorbed fluid reduces the local glass-transition temperature Tg of the polymer by an amount predicted by the **Fox equation**:

   $$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

   where *w₁, w₂* are the weight fractions of polymer and penetrant.
3. **Craze Initiation Stage:** Under the applied stress σ, the local yield stress of the plasticized layer drops below σ. **Sternstein's criterion** for craze initiation is satisfied.
4. **Craze Growth Stage:** Microvoids nucleate, grow, and are bridged by drawn fibrils. The craze tip advances and the craze widens.
5. **Craze Breakdown → Crack Initiation → Propagation Stage:** Fibrils fail (chain scission / disentanglement). A true, load-free crack appears. The crack then propagates to final fracture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of ESC:** The environment does *not* chemically attack the polymer. It merely **lowers the energy barrier** for chain mobility. The applied stress then triggers crazing, which would not have happened in dry conditions at the same stress level.

### 2.2 Crazing — Detailed Mechanism

- **Initiation sites:** Surface flaws, dust, micro-scratches, rubber-particle interfaces (in HIPS, ABS).
- **Initiation criterion (Sternstein & Ongchin, 1969):**

  $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = A(T) + \frac{B(T)}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}$$

  where *σ₁, σ₂, σ₃* are principal stresses and *A, B* are temperature-dependent material constants.
- **Craze tip advance:** Dislocation-like motion; the **craze tip stress** σ_t is amplified by the stress concentration of the tip:

  $$\sigma_t = \sigma_{\text{applied}} \cdot K_t$$

  where *K_t* is the elastic stress concentration factor (typically *2.0–3.0* for a craze).

- **Craze thickening:** Fibrils cold-draw; new material is drawn in from the bulk at the craze–bulk interface.

### 2.3 Craze-to-Crack Transition

A craze becomes a crack when the **craze fibril strain** reaches the **fracture strain of the fibril**, governed by:

$$\varepsilon_{f,\text{crit}} = \frac{d_{\text{drawn}} - d_0}{d_0}$$

When *ε_f > ε_f,crit*, fibril rupture occurs at multiple points → coalescence → crack.

### 2.4 Stress-Crack Propagation (Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics, LEFM)

For a sharp edge crack of length *a* in a wide plate under remote tensile stress *σ*:

$$K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \cdot Y\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)$$

- *K_I* = Mode-I stress intensity factor [MPa·m^(1/2)]
- *Y(a/W)* = finite-width geometry correction factor (≈ *1.12* for *a/W < 0.1*)

Failure when *K_I* reaches the **fracture toughness** *K_IC*:

$$K_{IC} = Y\sigma_c\sqrt{\pi a_c}$$

For ESC, the **critical stress intensity** in environment is *K_ISCC* (Stress-Corrosion-Cracking equivalent). If *K_I < K_ISCC*, the crack is dormant.

### 2.5 Crack Growth Kinetics (Paris–Erdogan Law)

$$\frac{da}{dN} = C \,(\Delta K)^m$$

- *a* = crack length, *N* = number of cycles
- *C, m* = material constants; *m* typically **3–7** for polymers.
- In an ESC environment, *C* increases by 2–4 orders of magnitude.

### 2.6 Time-to-Failure under Constant Stress (Klinger–Hojo / Arrhenius form)

$$\log t_f = \log t_0 - n \log \sigma + \frac{E_a}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

- *t_f* = time-to-failure [s or h]
- *n* = stress exponent (typically **1–3**)
- *E_a* = apparent activation energy [J/mol]
- For PE in Igepal, **E_a ≈ 70–90 kJ/mol**.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table as a one-shot revision tool before the exam. All equations below are **exam-grade** and may appear directly or in disguised form in **KTU 2024 Part B questions**.

| # | Equation (LaTeX form) | Meaning / Use | Typical Range / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$ | Fox equation — plasticization by penetrant | K⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = A(T) + \dfrac{B(T)}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}$ | Sternstein criterion for craze initiation | MPa |
| 3 | $\sigma_t = \sigma_{\text{app}} \cdot K_t$ | Craze tip stress amplification | Dimensionless *K_t* |
| 4 | $K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \, Y(a/W)$ | Mode-I stress intensity factor | MPa·m^(1/2) |
| 5 | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma_c \sqrt{\pi a_c}$ | Fracture-toughness condition at failure | MPa·m^(1/2) |
| 6 | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$ | Paris law for fatigue crack growth | m/cycle |
| 7 | $\log t_f = \log t_0 - n \log \sigma + \dfrac{E_a}{2.303 R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)$ | Time-to-failure under constant stress | hours |
| 8 | $v_{\text{crack}} = A_{\text{env}} \, K_I^{\,p}$ | Environment-assisted crack growth | m/s |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_{f,\text{crit}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{drawn}} - d_0}{d_0}$ | Craze-fibril failure strain | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\gamma_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}^{\,2}}{2E}$ (plane stress) | Critical energy release rate | J/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for ESC steps: "W-P-C-G-F" → Wet → Plasticize → Craze → Grow → Fracture.**

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polyethylene gas pipes** — must resist ESC from compressed natural gas and condensate (methanol).
- **Polycarbonate medical devices** — fail under stress + lipid emulsions or alcohol disinfectants.
- **Nylon automotive fuel lines** — exposure to zinc chloride + stress → brittle fracture.
- **PMMA aircraft windows** — craze formation under solvent + stress.
- **Food packaging films** — must survive oil + bending stress without ESC.

> [!TIP]
> In the exam, always link the *equation* to the *engineering case* — KTU examiners reward applied knowledge.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Craze-Initiating Stress from a Surface Flaw

Consider a small surface flaw of length *a* on a polymer surface under remote tension *σ*. The flaw acts as a **Griffith-type stress concentrator**.

**Step 1 — Local stress at the flaw tip from elasticity theory:**

$$\sigma_{yy}(x, 0) = \sigma\!\left[1 + \frac{a}{\sqrt{2ax - x^2}}\right] \quad \text{for } 0 < x < a$$

**Step 2 — Maximum stress occurs at the tip (*x → a*):**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \lim_{x \to a} \sigma_{yy} = \sigma\!\left[1 + \frac{a}{0}\right] \to \infty$$

The infinite stress is unphysical; we regularize by assuming a finite craze-tip radius *ρ*:

**Step 3 — Inglis–Neuber regularized tip stress:**

$$\sigma_{yy,\max} = 2\sigma \sqrt{\frac{a}{\rho}}$$

This is the **craze-tip stress** that must exceed the local yield stress of the *plasticized* polymer for the craze to nucleate.

**Step 4 — Critical applied stress for craze initiation:**

$$\sigma_c = \frac{1}{2} \, \sigma_{y,\text{local}} \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{a}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting environment effect on local yield stress (plasticization).** The plasticized yield stress obeys:

$$\sigma_{y,\text{local}} = \sigma_{y,0} - k \, w_2$$

where *w₂* is the local penetrant concentration and *k* is the plasticization coefficient. Therefore:

$$\sigma_c^{\text{ESC}} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\sigma_{y,0} - k w_2\right)\sqrt{\frac{\rho}{a}}$$

**Step 6 — The ESC effect quantified as the *ratio*:**

$$\frac{\sigma_c^{\text{ESC}}}{\sigma_c^{\text{dry}}} = 1 - \frac{k w_2}{\sigma_{y,0}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The presence of the environment reduces the critical stress for craze initiation by a factor directly proportional to the sorbed fluid concentration *w₂*. This is the **mathematical origin of ESC**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Time-to-Failure under Constant Stress

The Klinger–Hojo model assumes that chain scission at the craze tip controls failure. The rate of scission is thermally activated.

**Step 1 — Eyring rate equation for chain scission:**

$$\dot{r} = \nu \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^*}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Stress reduces the activation free energy by an amount *σ·V\** (Eyring shear-volume):**

$$\Delta G^* = \Delta G_0^* - \sigma V^*$$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**

$$\dot{r} = \nu \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G_0^* - \sigma V^*}{RT}\right) = \nu \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G_0^*}{RT}\right) \exp\!\left(\frac{\sigma V^*}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from *r = 0* to *r = r_c* (critical scission density):**

$$t_f = \int_0^{r_c} \frac{dr}{\dot{r}} = \frac{r_c}{\nu} \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta G_0^*}{RT}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{\sigma V^*}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln t_f = \ln\!\left(\frac{r_c}{\nu}\right) + \frac{\Delta G_0^*}{RT} - \frac{\sigma V^*}{RT}$$

**Step 6 — Re-arrange into the **standard KTU form**:**

$$\ln t_f = \ln t_0 - \frac{V^*}{R T}\,\sigma + \frac{\Delta G_0^*}{RT}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to base-10 logarithm and define *n = V*/(2.303 RT)*:**

$$\log t_f = \log t_0 - n \log \sigma + \frac{E_a}{2.303 R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

This is the **master equation** for constant-stress ESC testing (ASTM D1693 / NCTL).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The slope of *log t_f* versus *log σ* gives *−n*, the stress exponent. KTU expects students to plot the data and read off the slope.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Failure Modes in Glassy Polymers

| Feature | Crazing | Shear Yielding | Environmental Stress Cracking | Brittle Fracture |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stress level | Below yield | At/above yield | Below yield | At/near σ_u |
| Volume change | Dilatational (+) | Contractive (−) | Dilatational + plasticized | None |
| Energy absorption | Moderate (fibril drawing) | High (cold drawing) | Low (crazes + plasticization) | Very low |
| Stress field | σ₁ − σ₃ dominates | Octahedral shear dominates | Same as crazing but *σ_c* reduced | Local K_I dominates |
| Environment effect | Slight | None | **Dramatic** (drives the failure) | None |
| Visual appearance | White, milky | Neck, draw band | White patches, then crack | Smooth, mirror-mist |
| Temperature effect | T < Tg favored | T > Tg favored | T < Tg and penetrant | T < Tg |
| Detection | Optical microscopy, light scattering | Birefringence | Time-to-failure curve | SEM fractography |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crazes and shear bands are mutually exclusive** in pure form — a stress state that maximizes σ₁ − σ₃ (equibiaxial tension) promotes crazing; a stress state that maximizes octahedral shear promotes shear yielding.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice)

**Problem:** A polyethylene pipe carries natural gas (with methanol condensate) at 8 °C. The hoop stress is 5 MPa. The critical craze-initiation stress in dry air is 12 MPa, but the Fox equation predicts a 20 K drop in Tg at the surface. Given *dσ_y/dT = 1.5 MPa/K*, find the new craze-initiation stress.

**Solution — step by step:**

**Step 1 — Drop in surface Tg:**

$$\Delta T_g = 20 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Drop in local yield stress:**

$$\Delta \sigma_y = \left(\frac{d\sigma_y}{dT}\right) \cdot \Delta T_g = 1.5 \text{ MPa/K} \times 20 \text{ K} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

Wait — but the local yield stress can only drop by the *absolute value* of the current yield. We assume *σ_y,0 = 35 MPa* in dry conditions.

**Step 3 — Plasticized local yield stress:**

$$\sigma_{y,\text{local}} = 35 - 30 = 5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — New craze-initiation stress (assume *K_t* unchanged):**

$$\sigma_c^{\text{ESC}} \approx 5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Compare to applied:**

$$\sigma_{\text{applied}} = 5 \text{ MPa} \ge \sigma_c^{\text{ESC}} = 5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Conclusion:** The pipe is at the **threshold of crazing** and will fail by ESC. KTU model answer will say: "*operate below 5 MPa, change pipe material, or use a methanol-resistant liner.*"

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of ESC Failure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram below is a **flowchart of the ESC failure process**, drawn using the Mermaid `flowchart` syntax. Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters per the KTU engine safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Applied Tensile Stress] --> B1[Mechanical Energy Input]
    A2[Stress Cracking Agent Contact] --> B2[Surface Wetting and Diffusion]
    B1 --> C1[Local Stress Field at Flaw Tip]
    B2 --> C2[Penetrant Sorption into Amorphous Phase]
    C2 --> D1[Plasticization of Polymer Chains]
    D1 --> D2[Local Tg Reduction via Fox Equation]
    D2 --> E1[Reduction in Local Yield Stress]
    C1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Sternstein Craze Initiation Criterion Satisfied]
    F1 --> G1[Microvoid Nucleation in Plane of Max Principal Stress]
    G1 --> G2[Fibril Drawing and Craze Growth]
    G2 --> H1[Craze Fibril Strain Exceeds Critical Value]
    H1 --> H2[Fibril Rupture and Coalescence]
    H2 --> I1[True Crack Initiation]
    I1 --> I2[Paris Law Crack Propagation in Environment]
    I2 --> J1[Final Brittle Fracture]
    J1 --> J2[Time to Failure Logged as ESCR Metric]

    style A1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style A2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300
    style D2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    style F1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
    style I1 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#660000
    style J1 fill:#e06666,stroke:#330000,color:#ffffff
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- **Blue boxes (A1, B1, C1):** Pure mechanical path (dry).
- **Orange boxes (A2, B2, C2):** Pure environmental path (chemical/physical).
- **Yellow box (D2):** The *synergistic intersection* — the moment when the environment *modifies* the polymer.
- **Green box (F1):** Craze initiation — the key bifurcation event.
- **Red boxes (I1, J1):** The final, irreversible failure events.

### 4.2 Schematic — Craze Microstructure

A simplified ASCII representation of a craze, showing the void-fibril network:

```
            MAXIMUM PRINCIPAL STRESS (σ₁)
                        │
                        │  ◄── Craze plane (⊥ to σ₁)
                        ▼
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  Bulks Bulk      ◦Void  ◦Void  ◦Void   │  ← Craze matrix
   │  ◦Void  ║Fibril║  ◦Void  ║Fibril║  ◦Void│     (50% void)
   │  ║Fibril║  ◦Void  ║Fibril║  ◦Void  ║Fibr║│     (50% fibrils)
   │  ◦Void  ║Fibril║  ◦Void  ║Fibril║  ◦Void│
   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
                        ▲
                        │   Craze tip advances
                        │   into the bulk
                  (Polymer Bulk)
```

- **Vertical bars `║Fibril║`** = drawn polymer fibrils carrying load.
- **Circles `◦Void`** = interconnected microvoids.
- The total craze volume is *50–60 % void*, hence the white, light-scattering appearance.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP PEPOT867]**
*Define Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC) and list any four common stress-cracking agents used in polymer testing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Environmental Stress Cracking is the premature brittle failure of a polymer subjected to a combination of a tensile stress (typically below yield) and a chemically weak (physically aggressive) environment that alone would not attack the polymer.
- **Four SCAs (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Igepal CO-630** (non-ionic surfactant) — used for polyethylene.
  2. **ASTM Oil No. 3** — used for NBR and polyamides.
  3. **Methanol** — used for polycarbonate and PMMA.
  4. **Triton X-100** — used for polyolefin ESCR testing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP PEPOT867]**
*Differentiate between crazing and cracking in glassy polymers. (Any four points)*

**Model Answer (3 marks — table format expected):**

| # | Crazing | Cracking |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load-bearing — fibrils transmit stress | No load transfer — true free surfaces |
| 2 | ~50 % void, ~50 % drawn polymer fibrils | 100 % free crack faces |
| 3 | Appears white due to light scattering | Invisible until late stage |
| 4 | Sub-yield stress can produce it | Often requires higher stress concentration |
| 5 | Pre-cursor; can arrest | Final failure event |

(0.5 mark per valid point × 4 = 2 marks; introductory sentence 1 mark.)

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Format:** *Either (a) OR (b)* from **Q1**, and *Either (a) OR (b)* from **Q2**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. The model answer below gives the *full-marks* solution.

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified KTU Pattern]**
*(a) Explain with neat sketches the mechanism of environmental stress cracking in polymers.  (7 marks)*
*(b) Discuss the role of crazing in the failure of glassy polymers, including the Sternstein criterion.  (7 marks)*

---

**(a) Mechanism of ESC — Step-by-step Model Solution (7 marks)**

1. **Stage 1 — Wetting (1 mark):** The stress-cracking agent contacts the polymer surface. The contact angle θ is governed by Young's equation:
   
   $$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$$
   
   For θ → 0, complete wetting and rapid sorption occur.
   
2. **Stage 2 — Sorption & Plasticization (1.5 marks):** The fluid diffuses (Fickian / Case-II) into the amorphous phase. The Fox equation reduces the local Tg:
   
   $$\frac{1}{T_g^{\text{local}}} = \frac{w_{\text{poly}}}{T_{g,\text{poly}}} + \frac{w_{\text{fluid}}}{T_{g,\text{fluid}}}$$
   
   A drop in Tg reduces the local yield stress via *dσ_y/dT*.

3. **Stage 3 — Craze Initiation (1.5 marks):** The Sternstein criterion is satisfied at lower applied stress:
   
   $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \ge A(T) + \frac{B(T)}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}$$
   
   Microvoids nucleate in a plane perpendicular to σ₁.

4. **Stage 4 — Craze Growth (1 mark):** Fibrils cold-draw; the craze tip advances and the craze widens. Craze tip stress = σ × K_t.

5. **Stage 5 — Fibril rupture and crack propagation (1.5 marks):** Fibrils reach critical strain, rupture, voids coalesce → true crack. Crack grows per Paris law:
   
   $$\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$$
   
   Final fracture at *K_I = K_IC*.  *(Sketch: any one of the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4 may be redrawn.)*

6. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** ESC = Stress + Environment, *not* a chemical reaction.

---

**(b) Role of Crazing in Failure of Glassy Polymers — Model Solution (7 marks)**

1. **What is a craze (1 mark):** Planar, crack-like defect perpendicular to σ_max; consists of *50 % void* and *50 % highly drawn polymer fibrils*.
2. **Craze initiation criteria (2 marks):**
   - **Sternstein & Ongchin equation:** $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = A(T) + B(T)/(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)$
   - **Stress-triaxiality requirement:** positive hydrostatic stress is *necessary*.
3. **Craze as a toughening mechanism (1 mark):** Crazes absorb energy via fibril drawing; this is the principal toughening mode in HIPS, ABS, and rubber-toughened PVC.
4. **Craze as failure precursor (1.5 marks):** When fibril strain *ε_f* exceeds the critical value, fibrils rupture → crack → brittle fracture. Thus crazing is *both* a toughening *and* a failure-initiation mechanism.
5. **Difference between crazing and shear yielding (1 mark):** Crazing is *dilatational* (volumetric), shear yielding is *deviatoric*. Crazes are favoured by equibiaxial tension, shear bands by uniaxial tension.
6. **Environmental effect (0.5 mark):** ESC lowers the critical stress for crazing, accelerating the failure pathway.

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Pattern]**
*(a) Describe the Bell model and the criteria for craze initiation in amorphous polymers.  (7 marks)*
*(b) Explain the various stages of environmental stress cracking with the relevant Paris-law and Eyring-based equations.  (7 marks)*

---

**(a) Bell Model and Craze Initiation Criteria — Model Solution (7 marks)**

1. **Bell's Model (3 marks):** Bell proposed that crazes form by a *meniscus-instability* mechanism in a plastic zone ahead of a stress concentrator. The plastic zone softens, voids nucleate by cavitation, and a "finger" of voids advances into the bulk. The criterion for advance is:
   
   $$\sigma_{zz}^{\text{local}} \ge \sigma_c^{\text{plast}} - \frac{2 \gamma_{sv}}{r_{\text{tip}}}$$
   
   where *r_tip* is the craze-tip radius and *γ_sv* is the surface energy of the polymer.

2. **Sternstein criterion (2 marks):**
   
   $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = A(T) + \frac{B(T)}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}$$
   
   - The deviatoric term *σ₁ − σ₃* drives the void opening.
   - The hydrostatic term in the denominator shows that *compressive* hydrostatic stress *suppresses* crazing.

3. **Argon & Salama criterion (1 mark):**
   
   $$\sigma_{yy}^{\text{crit}} = \sigma_y \left[1 - \frac{\pi}{4 \sqrt{3}} \frac{E}{\sigma_y} \frac{a}{R}\right]$$
   
   showing that larger plastic-zone size *R* lowers the critical stress.

4. **Application (1 mark):** These criteria predict *why* PE fails in Igepal CO-630 and *why* PMMA crazes in ethanol.

---

**(b) Stages of ESC with Paris Law and Eyring Equation — Model Solution (7 marks)**

1. **Stage 1: Sorption (1 mark):** Fickian diffusion; the equilibrium concentration depends on the Flory–Huggins χ parameter.

2. **Stage 2: Plasticization (1 mark):** Fox equation reduces Tg. Lower σ_y.

3. **Stage 3: Craze initiation (1 mark):** Sternstein criterion satisfied. Microvoids form.

4. **Stage 4: Craze growth (1 mark):** Craze-tip stress σ_t = σ × K_t. Fibrils cold-draw.

5. **Stage 5: Crack initiation and Paris-law propagation (2 marks):**
   
   $$\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$$
   
   - In environment, *C* increases by 2–4 orders of magnitude.
   - The crack-growth rate is also written as *v = A_env × K_I^p* (continuous-creep form).

6. **Stage 6: Final fracture (1 mark):** Eyring-based time-to-failure:
   
   $$\ln t_f = \ln t_0 - \frac{V^* \sigma}{RT} + \frac{\Delta G_0^*}{RT}$$
   
   - Failure when *K_I → K_IC*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing crazing with cracking (–1 mark per occurrence).** Crazes *do* carry load; cracks *do not*. If your answer does not mention this, you lose the conceptual point.
> 2. **Writing Paris law as *da/dt = …*** — Paris law is per *cycle* (fatigue). For *time-based* ESC, use *da/dt = A·K_I^p* or the Eyring form. Mixing them is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Omitting units** — *K_IC* in MPa·m^(1/2), *E_a* in kJ/mol. KTU deducts **0.5 mark** per missing unit.
> 4. **Skipping the Fox equation** in plasticization — this is a **2-mark direct loss** in Part B.
> 5. **Drawing crazes perpendicular to σ_min** instead of σ_max — examiners flag this **immediately**.
> 6. **Using chemical-corrosion language** for ESC — ESC is a *physical* effect, not chemical attack. Wording matters.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision list — re-read within 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **ESC = Stress + Environment**, both required, neither alone sufficient.
- [ ] **Craze** = void + fibril, *50:50*, perpendicular to *σ_max*.
- [ ] **Crack** = true free surfaces, no load transfer.
- [ ] **Stages: W-P-C-G-F** → Wetting, Plasticization, Craze Initiation, Craze Growth, Fracture.
- [ ] **Fox equation** lowers *T_g*; *σ_y* drops; *σ_c* (craze) drops.
- [ ] **Sternstein criterion** — *σ₁ − σ₃ ≥ A + B/(σ₁+σ₂+σ₃)* — central equation.
- [ ] **Bell's model** — meniscus instability in plastic zone.
- [ ] **Craze tip stress** = *σ × K_t* (amplified).
- [ ] **LEFM fracture criterion:** *K_I = K_IC*; *K_I = σ√(πa)Y*.
- [ ] **Paris law:** *da/dN = C(ΔK)^m* — for fatigue; in ESC environment, *C* rises.
- [ ] **Eyring time-to-failure:** *ln t_f = ln t₀ − (V*σ/RT) + (ΔG₀*/RT)*.
- [ ] **Standard SCAs:** Igepal, Triton, ASTM Oil No. 3, methanol, ethanol.
- [ ] **Test methods:** ASTM D1693 (bent strip), ASTM D5393 (NCTL), ASTM D2561 (bottle ESCR).
- [ ] **Crazing is favoured by** low T, equibiaxial tension, surface flaws, plasticizing fluids.
- [ ] **Cracking is favoured by** same conditions plus time + sustained stress.
- [ ] **Crazing vs. shear yielding:** volumetric vs. deviatoric; complementary, not equivalent.
- [ ] **Engineering materials affected:** PE pipes, PC medical parts, NBR seals, PMMA windows, PA fuel lines.
- [ ] **Crazes are reversible** if the stress is removed early; **cracks are not**.
- [ ] **Visual signature of crazes** = white, milky, light-scattering, planar features.
- [ ] **Visual signature of ESC fracture** = smooth, mirror-like, low-roughness brittle surface.
- [ ] **Key constants to memorize:** *R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)*, *γ_water = 72 mN/m*, *m_Paris = 3–7*.
- [ ] **Important "why" for KTU answers:** environment *lowers the energy barrier* for chain motion; stress *biases* that motion to form voids.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for ESC equation sequence:** "Fick → Fox → Sternstein → Paris → K_IC."
- [ ] **Always** mention the *role of Tg* — the *single most important* material property in ESC.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Electrical Treeing and Water Treeing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dielectric Treeing** is the progressive, time-dependent formation of microscopic, self-propagating, branched channel-like degradation structures within a solid polymeric insulator subjected to combined electrical, thermal, mechanical, and chemical stresses. It is one of the most clinically documented **long-term failure mechanisms in underground HV cable insulation** (especially **XLPE**, **EPR**, and **LDPE**), and forms a compulsory topic in any KTU failure analysis syllabus.

Two structurally similar but mechanistically distinct phenomena dominate this topic:

### 1.1 Electrical Treeing

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 syllabus terminology):**
Electrical treeing is the formation of gas-filled, hollow, branching discharge channels (resembling a tree, bush, or fan) inside a solid polymeric dielectric when it is subjected to a local electric field exceeding the partial discharge (PD) inception threshold, leading to **electron avalanche ionization**, **impact ionization**, **local Joule heating**, and **chemical bond scission** of the polymer backbone.

- **Initiation Field (typical):** > **10 kV/mm** (local field at defect)
- **Inception Time:** hours to months depending on field, frequency, and morphology
- **Required Condition:** presence of an initiating defect (void, contaminant, protrusion, metallic inclusion) and AC stress above the Paschen minimum

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Lightning burning a tree-shaped scar inside a polymer"**
> Imagine a high-voltage wire wrapped in plastic insulation. Wherever the insulation is imperfect (a tiny air bubble, dust speck, or a sharp metallic edge), the electric field lines get compressed and squeezed, just like water pressure increases at a kink in a hose. When the local pressure (electric field) becomes intense enough, it rips electrons off polymer molecules. These energetic electrons crash into neighbouring molecules, releasing more electrons in a chain reaction — exactly like a microscopic lightning bolt. As this "lightning" wanders through the plastic, it melts, vaporises, and chemically chars the polymer into a permanent hollow, branching, tree-shaped tunnel. Once formed, the tunnel **never heals** — it grows toward the opposite electrode until the wall is perforated and the cable short-circuits.

### 1.2 Water Treeing

**Formal Definition:**
Water treeing is the growth of diffuse, water-saturated, non-conducting (or weakly conducting), bow-tie or fan-shaped degradation patterns in a polymer dielectric under the **combined action of an alternating electric field and moisture**, operating at field strengths **well below the partial discharge inception level**.

- **Operating Field:** typically **1–4 kV/mm** (one-tenth of electrical tree inception)
- **Required Condition:** simultaneous presence of **liquid water** and **AC electric field**
- **Inception Time:** months to years
- **Visual Appearance:** diffuse, milky-white, branched structure when stained; invisible in dry state

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Plant roots creeping through wet, stressed concrete"**
> Picture a polyethylene cable buried in moist soil. Groundwater slowly diffuses into the polymer. Wherever a tiny impurity sits, the alternating electric field (50 Hz hum) gently but relentlessly squeezes and stretches the polymer chains, like bending a paperclip back and forth until it snaps. Over months, microscopic fatigue cracks open, capillary action sucks more water in, and chemical reactions between water, ions, and the polymer erode the chains. The result is a slow-growing, waterlogged "root" structure — visually like the roots of a tree, but chemically more like a slow bruise than a burnt scar. Although each "root" is **not electrically conductive** when dry, it severely weakens the wall and acts as a precursor to a violent electrical tree.

---

### 1.3 Side-by-Side Comparative Snapshot

| Attribute | Electrical Treeing | Water Treeing |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| Driving Stress | High local field (E > PD inception) | Moderate field + moisture |
| Channel Nature | Hollow, gas-filled, dry | Water-saturated, diffuse |
| Conductivity of Tree | Conductive (carbonised) | Non-conductive when dry |
| Morphology | Branch / Bush / Bush-Branch | Bow-tie / Vented / Plume |
| Growth Rate | Fast (µm/min – mm/h) | Slow (µm/day – mm/year) |
| Reversibility | Irreversible | Partially reversible on drying |
| Dominant Mechanism | Partial discharge, impact ionisation | Electro-mechanical fatigue, chemical oxidation |
| Typical Voltage Class | HV / EHV cables | MV / HV cables in wet environments |
| Detection | Phase-resolved PD, DGA, visual | Dissipation factor (tan δ), visual after staining |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse the two phenomena. Remember the **golden triad** for water treeing — **Water + AC Field + Time**. Without **all three** water treeing will **not** initiate. Electrical treeing requires **only high AC field + defect**; water is not necessary.

---

### 1.4 Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters for KTU / Industry:**
> 1. **70–80 %** of in-service MV/HV cable failures worldwide are attributed to treeing-related degradation.
> 2. Understanding treeing is essential for **lifetime prediction** of underground power distribution assets.
> 3. It justifies the development of **TR-XLPE**, **EPR**, **water-tree-retardant (WTR) XLPE** compounds, and **super-smooth** semiconductor screens in modern cables.
> 4. Forms the theoretical basis for condition-monitoring techniques like **tan δ measurement**, **partial discharge mapping**, and **dielectric spectroscopy**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrical Treeing — Mechanistic Breakdown

Electrical treeing evolves through **three classical stages**, each governed by a distinct physical mechanism:

### Stage 1 — Initiation (Inception Phase)
- **Trigger site:** micro-void (typically 1–50 µm), contaminant (metal oxide, sulphur), semi-conducting layer protrusion, or crystalline-amorphous interface.
- **Field enhancement** at a sharp protrusion of tip radius **ρ** follows the well-known **Mason formula**:

$$E_{tip} \;=\; E_{0}\,\left(1 + \dfrac{2\,\sqrt{L/\rho}}{1 + \sqrt{L/\rho}}\right)$$

where $E_{0}$ is the applied (background) field, $L$ is the length of the protrusion. As $\rho \to 0$, $E_{tip} \to \infty$.

- **Initiation criterion:** local $E_{tip}$ exceeds the **PD inception field** of the entrapped gas (Paschen's law gives ~3 kV/mm for air at atmospheric pressure for 10 µm gaps).
- **Time scale:** $t_i \propto E^{-n}$ where $n = 4$–$8$ (highly field-sensitive).

### Stage 2 — Propagation (Growth Phase)
Once a sub-micron initial channel is formed, growth proceeds via:
1. **Partial discharge (PD) erosion** — micro-discharges inside the channel ionise gas and physically erode walls.
2. **Electron avalanche / impact ionisation** — hot electrons (~5–10 eV) break C–H and C–C bonds.
3. **Space-charge mediated field enhancement** — charges trapped on channel walls distort the field, redirecting tips into branched patterns.
4. **Photo-degradation** — UV photons emitted from PD excite C=O, leading to oxidative chain scission.

The empirical growth law is the **power-law (NH model — Nottingham / Hogg / Auckland)**:

$$L(t) \;=\; A \cdot t^{\,n}$$

where $L$ = tree length, $A$ = constant, $t$ = time under stress, $n$ = growth exponent.

| Value of n | Tree Type | Interpretation |
|------------|-----------|----------------|
| 0.3 – 0.5 | Branch tree | Fast, runaway growth |
| 0.5 – 0.7 | Bush tree | Field-limited |
| > 1.0 | Bush-branch | Super-accelerated, near breakdown |

### Stage 3 — Runaway / Breakdown
- When the channel tip approaches within ~50–100 µm of the opposite electrode, the field at the tip exceeds **10 MV/m**, causing streamer propagation and **final dielectric breakdown**.
- **Critical length $L_c$** for breakdown:

$$L_c \;=\; \dfrac{U}{E_{bd}} - d_{0}$$

where $U$ = applied voltage, $E_{bd}$ = bulk breakdown strength, $d_{0}$ = insulation thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Stage 1 may last years, Stage 2 takes months, Stage 3 takes microseconds. The **propagation stage** is the principal target for laboratory accelerated life testing.

---

## 2.2 Water Treeing — Mechanistic Breakdown

Water treeing is a **multi-physics, multi-stage** degradation phenomenon involving:

### Step 1 — Water Ingress and Saturation
- Diffusion of water molecules follows **Fick's second law**:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} \;=\; D\,\nabla^{2}C$$

Equilibrium water concentration $C_\infty$ in XLPE is ~**100–300 ppm** at 25 °C, scaling with relative humidity.

### Step 2 — Field-Driven Mechanical Fatigue
- An AC field exerts a **Maxwell electrostriction pressure** on the dielectric:

$$P_{e} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,(1 + \cos 2\omega t)\,E^{2}$$

- The pulsating component $(1 + \cos 2\omega t)$ mechanically fatigues polymer chains at vulnerable sites (impurities, crystal-amorphous boundaries).
- This produces **sub-micron micro-voids**, into which water is sucked by **dielectrophoresis**.

### Step 3 — Chemical Oxidation
- Water + ionic impurities + field → production of **hydroxyl radicals (·OH)**.
- Hydroxyl radicals attack polymer chains:

$$\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} + \cdot\text{OH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CH}_2\text{–}\dot{\text{CH}}\text{–} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

- Carboxylic acids, alcohols, ketones form → hydrophilic clusters grow.

### Step 4 — Cluster Coalescence and Tree Growth
- Water-filled microcavities coalesce into the characteristic **bow-tie** (initiated at bulk contaminant) or **vented** (initiated at insulation surface) morphology.
- Growth law for water trees is typically **logarithmic** or **square-root**:

$$L_{w}(t) \;=\; k_{w}\,\sqrt{t}$$

where $k_w$ depends on field, frequency, temperature, water content.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Frequency acceleration:** Water tree growth rate scales roughly **linearly with frequency** because each AC half-cycle imposes one mechanical pulse. This is why 50 Hz cables fail faster in lab tests at 500 Hz — a fact used in **IEEE 1407 / IEC 60502 accelerated ageing protocols**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield equation you may be required to derive or apply in KTU ESE / Module tests. Unit annotations use `\vert` instead of `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation (Symbolic) | Physical Meaning | Variables / Units | Domain of Validity |
|---|---------------------|------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $E_{tip} = E_0\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2\sqrt{L/\rho}}{1+\sqrt{L/\rho}}\right)$ | Field enhancement at a needle tip | $E_0$ [V/m], $L$ [m], $\rho$ [m] | Needle-plane geometry, $\rho \ll L$ |
| 2 | $L(t) = A\,t^{\,n}$ | NH power-law growth of electrical tree | $L$ [m], $t$ [s], $n$ = growth exponent (0.3–1.0) | Empirical; $L > L_{init}$ |
| 3 | $V_{PD} = 2.5\,(p\,d) + \dfrac{2400}{p\,d}$ | Paschen's law — PD inception voltage | $p$ [torr], $d$ [cm] | Gas-filled voids |
| 4 | $L_c = U/E_{bd} - d_0$ | Critical tree length for breakdown | $U$ [V], $E_{bd}$ [V/m] | Pre-final-stage |
| 5 | $\partial C/\partial t = D\nabla^{2}C$ | Fick's 2nd law — water diffusion | $D$ [m²/s], $C$ [mol/m³] | Isothermal, no chemical reaction |
| 6 | $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r(1+\cos 2\omega t)E^{2}$ | Electrostriction pressure | $P_e$ [Pa], $E$ [V/m], $\omega$ [rad/s] | Loss-less linear dielectric |
| 7 | $L_w(t) = k_w\sqrt{t}$ | Water-tree growth law | $L_w$ [m], $k_w$ [m/√s] | Empirical; $t \gg t_{incubation}$ |
| 8 | $\tan\delta \approx \omega\,\varepsilon_0\,\dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_r^{2}\varepsilon_0^{2}\omega^{2}}$ | Loss tangent — water tree diagnostic | dimensionless | MV cable, 0.1–10 Hz |
| 9 | $E_c = \dfrac{U_0}{d\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Critical field for tree inception | $U_0$ [V], $d$ [m] | Plane-plane electrode |
| 10 | $t_{50\,\%} \propto E^{-n}$ | Inverse-power life model | $n = 4$–$8$ | Weibull / IPM statistics |

---

## 2.4 Comparative Mechanistic Schematic (Conceptual)

```
   ELECTRICAL TREE                  WATER TREE
   ===============                  ==========
  Stress: E only                    Stress: E + H₂O
  Threshold: HIGH                   Threshold: LOW
  Channel: GAS                      Channel: WATER
  Carbonised walls                  Hydrated, oxidised walls
  Conductive paths                  Insulating when dry
  Branching: stochastic             Branching: lenticular bow-tie
  Detection: easy (PD)              Detection: hard (tan δ)
```

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Knowledge Pays Off

1. **Cable Lifetime Prediction** — Enables Weibull/IPM extrapolation from 1-year lab data to 30-year service life.
2. **Material Selection** — Justifies shift from standard XLPE to **TR-XLPE** (tree-retardant) and **WTR-XLPE** (water-tree-retardant).
3. **Diagnostic Technique Design** — Forms physical basis of **on-line tan δ monitoring**, **PD mapping**, and **dielectric spectroscopy** used by utilities like KSEB.
4. **Insulation Design Rules** — Drives the adoption of **super-smooth semi-con layers**, **dry-cured insulation**, and **dry-type nitrogen-pressurised cable systems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Field Enhancement Factor at a Hyperboloidal Needle Tip

The classical Mason (1959) field enhancement at the tip of a hyperboloidal needle of radius $\rho$ and length $L$ embedded in a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, with a background field $E_0$ at infinity, is derived as follows.

### Setup
We seek the maximum electric field at the needle tip when the geometry is a hyperboloid of revolution:

$$\dfrac{x^{2}}{a^{2}} - \dfrac{r^{2}}{b^{2}} \;=\; 1$$

with $a$ = semi-focal length, $b$ = minor radius, and tip radius $\rho = b^{2}/a$.

### Step 1 — Laplace Equation in Prolate Spheroidal Coordinates
Using separation of variables, the potential around the needle is:

$$\Phi(\xi,\eta) \;=\; -E_0\,a\,\xi\,\eta$$

where $\xi$ and $\eta$ are prolate spheroidal coordinates. The local electric field magnitude is:

$$E(\xi,\eta) \;=\; E_0\,\sqrt{\dfrac{1 - \eta^{2}}{\xi^{2} - 1} + \eta^{2}}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate at the Tip ($\eta = 1$)
At the needle surface $\eta = 1$, $E$ reduces to:

$$E_{tip} \;=\; \dfrac{E_0}{\sqrt{\xi^{2} - 1}}$$

### Step 3 — Relate $\xi$ to Physical Tip Radius
At the tip, $\xi = L/a$ where $L$ is the needle length. The tip radius is:

$$\rho \;=\; \dfrac{b^{2}}{a} \;=\; \dfrac{a^{2}(\xi^{2} - 1)}{a} \;=\; a(\xi^{2} - 1)$$

Therefore:

$$\xi^{2} - 1 \;=\; \dfrac{\rho}{a} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \xi \;=\; \sqrt{1 + \rho/a}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{tip} &= \dfrac{E_0}{\sqrt{\rho/a}} \;=\; E_0\,\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}} \\
&= E_0\,\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{\rho}}\quad\text{(since } a \to L \text{ for long needle)}
\end{aligned}$$

A more refined form incorporating finite needle length yields the Mason result quoted earlier:

$$E_{tip} \;=\; E_0\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2\sqrt{L/\rho}}{1 + \sqrt{L/\rho}}\right)$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A defect of tip radius $\rho = 1\,\mu m$ on a needle of $L = 1\,mm$ embedded in a cable of background field $E_0 = 5\,kV/mm$ generates:

$$E_{tip} \;=\; 5 \times 10^{6}\,\left(1 + \dfrac{2\sqrt{1000}}{1 + \sqrt{1000}}\right) \;\approx\; 5 \times 10^{6} \times 3.06 \;\approx\; 15.3\,MV/m$$

This is well above the PD inception threshold of ~3 kV/mm, explaining why even a microscopic defect initiates electrical treeing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Power-Law Growth of Electrical Trees (NH Model)

The empirical power-law $L = A\,t^n$ can be derived dimensionally by assuming that each PD event releases an energy $W$ that erodes a volume $V \propto W^\alpha$ of polymer at the channel tip.

### Step 1 — Energy per PD event
If the local field at the tip is $E_{tip}$ and the discharge column length is $\ell$:

$$W \;=\; q\,E_{tip}\,\ell$$

### Step 2 — Number of PD events in time dt
At supply frequency $f$, the number of events is $N = 2f\,dt$ (one positive and one negative PD per cycle).

### Step 3 — Incremental length gain
The incremental tree length is proportional to PD count and energy:

$$dL \;=\; k\,\beta\,(2f\,dt)^{n}$$

### Step 4 — Integrate

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{L} dL &= k\beta (2f)^{n} \int_0^{t} t^{\,n}\,dt \\
L &= \dfrac{k\beta (2f)^{n}}{n+1}\,t^{\,n+1}
\end{aligned}$$

Reabsorbing constants into a new constant $A$ yields:

$$L(t) \;=\; A\,t^{\,n+1} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; L(t) = A\,t^{\,m}$$

where $m = n+1$ is the empirical growth exponent.

**Note:** For a typical XLPE, $m \in [0.4, 1.2]$, consistent with experimental observations on laboratory-aged cable samples.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Water-Tree Growth Kinetics

Consider a hemispherical water-filled microcavity of radius $r(t)$ growing in a polymer under AC field $E$.

### Step 1 — Maxwell Stress on the cavity wall
The electrostriction pressure oscillates at $2\omega$:

$$P_e(t) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\,(1 + \cos 2\omega t)\,E^{2}$$

The amplitude is:

$$\Delta P_e \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r\,E^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Mechanical fatigue (Paris-type law)
Per the Paris law for fatigue crack growth in polymers:

$$\dfrac{da}{dN} \;=\; C\,(\Delta K)^{m}$$

where $\Delta K = Y\,\Delta P_e\,\sqrt{\pi a}$ is the stress intensity range and $N = 2f\,t$ is the number of cycles.

### Step 3 — Substitute and integrate
For a penny-shaped crack ($Y \approx 0.65$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dr}{dt} &= 2f\,C\,(0.65\,\Delta P_e\,\sqrt{\pi r})^{m} \\
&= 2f\,C\,\Delta P_e^{\,m}\,(0.65)^{m}\pi^{m/2}\,r^{m/2}
\end{aligned}$$

For $m = 2$ (typical polymer fatigue exponent):

$$\dfrac{dr}{dt} \;=\; K\,r \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; r(t) \;=\; r_0\,e^{K t}$$

For $m = 1$:

$$\dfrac{dr}{dt} \;=\; K\sqrt{r} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; r(t) \;=\; \left(\sqrt{r_0} + \tfrac{K}{2}\,t\right)^{2}$$

In the long-time limit $t \gg r_0$:

$$r(t) \;\approx\; \left(\tfrac{K}{2}\,t\right)^{2} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; r(t) \;\propto\; t^{2}$$

Comparing with empirical $L_w \propto t^{1/2}$ suggests $m = 1$ is the operative exponent in real water trees (consistent with stress-corrosion-cracking-like behaviour).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Initiation Criteria

| Parameter | Electrical Tree | Water Tree |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------|
| Minimum Field | 10–15 kV/mm (local) | 1–4 kV/mm |
| Initiation Time | $t_i \propto E^{-n}$ (n=4–8) | $t_i \propto E^{-2}$ |
| Frequency Dependence | Strong (n ∝ f) | Linear with f |
| Water Dependence | None | Essential (≥ 1000 ppm) |
| Temperature Coefficient | Positive (Arrhenius) | Positive up to 60 °C, then plateau |
| Critical Morphology | Sharp defect / void | Hydrophilic impurity / interface |
| Dominant Radical | e⁻, ·O, ·OH | ·OH, ·OOH |
| Reversibility | Irreversible | Partially reversible on drying |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-typical)

**Problem:** A 11 kV XLPE cable has insulation thickness $d = 3.5$ mm. The conductor screen contains a semi-con protrusion of length $L = 0.5$ mm and tip radius $\rho = 2\,\mu m$. The operating field is $E_0 = U/(d\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$ with $\varepsilon_r = 2.3$.

**(a)** Compute the background field.

$$E_0 \;=\; \dfrac{11 \times 10^{3}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2.3}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{11 \times 10^{3}}{5.31 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 2.07\,MV/m$$

**(b)** Compute the field enhancement factor.

$$\dfrac{L}{\rho} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 250$$

$$\eta \;=\; \sqrt{250} \;=\; 15.81$$

$$F \;=\; 1 + \dfrac{2\eta}{1+\eta} \;=\; 1 + \dfrac{2 \times 15.81}{16.81} \;=\; 1 + 1.881 \;=\; 2.881$$

**(c)** Compute $E_{tip}$.

$$E_{tip} \;=\; 2.07 \times 10^{6} \times 2.881 \;\approx\; 5.96\,MV/m$$

Since $E_{tip} > 3\,MV/m$ (PD inception in air at 10 µm), an electrical tree will initiate at this defect.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Electrical Tree Initiation to Breakdown

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Voltage AC Applied] --> B[Defect Present Void or Protrusion]
    B --> C[Local Field Enhancement at Tip]
    C --> D{Field above PD Inception?}
    D -- No --> E[No Tree Slow Ageing]
    D -- Yes --> F[Electron Avalanche in Microvoid]
    F --> G[Partial Discharge Activated]
    G --> H[Impact Ionisation of Polymer Chains]
    H --> I[Sub micron Channel Initiated]
    I --> J[Stage 2 Propagation PD Erosion]
    J --> K[Stage 2 Branching Space Charge Redirection]
    K --> L{Tree Length exceeds Critical Lc}
    L -- No --> M[Power Law Growth L equals A t to power n]
    L -- Yes --> N[Streamer Propagation]
    N --> O[Dielectric Breakdown Failure]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Water Tree Initiation to Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Moist Soil or Humid Environment] --> B[Water Diffusion into XLPE]
    B --> C[Equilibrium Water Concentration Reached]
    C --> D[AC Field Applied]
    D --> E[Maxwell Electrostriction Pressure on Polymer]
    E --> F[Sub micron Voids Form at Hydrophilic Sites]
    F --> G[Water Condensation in Voids]
    G --> H[Hydroxyl Radical Attack on Chains]
    H --> I[Bow Tie or Vented Water Tree Grows]
    I --> J{Long Term Stress Continues}
    J --> K[Chemical Conversion to Electrical Tree]
    J -- No --> L[Dielectric Loss Rises tan delta]
    K --> M[Final Dielectric Breakdown]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Diagnostic System for Treeing in Cables

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Field[Cable System Under Test]
        A1[HV Conductor] --> A2[Semi Con Screen]
        A2 --> A3[XLPE Insulation]
        A3 --> A4[Outer Semi Con Screen]
        A4 --> A5[Metallic Sheath]
    end

    subgraph Sensors[Condition Monitoring Sensors]
        S1[Tan Delta Bridge] --> R1[Loss Tangent Data]
        S2[HFCT Partial Discharge Sensor] --> R2[PD Pulse Data]
        S3[Dielectric Spectroscopy Module] --> R3[Frequency Response]
        S4[Optical Fibre DTS] --> R4[Hot Spot Profile]
    end

    subgraph Analysis[Diagnostic Engine]
        R1 --> E1[Treeing Index Calculator]
        R2 --> E1
        R3 --> E1
        R4 --> E1
        E1 --> E2{Threshold Exceeded}
        E2 -- Yes --> E3[Alarm Replace Cable Section]
        E2 -- No --> E4[Continue Operation Log Trend]
    end

    Field -. feeds .-> Sensors
```

## 4.4 Morphological Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Dielectric Tree] --> ET[Electrical Tree]
    R --> WT[Water Tree]

    ET --> ETB[Branch Tree]
    ET --> ETU[Bush Tree]
    ET --> ETBU[Bush Branch Tree]

    WT --> WTB[Bow Tie Tree]
    WT --> WTV[Vented Tree]
    WT --> WTP[Plume or Mist Tree]

    ETB -. grows from .- ED1[Needle Tip Defect]
    ETU -. grows from .- ED2[Void Cluster]
    ETBU -. grows from .- ED3[Semi Con Protrusion]

    WTB -. grows from .- WD1[Bulk Contaminant]
    WTV -. grows from .- WD2[Insulation Surface Interface]
    WTP -. grows from .- WD3[Distributed Moisture]
```

For water treeing, draw the **bow-tie and vented morphologies** clearly — examiners award full marks only when both shapes are illustrated.
